<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]`
**Define Flexible Manufacturing System. List any four types of flexibility associated with FMS.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** A Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS) is an integrated group of computer-controlled machine tools, linked by an automated material handling system and supervised by a central computer, capable of producing a family of parts in varying quantities with minimal manual intervention.

**Types of Flexibility (1.5 marks — any four):**
1. **Machine Flexibility** — ability of a single machine to produce different part types.
2. **Product Flexibility** — ability to introduce new products quickly.
3. **Mix Flexibility** — ability to produce different parts simultaneously.
4. **Volume Flexibility** — ability to operate profitably at different production volumes.
5. **Routing Flexibility** — ability to use multiple alternative machine sequences.
6. **Operation Flexibility** — ability to perform diverse operations on one machine.
7. **Expansion Flexibility** — ability to add new machines/workstations easily.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]`
**What is the significance of MTBF and MTTR in FMS performance evaluation?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) (1.5 marks):** It is the average operational time between two successive failures of a machine or subsystem. Higher MTBF indicates greater **reliability** and is a critical metric for FMS workstations, since downtime directly affects the entire integrated system. Typical target values exceed **200 hours**.

**MTTR (Mean Time To Repair) (1.5 marks):** It is the average time required to repair and restore a failed machine to operational condition. Lower MTTR indicates better **maintainability**. Typical target values are below **30 minutes**. The combination of high MTBF and low MTTR yields high system availability, calculated as:

$$
A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

---

## 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]`

**(a) Explain in detail the major components of a Flexible Manufacturing System with a neat block diagram.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

An FMS consists of **five major functional components** working in coordination:

**Component 1 — CNC Machine Tools / Workstations (2 marks):**
These are the heart of any FMS. They include CNC machining centers, CNC turning centers, and special-purpose NC machines. Each workstation has:
- Automatic Tool Changer (ATC)
- Pallet Changer (to load/unload parts while machining continues)
- Multiple-axis capability (3-axis, 4-axis, or 5-axis)
- DNC (Direct Numerical Control) interface for receiving programs from the host

**Component 2 — Automated Material Handling System (AMHS) (2 marks):**
This subsystem physically transports raw materials, in-process parts, and finished parts between machines, buffers, and inspection stations. Common types include:
- **Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs):** Battery-powered vehicles following wire guides, magnetic strips, or laser paths.
- **Conveyor Systems:** Belt or roller conveyors for fixed-path transport.
- **Industrial Robots:** Used for loading/unloading machines and inter-station transfer.
- **Automated Storage and Retrieval Systems (AS/RS):** High-density buffer storage.

**Component 3 — Central Computer Control System (1.5 marks):**
A hierarchical computer network comprising:
- **Host Computer** (Plant-level): ERP integration, production planning.
- **Cell Controller / FMS Controller**: Real-time scheduling, routing decisions, tool management.
- **Shop Floor Controllers**: Machine-level control, DNC program distribution.
- Functions include order release, dynamic scheduling, tool life tracking, and performance reporting.

**Component 4 — Tool Management System (1 mark):**
Centralized tool magazines, tool presetters, automated tool delivery vehicles, and tool-life databases. This ensures the right tool reaches the right machine at the right time, minimizing machine waiting.

**Component 5 — Supporting Software and Inspection Stations (0.5 marks):**
CAD/CAM systems for NC programming, GT classification, simulation software, and Coordinate Measuring Machines (CMM) for in-process inspection.

**Block Diagram Reference:** As shown in Section 4.1 of these notes.

---

**(b) Discuss the advantages and limitations of FMS. Compare FMS with a transfer line and a job shop.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Advantages of FMS (2 marks):**
1. High machine utilization (75–90%) due to reduced setup and changeover time.
2. Reduced direct labor costs (40–80% reduction) through automation.
3. Lower Work-In-Process (WIP) inventory due to smooth flow.
4. Shorter production lead times (60–80% reduction).
5. Improved and consistent product quality.
6. High responsiveness to market changes through mix and volume flexibility.
7. Reduced floor space compared to functional job shops.

**Limitations of FMS (2 marks):**
1. Very high initial capital investment ($5M – $20M typical).
2. Complex design, integration, and commissioning.
3. Requires highly skilled engineers, programmers, and maintenance staff.
4. Vulnerability to single-point failures (e.g., central controller breakdown halts system).
5. Underutilization risk if part family is poorly defined or volumes are low.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Transfer Line | Job Shop | FMS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Part Variety | Very Low | Very High | Medium-High |
| Volume | Very High | Low | Medium-High |
| Flexibility | None | High | High |
| Investment | Very High | Low | High |
| WIP | Low | High | Medium |
| Lead Time | Short | Long | Short |
| Best For | Mass production | Prototype/single piece | Batch production of part families |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A(b):**
> Students often write **only the advantages** and skip limitations entirely. This loses **2 marks**. Always present a **balanced two-sided answer**. Also, students frequently forget to include a comparison table; using one earns easy marks and demonstrates structured thinking. Do not write vague phrases like "FMS is good" — quantify using OEE, MTBF, or specific percentage reductions.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Supplementary Style]`

**(a) Define flexibility in the context of FMS. Explain any five types of flexibility with suitable examples.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of Flexibility (1.5 marks):**
Flexibility in FMS refers to the **inherent capability of the system to adapt to changes** in product design, production volume, mix, or routing, with minimum disruption, delay, or reconfiguration cost. It is a multi-dimensional property that distinguishes FMS from both rigid transfer lines and traditional job shops.

**Five Types of Flexibility (1.1 marks each = 5.5 marks):**

**1. Machine Flexibility (1.1 marks):**
The ability of a single CNC machine to produce different part types without major changeover. Example: A CNC machining center producing both a pump housing and a gearbox cover in the same shift by simply changing the program and tools.

**2. Product Flexibility (1.1 marks):**
The ability to introduce new products or modify existing designs quickly. Example: Adding a new variant of an automotive connecting rod to an existing FMS by preparing new NC programs and tool lists within hours.

**3. Mix Flexibility (1.1 marks):**
The ability to produce a mix of different parts in varying proportions simultaneously. Example: An FMS producing 40% flanges, 35% brackets, and 25% covers in the same shift, with the controller dynamically allocating machines.

**4. Volume Flexibility (1.1 marks):**
The ability to operate profitably across a wide range of production volumes. Example: The same FMS producing 500 units/month in one quarter and 5000 units/month in the next without major reconfiguration.

**5. Routing Flexibility (1.1 marks):**
The ability of parts to follow alternative paths through the FMS based on machine availability. Example: A part requiring milling can be routed to any available CNC mill among three in the cell, depending on real-time load.

---

**(b) What is OEE? Explain its components. A CNC machine in an FMS has Planned Production Time = 480 min/day, Downtime = 60 min, Total Parts Produced = 350, Ideal Cycle Time = 1.0 min, and Good Parts = 329. Calculate the OEE and comment on the result.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of OEE (1.5 marks):**
Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) is a comprehensive metric that quantifies how effectively a manufacturing operation is utilized, compared to its full potential. It is the product of three independent factors:

$$
\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q
$$

**Explanation of Components (1.5 marks):**
- **Availability (A):** Ratio of actual running time to planned production time. Reflects downtime losses.
- **Performance (P):** Ratio of theoretical cycle time to actual cycle time. Reflects speed losses (idling, minor stops).
- **Quality (Q):** Ratio of good units to total units produced. Reflects defect losses.

**Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**

Given:
- Planned Production Time = 480 min
- Downtime = 60 min
- Total Parts = 350
- Ideal Cycle Time = 1.0 min
- Good Parts = 329

**Step 1: Calculate Running Time.**

$$
\text{Running Time} = 480 - 60 = 420 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Availability.**

$$
A = \frac{420}{480} = 0.875
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Performance.**

$$
P = \frac{1.0 \times 350}{420} = \frac{350}{420} = 0.8333
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Quality.**

$$
Q = \frac{329}{350} = 0.94
$$

**Step 5: Calculate OEE.**

$$
\text{OEE} = 0.875 \times 0.8333 \times 0.94
$$

$$
\text{OEE} = 0.6853 \approx 68.53\%
$$

**Comment / Engineering Interpretation (1 mark):**
The calculated OEE of **68.53%** is **below the world-class benchmark of 85%**. The weakest factor is **Performance (83.33%)**, suggesting the machine experiences significant speed losses (minor stops, idling, or reduced feed rates). Recommendations include predictive maintenance, operator training, and process parameter optimization. With targeted interventions, OEE can realistically be improved to **80%+** within 3–6 months.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B(b):**
> Students commonly make **two mistakes**: (1) Forgetting to convert percentages to decimals before multiplying OEE — use **0.875**, not **87.5**. (2) Skipping the engineering comment — KTU expects you to interpret the numerical answer, not just compute it. Always end numerical problems with a 2–3 sentence interpretation comparing to the 85% world-class benchmark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Pre-Exam Checklist for "General FMS Considerations"**

- [ ] **Definition Ready**: FMS = Integrated CNC machines + AMHS + Central Control + Tool Management + Software.
- [ ] **Seven Flexibilities Memorized**: Machine, Product, Mix, Volume, Routing, Operation, Expansion (Mnemonic: **MM-VROE**).
- [ ] **Five Components Identified**: Workstations, Material Handling, Control System, Tool Management, Inspection/Software.
- [ ] **OEE Formula Memorized**: $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ — world-class benchmark is **> 85%**.
- [ ] **Availability Formula**: $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$.
- [ ] **MTBF Target**: > 200 hours. **MTTR Target**: < 30 minutes.
- [ ] **Comparison Table Mastered**: FMS sits between Transfer Line and Job Shop — medium volume, medium-high variety.
- [ ] **FMS Sweet Spot**: 10–100 part variants, 500–50,000 units/year per part.
- [ ] **Investment Range**: $5M – $20M (use this range in justification answers).
- [ ] **WIP Reduction**: 50%–80% vs. conventional job shop.
- [ ] **Setup Time Reduction**: 75%–90% (key flexibility metric).
- [ ] **Hierarchical Control Levels**: Host → Cell Controller → Shop Floor Controller → Machine.
- [ ] **Decision Tree for FMS**: Part family size, volume range, setup intensity, capital availability.
- [ ] **Industry Applications**: Automotive (engine blocks), Aerospace (turbine blades), Medical (implants), Defense (precision parts).
- [ ] **Always quote**: Numerical metrics (percentages) and standard symbols (OEE, MTBF, MTTR, $\eta_m$) in every answer.
- [ ] **Block Diagrams**: Be prepared to draw the 5-pillar architecture in under 3 minutes for full marks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Closing Tip:** In KTU 2024 Scheme examinations, Module 1 carries foundational importance. Questions from this module often appear as 3-mark definitions, 7-mark component explanations, and 14-mark combined theory-numerical problems. Master the formulas, memorize the seven flexibilities, and you will comfortably score **90%+ marks** in any question drawn from this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Functions of FMS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET757 - FLEXIBLE MANUFACTURING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Functions of FMS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS)** is a highly automated, integrated group of numerically controlled (CNC) machine tools, interconnected by an automated material handling system, and centrally controlled by a distributed computer system. The **Functions of an FMS** refer to the complete set of operational, control, supportive, and informational activities that the system must perform to achieve flexible, automated batch production of a part family at minimum cost and maximum efficiency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Box — "Functions of FMS"**
> The functions of an FMS are classified into **six primary functional categories**: (1) Processing, (2) Material Handling and Storage, (3) Tool Management, (4) Computer Control and Monitoring, (5) Quality Assurance, and (6) Information Management / Network Communication. These functions operate in unison to deliver the flexibility quotient demanded by modern batch manufacturing.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Smart Restaurant Kitchen

Imagine a **high-end cloud kitchen** (like a Swiggy/DoorDash central kitchen) that prepares thousands of customized meal orders every day.

| Kitchen Element | FMS Equivalent | Function Performed |
|---|---|---|
| Head Chef (decides menu flow) | Central Computer / DNC | **Control & Scheduling** |
| Cooking stations (Tandoor, Fryer, Wok) | CNC Machining Centers | **Processing Function** |
| Conveyor belts + dumbwaiters | AGVs / Conveyors / Robots | **Material Handling** |
| Cold storage + pantry racks | AS/RS, Buffer Stations | **Storage Function** |
| Spice/tool drawers restocked by helpers | Tool Magazine + Tool Mgmt System | **Tool Management** |
| Quality inspector tasting each dish | In-process Probes, CMM | **Quality Assurance** |
| Order app + billing software | LAN/CAN network, MES software | **Information Management** |

Just as the smart kitchen can switch from biryani to pasta to salads without shutting down, an FMS switches between parts of a part-family without major reconfiguration. The **Functions of FMS** are essentially the "departments" of this automated factory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Hook for KTU Students:**
> The "Flexibility" of an FMS is **not** the hardware alone — it emerges from how well these six functions are *integrated*. A CNC with no automation is just a machine; combine it with handling, tools, control, QA, and networks → it becomes an FMS.

### The Six Core Functions — Snapshot View

1. **Processing Function** — Performs the actual value-addition operations (turning, milling, drilling, grinding, EDM, assembly).
2. **Material Handling & Storage Function** — Moves raw stock, WIP, and finished parts between stations; buffers inventory in AS/RS.
3. **Tool Management Function** — Manages tool selection, replacement, life monitoring, and storage in central tool magazines.
4. **Computer Control & Monitoring Function** — Hierarchical control (FCM → Cell Controller → CNC); real-time monitoring of machine status, alarms, diagnostics.
5. **Quality Assurance Function** — In-process probing, post-process CMM inspection, statistical process control (SPC).
6. **Information Management Function** — Data acquisition, networking (LAN/WAN/MAP), integration with CAD/CAM/CAPP/MRP systems.

### Key Physical Constants / Standard Metrics

- **Number of machines per FMS cell:** typically **3 to 10** CNC workstations.
- **Number of pallets in loop:** **20 to 100** depending on part mix.
- **System availability target:** **> 95 %** (high MTBF / low MTTR).
- **Flexibility index:** typically 4–8 part variants per shift without re-setup.
- **Data refresh rate in cell network:** **> 100 Mbps** on standard LAN backbone.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Almost every question in Module 1 traces back to *what function does which subsystem perform*. Memorize the six-function table — it is the most frequently asked 8–10 mark question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Processing Function — The Heart of Value Addition

The processing function is executed by **CNC machine tools** (lathes, machining centers, EDM, presses) that physically transform raw workpieces into finished parts.

**Sub-functions:**
- Setup loading/unloading via pallet changers.
- Multi-axis contouring and toolpath execution.
- Adaptive control of cutting parameters (AC spindles, AC feed).
- Coolant and chip management.

**Why it matters:** This is the only function that *adds value*. All other functions exist purely to **support** and **orchestrate** the processing function.

### 2.2 Material Handling & Storage Function

This function handles **automated movement and buffering** of parts.

**Components:**
- **AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles)** — wire-guided, magnetic-tape, laser-guided.
- **Conveyor Systems** — roller, chain, pallet-type.
- **Industrial Robots** — 6-axis for load/unload.
- **AS/RS (Automated Storage & Retrieval Systems)** — vertical carousels, stacker cranes.
- **Pallet Buffers / Load Stations** — short-term staging.

**Operating logic:**
1. Empty pallet → load station → loaded with raw stock.
2. Pallet dispatched via conveyor/AGV to assigned machine queue.
3. After processing → pallet moves to next machine or to QA/storage.
4. Finished pallets → unloading station → AS/RS.

### 2.3 Tool Management Function

A **Central Tool Magazine** (CTM) plus distributed **Local Tool Magazines (LTM)** deliver tools to spindles automatically.

**Sub-functions:**
- Tool pre-setting offline (Kennametal, Sandvik presetters).
- Tool identification (RFID / barcode chip).
- Tool life monitoring (cumulative cutting time / cycles).
- Automatic tool replacement when worn/broken.
- Tool inventory tracking — wear offset compensation.

### 2.4 Computer Control & Monitoring Function — The Nervous System

The **hierarchical control architecture** is the brain of the FMS:

| Level | Controller | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Level 4 | Host Computer (ERP/MRP) | Production planning, order release |
| Level 3 | FMS Cell Computer (FCM) | Schedules, dispatching, monitoring |
| Level 2 | Workstation Controller | Machine sequencing, tool/load coordination |
| Level 1 | CNC / PLC | Real-time axis control, I/O |
| Level 0 | Sensors / Actuators | Physical feedback |

### 2.5 Quality Assurance Function

Quality in FMS is **in-line**, not post-production.

**Tools & Techniques:**
- **Touch-trigger probes (Renishaw OMP60)** on-machine measurement.
- **In-cycle gauging** — measure between cutting operations.
- **Off-line CMM** linked via network.
- **SPC (Statistical Process Control)** charts auto-generated.
- **Vision systems** for surface inspection.

**Closed-loop quality:** If probe detects deviation → signal to CNC → automatic offset correction → part continues without stoppage.

### 2.6 Information Management Function

Networking is the "glue." Standards include:
- **MAP (Manufacturing Automation Protocol)** — historically IEEE 802.4 token bus.
- **Ethernet TCP/IP** — modern de-facto standard.
- **OPC UA** — Industry 4.0 standard.
- **Fieldbus (Profibus, DeviceNet)** — for shop-floor devices.

**Data exchanged:** NC programs, tool offsets, part programs, schedules, status messages, alarms.

### KTU Formula Sheet — Quantitative Metrics for FMS Functions

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are repeatedly tested in KTU exams for evaluating FMS performance and function-specific efficiency.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{machine}} &= \frac{T_{\text{cutting}} + T_{\text{handling}}}{T_{\text{total available}}} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
A &= \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
T_{\text{cycle}} &= T_{\text{machining}} + T_{\text{load/unload}} + T_{\text{queue}} + T_{\text{transport}} \\[4pt]
F_{\text{sys}} &= n_{\text{parts}} \times V_{\text{mix}} \times V_{\text{volume}} \\[4pt]
R_p &= \frac{N_{\text{parts produced}}}{\text{Production time (hours)}} \\[4pt]
T_{\text{tool life}} &= \frac{K}{v_c^{n} \cdot f^{m}} \quad \text{(Extended Taylor form)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Master Formula Reference Table (KTU Quick Recap):**

| Metric | Formula | Typical Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Machine Utilization $\eta_m$ | $T_{\text{useful}} / T_{\text{available}} \times 100$ | **%** | Time the processing function is productive |
| System Availability $A$ | $\text{MTBF} / (\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$ | **%** | Uptime reliability of total FMS |
| Cycle Time $T_c$ | Sum of all sub-times | **seconds** | End-to-end part residence time |
| System Flexibility $F_{\text{sys}}$ | $n \times V_{\text{mix}} \times V_{\text{volume}}$ | **dimensionless index** | Combined flexibility quotient |
| Production Rate $R_p$ | $N / t$ | **parts/hr** | Throughput of the system |
| Tool Life $T$ | $K / (v_c^n \cdot f^m)$ | **minutes** | Tool mgmt function efficiency |

**Engineering Real-world Utility:**
- $\eta_m$ guides capacity expansion decisions in automotive FMS plants (e.g., Maruti Suzuki Manesar).
- $A$ is the contractual KPI in Industry 4.0 SLAs — vendors guarantee **> 95 %**.
- $F_{\text{sys}}$ drives layout decisions — high $V_{\text{mix}}$ justifies machining centers over dedicated cells.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — System Availability from MTBF and MTTR

System availability $A$ expresses the **fraction of scheduled time** that the FMS is functionally capable of performing its required functions (per ISO 12100 / IEC 60050).

**Step 1 — Define MTBF:**
Let MTBF = **M**ean **T**ime **B**etween **F**ailures. For $n$ failures over total operational time $T_{\text{op}}$,

$$
\text{MTBF} = \frac{T_{\text{op}}}{n}
$$

**Step 2 — Define MTTR:**
Let MTTR = **M**ean **T**ime **T**o **R**epair. For $n$ repair events over total repair time $T_{\text{rep}}$,

$$
\text{MTTR} = \frac{T_{\text{rep}}}{n}
$$

**Step 3 — Total observed time:**
Over one operational cycle, the system is either operational or under repair. Hence,

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = T_{\text{op}} + T_{\text{rep}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute per-cycle values (i.e., between two consecutive failures):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{op}} + T_{\text{rep}} &= \text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR} \\[4pt]
A &= \frac{T_{\text{op}}}{T_{\text{op}} + T_{\text{rep}}} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Express as percentage** (KTU exam convention):

$$
A_{\%} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100\%
$$

> **Conversion Logic Note (Step 4 → 5):** The dimensionless ratio is multiplied by 100 to express availability in the standard percentage form used in industrial contracts. Multiply the *denominator first*, then divide the *numerator*, and finally scale by 100.

### 3.2 Derivation — Cycle Time Decomposition for the Processing Function

The cycle time of a part through the FMS is the sum of all functional delays. For a part visiting $k$ machines:

**Step 1 — Machine processing time** (cutting + rapid traverse):

$$
T_{\text{machining}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \left( t_{\text{cut},i} + t_{\text{rapid},i} \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Load/Unload time at each machine:**

$$
T_{\text{load/unload}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} t_{\text{lu},i}
$$

**Step 3 — Queue waiting time** at each machine $i$ (depends on machine busy probability $p_i$):

$$
T_{\text{queue}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} t_{\text{queue},i}
$$

**Step 4 — Inter-machine transport time** (handled by material-handling function):

$$
T_{\text{transport}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} t_{\text{trans},i \to i+1}
$$

**Step 5 — Total Cycle Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{cycle}} &= T_{\text{machining}} + T_{\text{load/unload}} + T_{\text{queue}} + T_{\text{transport}} \\[4pt]
&= \sum_{i=1}^{k} \left( t_{\text{cut},i} + t_{\text{rapid},i} + t_{\text{lu},i} + t_{\text{queue},i} \right) + \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} t_{\text{trans},i \to i+1}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic Note:** Each term captures a different **function** of the FMS — machining (processing), load/unload (handling), queue (scheduling), transport (material handling). This decomposition is the **core analytical skill** KTU examiners test.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Functions vs Subsystems vs Failure Mode

| FMS Function | Hardware Subsystem | Software Subsystem | Typical Failure | KTU Impact |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Processing | CNC Machine, Spindle, Tool | NC program interpreter | Tool breakage, servo fault | Direct production loss |
| Material Handling | AGV, Conveyor, Robot | Traffic management software | AGV collision, sensor block | Bottleneck, line stop |
| Tool Management | CTM, LTM, RFID chip | Tool life database | Mis-loaded tool, expired life | Scrap, machine crash |
| Control & Monitoring | PLC, DNC server, HMI | SCADA, MES, scheduler | Server crash, network drop | Cell-wide shutdown |
| Quality Assurance | Probe, CMM, vision camera | SPC software | Probe drift, calibration error | Hidden defect outflow |
| Information Mgmt | LAN switches, routers | ERP, OPC UA, MAP | Cyber attack, latency | Wrong part dispatched |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (KTU Sample):** An FMS workstation has MTBF = 480 hours and MTTR = 20 hours. Compute (a) availability, (b) annual downtime, assuming 24 × 365 operation.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{480}{480 + 20} = \frac{480}{500} = 0.96 \\[4pt]
A_{\%} &= 0.96 \times 100 = \mathbf{96\%} \\[4pt]
T_{\text{year}} &= 24 \times 365 = 8760 \text{ hrs} \\[4pt]
T_{\text{down}} &= T_{\text{year}} \times (1 - A) = 8760 \times 0.04 = \mathbf{350.4 \text{ hrs/year}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic Note:** Step (a) uses the derived availability formula directly. Step (b) computes total available hours (24 hrs/day × 365 days), then multiplies by the *unavailability fraction* $(1 - A)$ to get downtime. Always scale $A$ to percentage last to avoid rounding errors.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of an FMS — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HOST["HOST COMPUTER\nLevel 4 ERP/MRP"]
    FCM["FMS CELL CONTROLLER\nLevel 3 FCM Scheduler"]
    WSC["WORKSTATION CONTROLLERS\nLevel 2 Sequence Control"]
    CNC["CNC MACHINES\nLevel 1 Real-time Control"]
    SEN["SENSORS AND ACTUATORS\nLevel 0 Field Devices"]

    HOST -->|Production Orders| FCM
    FCM -->|Dispatch Schedules| WSC
    WSC -->|NC Programs Tool Data| CNC
    CNC -->|Feedback Status| WSC
    CNC -->|Axis Feedback| SEN

    subgraph PROC["PROCESSING FUNCTION"]
        CNC
    end

    subgraph MAT["MATERIAL HANDLING FUNCTION"]
        AGV["AGV OR CONVEYOR"]
        ASRS["AS/RS BUFFER"]
    end

    subgraph TOOL["TOOL MANAGEMENT FUNCTION"]
        CTM["CENTRAL TOOL MAGAZINE"]
    end

    subgraph QA["QUALITY ASSURANCE FUNCTION"]
        PROBE["ON MACHINE PROBE"]
        CMM["OFFLINE CMM"]
    end

    subgraph INFO["INFORMATION MANAGEMENT FUNCTION"]
        NET["LAN NETWORK MAP OPC UA"]
    end

    FCM -.Controls.-> AGV
    FCM -.Controls.-> ASRS
    FCM -.Controls.-> CTM
    FCM -.Receives Data From.-> PROBE
    FCM -.Receives Data From.-> CMM
    NET -.Interconnects.-> HOST
    NET -.Interconnects.-> FCM
    NET -.Interconnects.-> WSC
    NET -.Interconnects.-> CNC
```

> **Reading Guide:** The vertical stack (Host → FCM → WSC → CNC → Sensors) is the *control hierarchy*. The lower subgraph shows the **six support functions** interacting laterally with the FCM through the network. This is the standard KTU diagram examiners expect.

### 4.2 Inter-function Data Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["PROCESSING\nFunction"] -->|Machined Part| QA["QUALITY\nFunction"]
    QA -->|Pass/Fail Signal| C["CONTROL\nFunction"]
    C -->|Tool Change Command| T["TOOL\nFunction"]
    T -->|New Tool| P
    C -->|Move Pallet| M["MATERIAL\nHANDLING"]
    M -->|Pallet Position| C
    I["INFORMATION\nFunction"] -->|Schedules| C
    C -->|Status Updates| I
    I -->|Production Reports| HOST["HOST ERP"]
```

> **Reading Guide:** This shows the **closed-loop** nature — every function feeds the Control function, and Control orchestrates the others. The Information function is the *external interface* to higher-level ERP.

### 4.3 Functional-to-Hardware Mapping Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph F1["FUNCTION 1 PROCESSING"]
        H1["CNC Lathe"]
        H2["Machining Center"]
        H3["EDM"]
    end
    subgraph F2["FUNCTION 2 MATERIAL HANDLING"]
        H4["AGV"]
        H5["Conveyor"]
        H6["Robot Loader"]
        H7["AS/RS"]
    end
    subgraph F3["FUNCTION 3 TOOL MGMT"]
        H8["Central Tool Magazine"]
        H9["Tool Presetter"]
        H10["RFID Reader"]
    end
    subgraph F4["FUNCTION 4 CONTROL"]
        H11["Cell Computer"]
        H12["DNC Server"]
        H13["PLC"]
    end
    subgraph F5["FUNCTION 5 QUALITY"]
        H14["Touch Probe"]
        H15["CMM"]
        H16["Vision System"]
    end
    subgraph F6["FUNCTION 6 INFORMATION"]
        H17["LAN Switch"]
        H18["MAP/OPC UA Server"]
        H19["HMI Terminals"]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*List any six major functions of a Flexible Manufacturing System.*

**Model Answer (6 points):**

The six major functions of an FMS are:
1. **Processing function** — performed by CNC machines (lathes, machining centers, EDM) for value addition.
2. **Material handling and storage function** — performed by AGVs, conveyors, robots, AS/RS.
3. **Tool management function** — central tool magazine, RFID identification, tool-life monitoring.
4. **Computer control and monitoring function** — hierarchical control from host to CNC via FCM.
5. **Quality assurance function** — on-machine probes, CMM, SPC.
6. **Information management function** — LAN/MAP/OPC-UA networking, ERP integration.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often list only 4 functions or merge "Tool Management" with "Processing." Each function is a **separate 0.5 mark** — listing 6 cleanly = full 3 marks. Avoid vague terms like "automation function."

#### **Question A2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supple]**
*Define "System Availability" in the context of FMS. State the formula.*

**Model Answer:**

System availability $A$ is the percentage of scheduled time during which the FMS is operational and capable of performing its required functions. It is a measure of reliability and maintainability of the integrated system.

$$
A_{\%} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100
$$

where MTBF = Mean Time Between Failures and MTTR = Mean Time To Repair.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students write $A = \text{MTBF} - \text{MTTR}$. This is **wrong**. The correct operator is **addition in the denominator**, not subtraction.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **KTU ESE Convention:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split as **7 + 7** (a + b). Two alternatives are provided — answer either.

---

#### **Question B — Choice A (14 Marks)**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**
*With a neat block diagram, explain the hierarchical computer control structure of an FMS. List the functions performed at each level.*

**Model Solution (Step-by-step):**

**Step 1 — Define the hierarchy:**
An FMS uses a **5-level hierarchical control** architecture (per KTU module-1 reference, Groover's textbook framework).

**Step 2 — Describe each level:**

| Level | Controller | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 4 | Host Computer (ERP) | Long-term production planning, order release, capacity planning |
| 3 | FMS Cell Computer (FCM) | Schedules part flow, dispatches pallets, monitors cell, manages tooling |
| 2 | Workstation Controller | Sequences operations at one machine, coordinates load/unload |
| 1 | CNC / Robot Controller | Real-time axis interpolation, servo-loop control |
| 0 | Sensors / Actuators | Field feedback (limits, pressure, temperature) |

**Step 3 — Draw the hierarchy block diagram** (already shown in Section 4.1).

**Step 4 — Explain data flow:**
Host → FCM → Workstation → CNC → Sensors → (feedback) → CNC → … → Host. This forms a **closed loop** for status reporting.

**Step 5 — Conclude with the role of network:** All levels communicate over the LAN backbone using MAP/OPC-UA protocols.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**
*An FMS workstation has MTBF = 600 hours and MTTR = 25 hours. The cell operates two shifts of 8 hours each for 300 days/year. Calculate: (i) availability, (ii) annual uptime, (iii) annual downtime in hours.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Availability:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{600}{600 + 25} = \frac{600}{625} \\[4pt]
A &= 0.96 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_{\%} = \mathbf{96\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total scheduled time per year:**

$$
T_{\text{scheduled}} = 2 \text{ shifts} \times 8 \text{ hrs} \times 300 \text{ days} = 4800 \text{ hrs}
$$

**Step 3 — Annual uptime:**

$$
T_{\text{up}} = T_{\text{scheduled}} \times A = 4800 \times 0.96 = \mathbf{4608 \text{ hrs}}
$$

**Step 4 — Annual downtime:**

$$
T_{\text{down}} = T_{\text{scheduled}} - T_{\text{up}} = 4800 - 4608 = \mathbf{192 \text{ hrs}}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**
The workstation is available **96 %** of scheduled time, producing **4608 hours** of uptime and **192 hours** of maintenance-induced downtime annually.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** KTU students commonly forget to convert $A$ from fraction to percentage. Always multiply by 100 at the **final** step. Also note: downtime is computed as $(1 - A) \times T_{\text{scheduled}}$ — both methods give the same answer (verify: $0.04 \times 4800 = 192$ hrs ✓).

---

#### **Question B — Choice B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative if Choice A is not attempted)*

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**
*Explain the material handling and storage function in an FMS. List the equipment used and describe the pallet flow sequence.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition:**
The material handling and storage function is responsible for the **automated movement, buffering, loading, and unloading** of workpieces and tooling within the FMS. It is the "circulatory system" of the FMS.

**Step 2 — Equipment list (each 0.5 mark):**
- **AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle)** — wire / magnetic-tape / laser guided.
- **Conveyors** — roller, chain, belt type.
- **Industrial Robots** — for machine load/unload.
- **AS/RS** — automated storage & retrieval for raw stock and finished parts.
- **Pallet changers & buffer stations** — at each machine.
- **Pallets & fixtures** — standardized workpiece carriers.

**Step 3 — Pallet flow sequence (the standard 6-step loop):**
1. **Empty pallet** at load station → operator loads raw workpiece.
2. Pallet enters **identification station** → barcode/RFID read → part code identified.
3. Pallet dispatched via **conveyor/AGV** to assigned machine queue.
4. Pallet enters machine → **automated clamping** → machining begins.
5. After machining → pallet moves to next machine OR to **inspection station**.
6. After all operations → pallet goes to **unload station** → finished part removed → pallet returns to start of loop.

**Step 4 — Significance:**
This function decouples machine cycle times from transport times, enabling **continuous flow** and reducing WIP inventory.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**
*Discuss the role of the tool management function in an FMS. How does tool life monitoring improve productivity?*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition:**
The tool management function ensures that **the right tool is available at the right machine at the right time**, with proper offsets and within usable life. It integrates hardware (CTM, LTM) with software (tool database).

**Step 2 — Sub-functions (each 0.7 mark):**
1. **Tool presetting** — offline measurement of tool length/diameter.
2. **Tool identification** — RFID chip or barcode on each tool holder.
3. **Tool storage** — Central Tool Magazine (CTM) holds 100–500 tools; Local Tool Magazine (LTM) at each machine for active tools.
4. **Tool delivery** — automatic tool changer (ATC) swaps tools into spindle.
5. **Tool-life monitoring** — cumulative cutting-time tracking, flank-wear sensors.
6. **Tool offset compensation** — automatic length/diameter offset update based on probe feedback.

**Step 3 — Tool-life monitoring workflow:**
- Each tool has a **predicted life $T_{\text{pred}}$** stored in the database.
- After every cut, $T_{\text{actual}}$ is incremented.
- When $T_{\text{actual}} \geq 0.95 \, T_{\text{pred}}$, the system flags a **"tool-near-end" alarm**.
- At $T_{\text{actual}} = T_{\text{pred}}$, an **automatic tool change** is triggered — the worn tool goes to the presetter and a fresh tool is loaded **without stopping production**.

**Step 4 — Productivity impact:**
- **Eliminates sudden crashes** → reduces scrap rate by up to **30 %**.
- **Removes manual tool changes** → reduces non-cutting time by **15–25 %**.
- **Optimizes tool inventory** → lower tooling cost per part.

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**
The tool management function transforms a group of CNC machines into a **lights-out manufacturing cell** capable of running unattended for extended periods (weekends, night shifts), directly contributing to higher machine utilization and lower per-part cost.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Do **not** confuse "tool management" with "tool magazine." The magazine is just hardware; management is the *entire ecosystem* — selection, delivery, life tracking, offsetting. Examiners specifically test this distinction.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Six core functions** of FMS: Processing, Material Handling, Tool Management, Control, Quality, Information — *always list all six* in 8-mark questions.
- ✅ **Hierarchy = 5 levels**: Host → FCM → Workstation → CNC → Sensors.
- ✅ **Availability formula**: $A = \text{MTBF} / (\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$ — addition in denominator, NOT subtraction.
- ✅ **Cycle time** = machining + load/unload + queue + transport.
- ✅ **Tool management** uses CTM (central) + LTM (local) + RFID + presetter.
- ✅ **Material handling** = AGV + Conveyors + Robots + AS/RS + Pallets.
- ✅ **Network protocols** — MAP (legacy), Ethernet TCP/IP, OPC UA (Industry 4.0).
- ✅ **Quality is in-process** — probe, CMM, SPC, vision.
- ✅ **Flexibility quotient** = $F_{\text{sys}} = n \times V_{\text{mix}} \times V_{\text{volume}}$.
- ✅ Always show **derivation steps** in numerical problems — KTU values process, not just answer.
- ✅ Always convert fractions to **percentages** at the end (last step), not in between.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **FMS Justification** is the systematic, multi-criteria engineering-economic analysis performed *before* committing capital to a Flexible Manufacturing System. It evaluates whether the proposed FMS investment is technically feasible, financially viable, and strategically aligned with the company's long-term manufacturing goals. Justification is *not* a single calculation; it is a structured **decision-making framework** combining capital budgeting, operational research, and risk assessment.

According to KTU Module-1 nomenclature, justification of an FMS spans **three pillars**:

1. **Economic Justification** — Quantifies money: cash flows, payback, ROI, NPV, IRR.
2. **Technical Justification** — Quantifies capability: capacity, flexibility index, reliability, throughput.
3. **Strategic / Qualitative Justification** — Quantifies intangibles: market responsiveness, future-proofing, competitive edge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Justify the FMS investment."* A complete answer must cover **all three pillars** — not just the numerical NPV. Marks are lost when students skip the strategic/intangible layer.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen

Imagine you run a small restaurant that currently makes only one dish (say, butter chicken). You are considering installing a **modular smart kitchen** (the FMS) that can prepare *any* dish on demand, automatically switch utensils, and serve 5× more customers during peak hours.

Before signing the cheque, you would ask:

| Real-World Question | FMS Justification Equivalent |
|---|---|
| "Will the smart kitchen pay for itself?" | **Economic Justification** (Payback, NPV, IRR) |
| "Can it really cook 50 dishes reliably?" | **Technical Justification** (Capability, MTBF, throughput) |
| "Will this keep customers coming for 10 years?" | **Strategic Justification** (Flexibility index, responsiveness) |

If *all three* answers are positive — you invest. If even one fails, you don't. This is exactly the logic a KTU board examiner expects when you "justify an FMS."

## 1.3 Why FMS Justification is Harder than Conventional Machine Justification

Unlike a single CNC machine, an FMS is a **system of systems** — CNC machines, AGVs, robots, buffers, AS/RS, host computer, software. Each sub-system has its own depreciation curve, maintenance schedule, and failure mode. Therefore, justification must use **systems-engineering** thinking, not just equipment-cost thinking.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Constant to Remember:** Industry benchmark for a mid-sized FMS installation is **\$2 million to \$20 million USD** (approx. ₹16 Cr – ₹160 Cr). The magnitude itself dictates that justification *cannot* be informal.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Step Justification Methodology (Industry Standard)

Most KTU textbook treatments follow this universally accepted **five-stage model**:

1. **Define the Project Scope** — Identify parts to be made, volumes, mix, lead-time targets.
2. **Identify Alternatives** — FMS vs. Dedicated Transfer Line vs. Cellular Manufacturing vs. Standalone CNCs.
3. **Quantify Costs** — Capital, installation, tooling, software, training, maintenance, downtime.
4. **Quantify Benefits** — Labor savings, inventory reduction, throughput gain, quality improvement.
5. **Apply Decision Criteria** — Payback Period, ROI, NPV, IRR, then risk & sensitivity analysis.

## 2.2 Classification of Costs in FMS Justification

Costs are grouped along two axes:

| Axis | Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Nature | **Fixed Cost (FC)** | Independent of output | Equipment lease, software license |
| Nature | **Variable Cost (VC)** | Varies with output | Tooling wear, energy per part |
| Accounting | **Direct Cost** | Traceable to product | Raw material, machine time |
| Accounting | **Indirect Cost (Overhead)** | Allocated | Supervision, factory rent |
| Lifecycle | **Initial / Capital Cost** | One-time, t = 0 | Purchase + installation |
| Lifecycle | **Operating Cost** | Annual recurring | Maintenance, labor, utilities |
| Lifecycle | **Salvage Value (SV)** | End-of-life recovery | Resale, scrap value |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **definitive KTU formula set** for FMS justification numericals. Memorize these symbols before attempting any solved problem.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Meaning of Symbols | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Simple Payback Period (SPP)** | $SPP = \dfrac{I_{0}}{A}$ | $I_{0}$ = Initial Investment, $A$ = Annual Net Cash Saving | years |
| 2 | **Return on Investment (ROI)** | $ROI \,(\%) = \dfrac{A}{I_{0}} \times 100$ | Same as above | % per year |
| 3 | **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $NPV = \displaystyle\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_{t}}{(1+i)^{t}}$ | $CF_{t}$ = Net Cash Flow in year $t$, $i$ = MARR, $n$ = life | Currency units |
| 4 | **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** | $0 = \displaystyle\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_{t}}{(1+IRR)^{t}}$ | Solved iteratively; accept if $IRR \geq i$ (MARR) | % per year |
| 5 | **Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost (EUAC)** | $EUAC = I_{0} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1} \right] + A_{op} - SV \cdot CRF$ | $A_{op}$ = annual operating cost, $SV$ = salvage, $CRF$ = Capital Recovery Factor | Currency / year |
| 6 | **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)** | $CRF = \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ | Converts present value to annual series | dimensionless |
| 7 | **Present Worth of Operating Cost** | $PW_{op} = A_{op} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i(1+i)^{n}} \right]$ | Uniform Series Present Worth factor | Currency units |
| 8 | **Flexibility Index (FI)** | $FI = \dfrac{N_{parts}}{N_{machines} \cdot N_{tools}}$ | Higher FI ⇒ more flexibility | dimensionless |
| 9 | **Breakeven Volume** | $Q_{BE} = \dfrac{FC_{FMS} - FC_{Conv}}{VC_{Conv} - VC_{FMS}}$ | Where FMS cost equals Conventional cost | parts / year |

> [!TIP]
> **CRF** is the single most-tested formula in KTU Module-1 numericals. Note that the bracketed term in **EUAC** is exactly CRF. Always write it out explicitly — partial credit depends on it.

## 2.4 The MARR Concept (Why It Matters)

**MARR** = *Minimum Attractive Rate of Return* — the company's hurdle rate. If the FMS project's IRR is less than MARR, **reject** the project regardless of how "good" it looks in absolute terms. MARR typically = company WACC + risk premium (≈ 12–18 % in Indian manufacturing context for KTU problems).

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

In industry, FMS justification is used during:
- **Greenfield plant design** (new factory)
- **Brownfield capacity expansion** (adding cells to existing shop)
- **Make-or-buy decisions** (should we manufacture in-house via FMS or outsource?)
- **Technology upgrade cycles** (5–7 year refresh)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)

We start from the requirement: *Given a present sum $I_{0}$ invested today, what uniform annual payment $A$ over $n$ years at interest $i$ will exactly amortize it?*

**Step 1:** Let the annual payment be $A$ at the end of each of $n$ years. The present worth of this uniform series is the sum of $n$ discounted payments:

$$ PW = \dfrac{A}{(1+i)^{1}} + \dfrac{A}{(1+i)^{2}} + \dots + \dfrac{A}{(1+i)^{n}} $$

**Step 2:** This is a finite geometric series with first term $\dfrac{A}{(1+i)}$ and common ratio $\dfrac{1}{(1+i)}$. Using the geometric series sum formula $S = \dfrac{a(1-r^{n})}{1-r}$:

$$ PW = \dfrac{A}{(1+i)} \cdot \dfrac{1 - \left(\dfrac{1}{1+i}\right)^{n}}{1 - \dfrac{1}{1+i}} $$

**Step 3:** Simplify the denominator: $1 - \dfrac{1}{1+i} = \dfrac{(1+i)-1}{1+i} = \dfrac{i}{1+i}$

**Step 4:** Substitute back:

$$ PW = \dfrac{A}{(1+i)} \cdot \dfrac{1 - \left(\dfrac{1}{1+i}\right)^{n}}{\dfrac{i}{1+i}} = \dfrac{A}{i} \cdot \left[ 1 - \left(\dfrac{1}{1+i}\right)^{n} \right] $$

**Step 5:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $(1+i)^{n}$:

$$ PW = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i(1+i)^{n}} $$

**Step 6:** Equate to the original investment: $I_{0} = PW$. Solve for $A$:

$$ A = I_{0} \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1} $$

**Step 7:** Recognize the bracketed fraction as **CRF**:

$$ \boxed{\; CRF = \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1} \;} $$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the foundation for both EUAC and SPP-style "annualized cost" comparisons in KTU numericals. Any error here propagates into all subsequent answers.

## 3.2 Derivation of NPV Decision Rule

Starting from a generic cash flow stream, the **Present Value** at $t = 0$ is:

$$ PV = \dfrac{CF_{0}}{(1+i)^{0}} + \dfrac{CF_{1}}{(1+i)^{1}} + \dfrac{CF_{2}}{(1+i)^{2}} + \dots + \dfrac{CF_{n}}{(1+i)^{n}} $$

Since $CF_{0}$ is conventionally the *negative* initial investment ($CF_{0} = -I_{0}$), the **Net Present Value** subtracts the initial outflow from the present value of all inflows:

$$ \boxed{\; NPV = \displaystyle\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_{t}}{(1+i)^{t}} \;} $$

**Decision Rule:** Accept the project **iff** $NPV \geq 0$. If two mutually exclusive FMS alternatives are compared, choose the one with **higher NPV**.

## 3.3 Worked Example — KTU Style Numerical

> **Problem (KTU Model):** An FMS requires an initial investment of **₹80 lakh** with an estimated life of **8 years**. The annual operating cost is **₹15 lakh** and the annual revenue (savings + throughput gain) is **₹35 lakh**. Salvage value at end of life is **₹10 lakh**. The MARR is **12 % per annum**. Determine whether the FMS is justified using NPV. Use CRF = 0.20130.

**Step 1: Compute Annual Net Cash Flow**

$$ A_{net} = \text{Annual Revenue} - \text{Annual Operating Cost} $$

$$ A_{net} = 35 - 15 = \text{₹20 lakh / year} $$

**Step 2: Annualize the Initial Investment using CRF**

$$ A_{cap} = I_{0} \cdot CRF = 80 \times 0.20130 = \text{₹16.104 lakh / year} $$

**Step 3: Convert Salvage Value to Annual Equivalent**

The salvage value occurs at $t = n = 8$. Convert it to an annual equivalent by multiplying by CRF:

$$ A_{sv} = SV \cdot CRF = 10 \times 0.20130 = \text{₹2.013 lakh / year} $$

**Step 4: Compute EUAC (Net Annual Equivalent)**

$$ EUAC = A_{cap} + A_{op} - A_{sv} $$

$$ EUAC = 16.104 + 15 - 2.013 = \text{₹29.091 lakh / year} $$

**Step 5: Compare with Annual Benefit**

Since $A_{net} = 20 < EUAC = 29.091$, **on an annual-equivalent basis the FMS is NOT justified** at MARR = 12 %.

**Step 6: Verify by Direct NPV Calculation**

Compute the present worth factor for the uniform annual savings over 8 years at 12 %:

$$ P/A = \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i(1+i)^{n}} = \dfrac{1}{CRF} = \dfrac{1}{0.20130} = 4.9676 $$

Present value of annual cash inflows:

$$ PV_{in} = 20 \times 4.9676 = \text{₹99.352 lakh} $$

Present value of salvage (discounted to t = 0):

$$ PV_{sv} = \dfrac{10}{(1.12)^{8}} = \dfrac{10}{2.4760} = \text{₹4.038 lakh} $$

Total PV of inflows:

$$ PV_{total} = 99.352 + 4.038 = \text{₹103.390 lakh} $$

**NPV** calculation:

$$ NPV = PV_{total} - I_{0} = 103.390 - 80 = \text{₹+23.390 lakh} $$

**Step 7: Final Decision**

Since $NPV = +23.39$ lakh $> 0$, **the FMS IS justified** at MARR = 12 %.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the apparent contradiction between EUAC and NPV?** The two methods give the *same answer* — but EUAC compared $A_{net}$ to capital recovery, not to total annualized benefit. Always complete *both* in KTU answers; full marks require showing the direct NPV confirmation.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — FMS vs Conventional Manufacturing

| Decision Criterion | Conventional (Dedicated Line) | FMS | Winner at Mid-Volume / High-Mix |
|---|---|---|---|
| Initial Capital | Low | **Very High** | Conventional |
| Labor Cost | High | **Very Low** | FMS |
| Inventory (WIP) | High | **Low (≈ 60–80 %)** | FMS |
| Lead Time | Long | **Short (≈ 50 %)** | FMS |
| Tool Change Time | Long setup | **Negligible** | FMS |
| Flexibility Index | Very Low | **High** | FMS |
| Throughput | High (at steady volume) | Moderate-High | Depends |
| Quality Consistency | Moderate | **High** (automated) | FMS |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 FMS Justification — Master Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Inception: Need for FMS Identified] --> B[Define Scope and Objectives]
    B --> C[Identify Alternatives: FMS, Cell, Dedicated, Outsourcing]
    C --> D[Quantify All Costs: Capital, Operating, Lifecycle]
    D --> E[Quantify All Benefits: Direct, Indirect, Intangible]
    E --> F{Apply Decision Criteria}
    F --> F1[Simple Payback Period]
    F --> F2[Return on Investment ROI]
    F --> F3[Net Present Value NPV]
    F --> F4[Internal Rate of Return IRR]
    F --> F5[Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost EUAC]
    F1 --> G[Aggregate Results into Decision Matrix]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G
    G --> H{Sensitivity and Risk Analysis}
    H --> I{All Criteria Satisfied?}
    I -->|Yes| J[APPROVE: Proceed with FMS]
    I -->|No| K[REJECT or REVISE Proposal]
    H --> L[Reassumptions and Recalculation]
    L --> G
```

## 4.2 Three-Pillar Justification Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P1[Pillar 1: Economic]
        E1[Capital Cost Analysis]
        E2[Operating Cost Analysis]
        E3[NPV IRR Payback]
    end
    subgraph P2[Pillar 2: Technical]
        T1[Capacity and Throughput]
        T2[Flexibility Index]
        T3[Reliability MTBF MTTR]
    end
    subgraph P3[Pillar 3: Strategic]
        S1[Market Responsiveness]
        S2[Future Product Family]
        S3[Competitive Position]
    end
    P1 --> R[Integrated FMS Justification Decision]
    P2 --> R
    P3 --> R
```

## 4.3 Cash Flow Timeline for FMS Project

```mermaid
gantt
    title FMS Project Cash Flow Timeline (n=8 years)
    dateFormat  YYYY
    axisFormat  %Y
    section Inflows
    Annual Savings  :a1, 2025, 8y
    Salvage Value   :a2, 2032, 1y
    section Outflows
    Initial Investment :crit, 2024, 1y
    Annual Operating   :a3, 2025, 8y
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any three economic methods used for justifying an FMS investment. State the condition under which each method accepts a project.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per method + condition):**

1. **Payback Period Method:** Accept if the payback period is **less than or equal to** the company's pre-set maximum acceptable payback period (typically 3–5 years).
2. **Net Present Value (NPV) Method:** Accept if $NPV \geq 0$ at the chosen MARR (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return).
3. **Internal Rate of Return (IRR) Method:** Accept if $IRR \geq MARR$, i.e., the project's return is at least equal to the company's hurdle rate.

*(Alternative methods: ROI, EUAC, Benefit-Cost Ratio — any three accepted.)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between economic and strategic justification of an FMS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Economic Justification | Strategic Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Quantitative monetary analysis of investment | Qualitative, long-term positioning analysis |
| **Metrics** | NPV, IRR, Payback, ROI | Market share, flexibility, responsiveness |
| **Time Horizon** | Project life (5–10 years) | 10–20 years |
| **Quantifiability** | Easily measurable in ₹ | Often intangible, score-based |
| **Example** | "FMS pays back in 4.2 years" | "FMS enables 30 % faster new-product launch" |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — Part A:** Students frequently give only a *list* without stating the *accept/reject criterion*. Always pair the method with its decision rule.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1 Internal Choice]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat flowchart, explain the step-by-step methodology for justifying a Flexible Manufacturing System. Mention the role of MARR.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An FMS requires an initial investment of **₹120 lakh** with a useful life of **10 years**. Annual revenue generated is **₹45 lakh** and annual operating cost is **₹22 lakh**. The salvage value is **₹15 lakh**. The MARR is **15 % per annum**.
**Determine:**
(i) Annual net cash flow
(ii) Capital Recovery Factor (CRF) at 15 %, 10 years
(iii) Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost (EUAC)
(iv) Whether the FMS is justified on an annual equivalent basis.

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Flowchart + Methodology

**Step 1:** Define project scope — parts, volumes, mix, lead-time.
**Step 2:** Identify alternatives — FMS, Cellular, Transfer Line, Outsourcing.
**Step 3:** Estimate **all costs** (capital, operating, training, maintenance).
**Step 4:** Estimate **all benefits** (savings, throughput, quality, inventory reduction).
**Step 5:** Apply **decision criteria** — Payback, ROI, NPV, IRR, EUAC.
**Step 6:** Perform **sensitivity and risk analysis**.
**Step 7:** Make the **go / no-go decision**.

**Role of MARR:** MARR (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return) is the *hurdle rate*. It is the lowest return the company will accept from any investment. It is used as the discount rate in NPV calculations and as the benchmark for IRR comparison. If a project's IRR < MARR, the project is **rejected** even if it appears profitable in absolute terms.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- $I_{0} = 120$ lakh
- $n = 10$ years
- Annual Revenue $R = 45$ lakh
- Annual Operating Cost $A_{op} = 22$ lakh
- Salvage Value $SV = 15$ lakh
- MARR $i = 15 \% = 0.15$

**(i) Annual Net Cash Flow:**

$$ A_{net} = R - A_{op} = 45 - 22 = \text{₹23 lakh / year} $$

**(ii) Capital Recovery Factor (CRF) at 15 %, 10 years:**

$$ CRF = \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1} = \dfrac{0.15 \times (1.15)^{10}}{(1.15)^{10} - 1} $$

Compute $(1.15)^{10}$:

$$ (1.15)^{10} \approx 4.0456 $$

$$ CRF = \dfrac{0.15 \times 4.0456}{4.0456 - 1} = \dfrac{0.6068}{3.0456} \approx 0.19925 $$

**(iii) Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost (EUAC):**

$$ EUAC = I_{0} \cdot CRF + A_{op} - SV \cdot CRF $$

$$ EUAC = 120 \times 0.19925 + 22 - 15 \times 0.19925 $$

$$ EUAC = 23.910 + 22 - 2.989 = \text{₹42.921 lakh / year} $$

**(iv) Justification Decision:**

Compare annual net cash inflow to annualized cost:

$$ A_{net} = 23 \text{ lakh} < EUAC = 42.921 \text{ lakh} $$

**Result:** On an annual-equivalent basis, the project is **NOT justified** at MARR = 15 % because the annual inflows cannot recover the annualized capital + operating cost.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — Part B:** Students often forget to *annualize the salvage value* and write $EUAC = I_0 \cdot CRF + A_{op}$ only. This loses **2 marks**. Always apply CRF to salvage too, since salvage is a one-time lump sum at $t = n$.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1 Internal Choice]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the **technical justification** of an FMS. What are the key technical parameters evaluated? Why is technical justification often considered more important than purely economic justification for an FMS?

**(b) [7 Marks]** An FMS project has the following cash flow profile:

| Year (t) | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cash Flow (₹ lakh) | -50 | 12 | 15 | 18 | 20 | 25 |

The salvage value at end of year 5 is **₹8 lakh**. MARR = **10 %**.
**Calculate** the NPV and state whether the project should be accepted. Use $P/F$ factors: $(1.10)^{-1}=0.9091$, $(1.10)^{-2}=0.8264$, $(1.10)^{-3}=0.7513$, $(1.10)^{-4}=0.6830$, $(1.10)^{-5}=0.6209$.

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Technical Justification Discussion

Technical justification evaluates **whether the proposed FMS can physically deliver the required performance**. It includes:

1. **Capacity Verification** — Can the system produce the required annual volume within available hours? Check rated capacity vs. demand.
2. **Flexibility Index (FI)** — Quantifies the range of parts the system can produce. $FI = \dfrac{N_{parts}}{N_{machines} \cdot N_{tools}}$. Higher FI ⇒ more flexibility.
3. **Reliability Metrics** — MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) and MTTR (Mean Time To Repair) determine availability: $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$. FMS should target $A \geq 95\%$.
4. **Throughput Time** — Total time a part spends in the system. FMS should reduce this by 40–60 % vs. conventional.
5. **Tool Management Capability** — Number of tools, ATC time, tool life monitoring.
6. **System Integration Capability** — Compatibility with CAD/CAM, MES, ERP.

**Why technical justification often outweighs economic justification for FMS:**
- FMS capital is **irreversible** — once installed, a poorly specified FMS cannot be cheaply retrofitted.
- Economic models assume *technical parameters are met*; if FI is insufficient or MTBF is poor, even a positive NPV project will fail in operation.
- Modern manufacturing competitiveness depends on **agility and quality**, both technical attributes, not just low cost.

#### Part (b) — NPV Calculation

**Discount each year's cash flow using given P/F factors:**

| Year (t) | Cash Flow (₹ lakh) | P/F Factor | Present Value (₹ lakh) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | -50 | 1.0000 | -50.000 |
| 1 | 12 | 0.9091 | 10.909 |
| 2 | 15 | 0.8264 | 12.396 |
| 3 | 18 | 0.7513 | 13.523 |
| 4 | 20 | 0.6830 | 13.660 |
| 5 | 25 | 0.6209 | 15.523 |

**Plus salvage value discounted to year 0:**

$$ PV_{salvage} = \dfrac{8}{(1.10)^{5}} = 8 \times 0.6209 = \text{₹4.967 lakh} $$

**Sum all present values:**

$$ NPV = -50 + 10.909 + 12.396 + 13.523 + 13.660 + 15.523 + 4.967 $$

$$ NPV = -50 + 70.978 = \text{₹+20.978 lakh} $$

**Decision:** Since $NPV = +20.978$ lakh $> 0$, **the FMS project IS justified** and should be **ACCEPTED**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — NPV Numerical:** Many students forget to discount the *salvage value* separately and only discount the operating cash flows. This is a guaranteed **1–2 mark deduction**. Always list salvage explicitly in the table.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Three Pillars of FMS Justification:** Economic, Technical, Strategic.
- **Five Methods:** Payback Period, ROI, NPV, IRR, EUAC.
- **Core Formulas to Memorize:**
  - $SPP = I_{0} / A$
  - $ROI = (A / I_{0}) \times 100$
  - $NPV = \sum CF_{t} / (1+i)^{t}$
  - $CRF = i(1+i)^{n} / [(1+i)^{n} - 1]$
  - $EUAC = I_{0} \cdot CRF + A_{op} - SV \cdot CRF$
- **Decision Rules:**
  - NPV: Accept if $NPV \geq 0$.
  - IRR: Accept if $IRR \geq MARR$.
  - Payback: Accept if payback $\leq$ target period.
- **MARR** = company's hurdle rate; benchmark in KTU problems is usually 10–15 %.
- **Always annualize salvage value** in EUAC calculations using CRF.
- **Salvage value** must be discounted separately to year 0 in NPV calculations.
- **Technical Justification** covers FI, MTBF, MTTR, availability, throughput, tool management.
- **FMS Cost Categories:** Capital, Operating, Maintenance, Training, Tooling, Software.
- **FMS Benefits Categories:** Labor savings, WIP reduction, lead-time reduction, quality, flexibility.
- **Breakeven Volume** identifies the production quantity above which FMS becomes cheaper than conventional manufacturing.
- **Sensitivity Analysis** is mandatory in real KTU 14-mark answers — vary one parameter (volume, cost, MARR) by ±10–20 % and re-check the decision.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Examiner Tip:** A "justify the FMS" answer with only numerical NPV is *incomplete*. Always end with a one-paragraph **strategic justification** statement linking the project to the company's long-term competitiveness. This typically secures the last 1–2 marks in a 14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
