A central computer tracks every book's "address" down to the *exact rack, bay, level, and depth cell*. 

This is precisely how an **AS/RS** operates:

- The **library shelves** = the *storage rack structure* (typically 10–40 m tall).
- The **robotic crane** = the *Storage/Retrieval (S/R) machine*.
- The **central library index** = the *WMS (Warehouse Management System)* / host computer.
- The **books** = unit loads (pallets, totes, containers).
- The **motion along the aisle, up the rack, and into the depth** = the **X, Y, Z axis motion** of the S/R machine.

The crane can locate *any single load* in a warehouse of tens of thousands of cells within **60–90 seconds** — a feat no human picker can replicate consistently.

## 1.3 Why AS/RS Exists in an FMS — The "Why" Behind the System

In a conventional factory, storage occupies **15–20% of the floor area** and accounts for up to **30% of total labor cost**. Manual storage systems suffer from:

1. **Low space utilization** (aisle widths of 3–4 m for forklift maneuvering).
2. **Inventory inaccuracy** (typical 65–80% accuracy in manual systems vs. **99.9%** in AS/RS).
3. **High damage rate** due to human error.
4. **Slow throughput** during peak demand.

AS/RS solves all four by replacing wide aisles with **narrow aisles (1–1.5 m)**, eliminating manual handling, providing **real-time WIP visibility**, and operating reliably **24×7 with no fatigue**.

## 1.4 Key Terminology (Board-Exam Ready)

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Unit Load** | The standardized pallet, tote, or container handled by the S/R machine |
| **Storage Rack** | The structural framework holding the unit loads in a 3-D grid |
| **S/R Machine** | The automated crane that travels on rails to store/retrieve loads |
| **Aisle** | The horizontal corridor in which the S/R machine travels |
| **Bay** | The vertical column of storage positions along the rack face |
| **Level** | The horizontal row of storage positions at a given height |
| **WMS** | Warehouse Management Software – the supervisory control layer |
| **Cycle Time** | Time for one complete store or retrieve operation |
| **Turnover Ratio** | Ratio of total SKUs to total storage positions (utilization indicator) |
| **Pick Face** | The I/O point where loads enter/leave the AS/RS |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Pearl:** KTU examiners frequently test the difference between **Storage Position**, **Storage Location**, and **Storage Cell**. Memorize: *Position = physical slot; Location = logical address in WMS; Cell = smallest addressable unit (rack–bay–level–depth).*

## 1.5 KTU-Mandated Classification of AS/RS

Per the **PEMET757 syllabus**, AS/RS systems are classified along two axes:

**A. Based on Unit Load Size:**
- **Unit-Load AS/RS** – handles full pallets (typically **1000–2000 kg**).
- **Mini-Load AS/RS** – handles small totes, bins, or cartons (typically **< 250 kg**).

**B. Based on Storage Geometry / Mechanism:**
- **Horizontal Carousel AS/RS**
- **Vertical Carousel AS/RS**
- **Vertical Lift Module (VLM)**
- **Fixed-Aisle Unit-Load S/R Crane**
- **Moveable-Aisle (Movable Rack) AS/RS**

This classification will appear in either Part A (definition-type) or Part B (descriptive 7/14-mark) questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Anatomy of an AS/RS

An AS/RS is composed of **four tightly coupled sub-systems**. Understanding each sub-system in depth is critical for KTU Part-B questions.

### 2.1.1 Storage Rack Structure
The rack is the *static skeleton* of the system. It must be engineered to withstand:
- **Static load** of stored inventory (seismic load in Kerala's Zone III).
- **Dynamic load** induced by the accelerating/decelerating S/R machine (typically **0.2–0.5g**).
- **Eccentric loading** when the S/R machine extends its fork asymmetrically.

Rack configurations include:
- **Single-deep racks** (1 unit load deep per cell).
- **Double-deep racks** (2 unit loads deep — requires reach-extended fork).
- **Flow racks** (with gravity roller conveyors — used for high-turnover SKUs).
- **Cantilever racks** (used for long items).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Rack Heights:** Unit-load AS/RS racks are typically **10–40 m** tall. Kerala's KTU board examiners commonly cite **30 m as a benchmark** for cycle-time problems.

### 2.1.2 Storage / Retrieval (S/R) Machine
The S/R machine is the *dynamic heart* of the AS/RS. It performs **simultaneous motion in three axes**:

| Axis | Motion | Typical Velocity | Drive Mechanism |
|------|--------|------------------|-----------------|
| **X-axis** | Horizontal travel along aisle length | **50–200 m/min** | Wheel + rail (VFD-controlled AC motor) |
| **Y-axis** | Vertical hoist of mast/telescope | **20–40 m/min** | Wire-rope hoist with counterweight |
| **Z-axis** | Fork extension/retraction into rack | **10–30 m/min** | Telescopic fork or shuttle mechanism |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Concept Check:** The three-axis *simultaneous* motion is what gives AS/RS its speed. A *sequential* motion design (move X, then Y, then Z) is cheaper but **40–60% slower** and is rarely used in FMS applications.

### 2.1.3 Input/Output (I/O) Stations
These are the *pick-face windows* through which loads enter and exit the AS/RS. They are typically:
- **Conveyor-fed** (roller or chain conveyor).
- **AGV-fed** (in an FMS environment).
- **Operator-fed** (in maintenance or low-volume mode).

A standard AS/RS has **2–4 I/O stations per aisle**, depending on throughput demand.

### 2.1.4 Control System (WMS + PLC Layer)
The control architecture is a **two-tier hierarchy**:

1. **Host Computer (WMS layer):** Manages inventory records, SKU addresses, slotting logic (random vs. class-based), and WIP visibility.
2. **PLC / S/R Machine Controller:** Receives store/retrieve commands from WMS and executes real-time servo motion control.

> [!NOTE]
> **FMS Integration:** In a fully integrated FMS, the AS/RS WMS communicates with the **Cell Controller** (e.g., of a CNC cell) via Ethernet/IP, PROFINET, or OPC-UA — not via paper tickets.

## 2.2 Operating Modes

The S/R machine operates in two canonical modes — both heavily tested in KTU board exams:

### Mode 1: Single-Command (SC) Cycle
The S/R machine performs **either a store OR a retrieve** in one trip from the I/O station. It travels to a cell, executes the operation, and returns to the I/O station.

**Typical application:** Low-throughput zones, asymmetric demand, end-of-shift batch loading.

### Mode 2: Dual-Command (DC) Cycle
The S/R machine performs **a store AND a retrieve simultaneously** in one trip. It first travels to a storage cell to deposit one load, then proceeds to a retrieval cell to pick another, returning to the I/O station with both operations complete.

**Typical application:** High-throughput FMS cells, where incoming raw stock and outgoing WIP are balanced.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Dual-command cycles are **30–50% more efficient** than single-command cycles because they amortize the aisle travel time over two transactions. KTU examiners *always* ask for the comparative cycle-time derivation.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — The Complete AS/RS Cheat Sheet

The following formulas are the **mathematical core** of any KTU AS/RS question. They are mathematically derived in Section 3.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | X-axis travel time | $t_x = \dfrac{L_x}{V_x}$ | s | $L_x$ = aisle length (m), $V_x$ = horizontal velocity (m/s) |
| 2 | Y-axis travel time | $t_y = \dfrac{L_y}{V_y}$ | s | $L_y$ = lift height (m), $V_y$ = vertical velocity (m/s) |
| 3 | Z-axis extension time | $t_z = \dfrac{L_z}{V_z}$ | s | $L_z$ = fork extension (m), $V_z$ = fork velocity (m/s) |
| 4 | **Single-Command (SC) Cycle Time** | $T_{sc} = 2\,t_x + 2\,t_y + t_z$ | s | Includes fork-extend time on both ends |
| 5 | **Dual-Command (DC) Cycle Time** | $T_{dc} = t_x + t_y + t_z + \sqrt{t_x^2 + t_y^2}$ | s | Diagonal simplification often used |
| 6 | **Turnover Ratio** | $R_{to} = \dfrac{N_{sku}}{N_{slot}} \times 100\%$ | % | $N_{sku}$ = active SKUs, $N_{slot}$ = total slots |
| 7 | **Space Utilization** | $\eta_s = \dfrac{V_{stored}}{V_{rack}} \times 100\%$ | % | Includes rack envelope volume |
| 8 | **S/R Machine Utilization** | $U_{sr} = \dfrac{T_{busy}}{T_{shift}} \times 100\%$ | % | $T_{busy}$ = sum of cycle times per shift |
| 9 | **Throughput Rate** | $\lambda = \dfrac{3600}{T_c}$ | loads/hr | For SC: $T_c = T_{sc}$; For DC: $T_c = T_{dc}/2$ |
| 10 | **Hitchhiker / Composite Cycle** | $T_{ch} = T_{dc} \cdot k_c + T_{sc} \cdot (1-k_c)$ | s | $k_c$ = fraction of DC cycles (0 to 1) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** The cycle-time formula is **scenario-dependent**. Never blindly apply $T = 2t_x + 2t_y + t_z$. Read the question carefully — if it says *dual-command*, the formula structure changes. Examiners award **zero partial credit** for the wrong cycle-time form.

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Formula

- **Why three travel times?** Because the S/R machine has three independent axes, and *each axis must be modeled separately* to compute realistic cycle times.
- **Why SC vs. DC?** Because FMS throughput depends on whether inbound and outbound flows can be *balanced* — DC works only when both directions have demand simultaneously.
- **Why turnover ratio?** Because empty slots cost money; an AS/RS with 30% turnover is a *financially poor* system, even if it functions mechanically.
- **Why utilization factor?** Because S/R machines are capital-intensive (INR 1–5 Cr per machine); under-utilization is a direct financial loss.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive FMS:** AS/RS stores engine blocks, gear-box housings, and sub-assemblies feeding robotic welding lines.
- **Pharmaceutical Warehousing:** Mini-load AS/RS stores vials and SKUs in **GMP-controlled** environments.
- **E-Commerce Fulfillment:** Carousel + mini-load hybrid systems handle 100,000+ SKUs in < 2,000 m² footprints.
- **Kerala Industrial Context:** KSIDC-promoted industrial parks (e.g., Cochin SEZ) routinely deploy mini-load AS/RS for marine-export and spice-packaging FMS cells.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section presents the **exhaustive mathematical derivation** of every formula appearing in the KTU Formula Sheet. Each transition is written out in full, with commentary on the engineering logic.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Single-Command (SC) Cycle Time

**Statement:** A single-command cycle begins at the I/O station, travels to a target storage cell, performs the operation, and returns to the I/O station.

**Step 1 — Identify the motion sequence.**

The S/R machine executes the following sequence in one SC cycle:

1. Travel **horizontally (X)** from I/O station to the target bay: time = $t_x$.
2. Travel **vertically (Y)** from ground level to the target level: time = $t_y$.
3. **Extend fork (Z)** into the rack to deposit/retrieve: time = $t_z$.
4. **Retract fork (Z)** back to the mast: time = $t_z$.
5. Travel **vertically (Y)** back down: time = $t_y$.
6. Travel **horizontally (X)** back to the I/O station: time = $t_x$.

**Step 2 — Define travel velocities.**

By definition, the time to traverse a distance at constant velocity is:

$$
t_x = \frac{L_x}{V_x}, \quad t_y = \frac{L_y}{V_y}, \quad t_z = \frac{L_z}{V_z}
$$

where:
- $L_x$ = horizontal aisle traversal distance (m).
- $L_y$ = vertical lift distance (m).
- $L_z$ = fork extension distance (m).
- $V_x, V_y, V_z$ = respective constant velocities (m/s).

**Step 3 — Sum the motion segments.**

Total SC cycle time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{sc} &= t_x + t_y + t_z + t_z + t_y + t_x \\
&= 2t_x + 2t_y + 2t_z
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Adopt the conventional KTU simplification.**

In most KTU textbook problems, fork extension and retraction are combined into a **single representative time $t_z$** (because $t_z$ is small relative to $t_x$ and $t_y$, and its inclusion on both ends would double-count the minor acceleration phases). This yields the standard form:

$$
\boxed{T_{sc} = 2t_x + 2t_y + t_z}
$$

> **Logic note:** This form is a *convention*, not a physical derivation. The KTU board examiner accepts either form, but the **standard textbook form** is $T_{sc} = 2t_x + 2t_y + t_z$. If a student's derivation arrives at $2t_x + 2t_y + 2t_z$, **full marks are still awarded** with the note "fork retracted separately."

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Dual-Command (DC) Cycle Time

**Statement:** In a dual-command cycle, the S/R machine executes one store operation AND one retrieve operation in a single trip.

**Step 1 — Conceptualize the DC motion path.**

The S/R machine must visit *two cells*: a storage cell and a retrieval cell. The geometric problem reduces to: *given two target points $(x_1, y_1)$ and $(x_2, y_2)$ in the rack's 2-D address plane, what is the minimum total travel time?*

**Step 2 — Define the DC motion sequence.**

The DC cycle follows the path: **I/O → Store cell → Retrieve cell → I/O**.

Total path components:

- I/O to Store cell: $t_x^{(1)} + t_y^{(1)}$
- Store cell to Retrieve cell: diagonal motion combining $\Delta t_x$ and $\Delta t_y$
- Retrieve cell to I/O: $t_x^{(2)} + t_y^{(2)}$

**Step 3 — Conservative KTU textbook form.**

The conservative (worst-case) form treats each leg independently:

$$
T_{dc} = 2t_x + 2t_y + 2t_z
$$

This is identical to SC — but it *overestimates* because the storage and retrieval cells are often closer to each other than to the I/O.

**Step 4 — Optimized DC form.**

If the storage and retrieval cells are reasonably close (a common FMS assumption), the diagonal simplification yields:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{dc} &\approx t_x + t_y + t_z + \sqrt{\,t_x^{2} + t_y^{2}\,} \\
&= t_x + t_y + t_z + \sqrt{\,t_x^{2} + t_y^{2}\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

The term $\sqrt{t_x^{2} + t_y^{2}}$ is the *Chebyshev-norm-like* combined travel between the store cell and the retrieve cell — assuming simultaneous X and Y motion.

**Step 5 — Comparison.**

- If both store and retrieve are at I/O adjacent cells, $T_{dc} \approx T_{sc}$.
- If both store and retrieve are at far cells, $T_{dc}$ approaches $2T_{sc}$ at worst.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Strategy:** KTU problems usually specify the cells. **Read the cell coordinates carefully**, compute $t_x$ and $t_y$ for each, and use the cell-to-cell distance for the inter-cell segment.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Turnover Ratio

**Statement:** The turnover ratio measures how *actively* the AS/RS storage is being used.

**Step 1 — Definition.**

$$
R_{to} = \frac{N_{sku}}{N_{slot}}
$$

where:
- $N_{sku}$ = number of active stock-keeping units (each occupying one slot, ideally).
- $N_{slot}$ = total available storage slots in the rack envelope.

**Step 2 — Percentage form for reporting.**

$$
R_{to}\,[ \%] = \frac{N_{sku}}{N_{slot}} \times 100
$$

**Step 3 — Engineering target.**

A well-managed AS/RS aims for **80–95% turnover**. Below 60% indicates over-investment in capacity; above 95% indicates risk of slot exhaustion and inbound congestion.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Throughput Rate

**Statement:** Throughput is the number of load transactions completed per unit time.

**Step 1 — Generic form.**

$$
\lambda = \frac{3600}{T_c}
$$

where $T_c$ is the effective cycle time (in seconds) and $\lambda$ is in loads per hour.

**Step 2 — For a mixed-mode operation.**

If the system uses DC for fraction $k_c$ of transactions and SC for the remaining $(1 - k_c)$, the *effective* cycle time is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{eff} &= k_c \cdot T_{dc} + (1 - k_c) \cdot T_{sc} \\
\lambda_{mixed} &= \frac{3600}{T_{eff}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Numeric sanity check.**

If $T_{sc} = 60\,s$ and $T_{dc} = 80\,s$, and $k_c = 0.7$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{eff} &= 0.7 \times 80 + 0.3 \times 60 \\
&= 56 + 18 = 74\,s \\
\lambda_{mixed} &= \frac{3600}{74} \approx 48.6\,\text{loads/hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic note:** Mixed-mode throughput is *always* less than the theoretical pure-DC throughput, because SC cycles consume more time per transaction.

---

## 3.5 Workhorse Numerical Problem — KTU Board Pattern

**Problem (KTU Model — 14 marks):**
An FMS cell requires an AS/RS with the following specifications:
- Aisle length: $L_x = 80\,m$.
- Rack height: $L_y = 12\,m$.
- Fork extension: $L_z = 1.5\,m$.
- Horizontal velocity: $V_x = 100\,m/min$.
- Vertical velocity: $V_y = 20\,m/min$.
- Fork velocity: $V_z = 15\,m/min$.
- 60% of transactions are dual-command.

**Compute:** (a) the SC and DC cycle times, and (b) the throughput rate.

**Solution — Part (a):**

Convert velocities to m/s:
- $V_x = 100/60 = 1.667\,m/s$.
- $V_y = 20/60 = 0.333\,m/s$.
- $V_z = 15/60 = 0.25\,m/s$.

Compute travel times:
- $t_x = 80 / 1.667 = 48\,s$.
- $t_y = 12 / 0.333 = 36\,s$.
- $t_z = 1.5 / 0.25 = 6\,s$.

SC cycle time:
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{sc} &= 2t_x + 2t_y + t_z \\
&= 2(48) + 2(36) + 6 \\
&= 96 + 72 + 6 = 174\,s
\end{aligned}
$$

DC cycle time (optimized form):
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{dc} &= t_x + t_y + t_z + \sqrt{t_x^{2} + t_y^{2}} \\
&= 48 + 36 + 6 + \sqrt{48^{2} + 36^{2}} \\
&= 90 + \sqrt{2304 + 1296} \\
&= 90 + \sqrt{3600} \\
&= 90 + 60 = 150\,s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution — Part (b):**

Effective cycle time with $k_c = 0.6$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{eff} &= 0.6 \times 150 + 0.4 \times 174 \\
&= 90 + 69.6 = 159.6\,s
\end{aligned}
$$

Throughput:
$$
\lambda_{mixed} = \frac{3600}{159.6} \approx 22.56\,\text{loads/hr}
$$

> **Sanity check:** A pure-SC throughput would be $3600 / 174 = 20.7$ loads/hr; a pure-DC throughput would be $3600 / 75 = 48$ loads/hr (since DC handles 2 transactions in 150 s). The mixed throughput of **22.56 loads/hr** lies between, as expected.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 5: S/R Machine Utilization

**Statement:** Utilization is the fraction of available shift time during which the S/R machine is executing productive cycles.

$$
U_{sr} = \frac{T_{busy}}{T_{shift}}
$$

where:
- $T_{busy} = N_{cycles} \times T_{eff}$.
- $T_{shift}$ = total shift duration in seconds (e.g., 8 h = 28,800 s).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Rule:** If $U_{sr} > 85\%$, the system is **starved for capacity** and a second S/R machine should be considered. If $U_{sr} < 30\%$, the AS/RS is **over-capitalized**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of an AS/RS

The following diagram maps the **functional architecture** of an AS/RS integrated with an FMS. Each block represents a subsystem, and arrows indicate the **information flow** (solid) and **material flow** (dashed).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HOSTLAYER["HOST COMPUTER LAYER WMS and Cell Controller"]
        WMS["WMS - Warehouse Management Software"]
        CC["FMS Cell Controller"]
    end

    subgraph CTRLLAYER["CONTROL LAYER PLC and Servo Drives"]
        PLC["PLC - S/R Machine Controller"]
        VFDX["VFD Drive - X Axis Horizontal"]
        VFDY["VFD Drive - Y Axis Vertical"]
        VFDZ["VFD Drive - Z Axis Fork"]
    end

    subgraph HARDLAYER["HARDWARE LAYER Mechanical Structure"]
        SR["S/R Machine Crane"]
        RACK["Storage Rack Structure"]
        IOS["I/O Stations Pick Face"]
        CONV["Conveyor or AGV Interface"]
    end

    WMS -- Inventory Records --> CC
    CC -- Store Retrieve Command --> PLC
    PLC -- Motion Command X --> VFDX
    PLC -- Motion Command Y --> VFDY
    PLC -- Motion Command Z --> VFDZ
    VFDX --> SR
    VFDY --> SR
    VFDZ --> SR
    SR -- Stores Retrieves --> RACK
    RACK -- Loads In Out --> IOS
    IOS -- Material Flow --> CONV
    CONV -- To From FMS Cell --> CC
```

> **How to read this diagram (KTU exam tip):** When asked *"Explain the integration of AS/RS with FMS"*, walk the examiner through the arrows in the order: *Host → Control → Hardware → Host*. This is the **canonical KTU answer structure**.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — AS/RS Classification Tree

The classification tree below captures the **canonical taxonomy** of AS/RS systems, which is a frequent 3-mark and 7-mark KTU question topic.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ASRS["Automated Storage and Retrieval System ASRS"]

    ASRS --> UL["Unit Load ASRS Pallets 1000 to 2000 kg"]
    ASRS --> ML["Mini Load ASRS Totes and Bins less than 250 kg"]

    UL --> FA["Fixed Aisle S R Crane"]
    UL --> MA["Movable Aisle S R Crane"]
    UL --> EC["End of Aisle Crane"]

    ML --> CR["Carousel Systems"]
    ML --> VLM["Vertical Lift Module VLM"]
    ML --> MM["Mini Load S R Crane"]

    CR --> HC["Horizontal Carousel"]
    CR --> VC["Vertical Carousel"]

    FA --> SD["Single Deep Racks"]
    FA --> DD["Double Deep Racks"]

    MA --> RAC["Rack Clamping Crane"]
```

## 4.3 Coordinate-Axis Schematic (Text Representation)

For a rack of dimensions $L_x$ (length) × $L_y$ (height) × $L_z$ (depth), the storage address of any load is the 3-tuple **(Bay, Level, Depth)**:

- **Bay** = X-coordinate along the aisle (1 to $N_{bay}$).
- **Level** = Y-coordinate along the height (1 to $N_{level}$).
- **Depth** = Z-coordinate into the rack (1 for single-deep, 1–2 for double-deep).

The S/R machine's instantaneous position is therefore $(x, y, z)$, and the WMS stores loads as address-triplets.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Visualization Tip:** When drawing a rack on the answer sheet, label the **X-axis along the aisle**, **Y-axis vertically**, and **Z-axis into the page (depth)**. KTU examiners award partial marks for correctly labeled coordinate axes even if the rest of the diagram is approximate.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define an Automated Storage and Retrieval System (AS/RS). List any four advantages of using AS/RS in a Flexible Manufacturing System.**

**Model Answer (≈ 100 words):**

An **Automated Storage and Retrieval System (AS/RS)** is a computer-controlled, integrated material handling system that automatically stores and retrieves loads in a defined rack structure using dedicated Storage/Retrieval (S/R) machines.

Four advantages in FMS context:

1. **High space utilization** — narrow aisles (1–1.5 m) replace wide forklift aisles.
2. **Improved inventory accuracy** — WMS tracking yields **99.9% accuracy** vs. 65–80% in manual systems.
3. **Reduced labor cost** — eliminates manual storage labor and shift-dependence.
4. **Faster throughput** — S/R machine cycle times of **60–90 s** enable tight FMS cell coupling.
5. *Bonus:* Enhanced safety and reduced product damage.

> **Valuator's note:** Award **1 mark** for the definition, **2 marks** for the four advantages (½ mark each). A fifth advantage as a bonus is good academic practice.

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Unit-Load AS/RS and Mini-Load AS/RS.**

**Model Answer (Tabular form, KTU-preferred):**

| Parameter | Unit-Load AS/RS | Mini-Load AS/RS |
|-----------|-----------------|-----------------|
| Load type | Pallets, large containers | Totes, bins, small cartons |
| Load weight | **1000–2000 kg** | **< 250 kg** |
| Rack height | 10–40 m | 4–12 m |
| S/R machine type | Heavy-duty aisle crane | Light-duty crane or carousel |
| Typical application | Heavy FMS, automotive | Pharmaceutical, e-commerce |
| Footprint | Large | Compact |
| Throughput | High per crane | Moderate |

> **Valuator's note:** Three or more parameter distinctions earn **full 3 marks**. A pure prose answer without table earns at most **2 marks**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:]** Answer **any ONE** of the following: Q(A) or Q(B). Each carries **14 marks** divided into sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION (A) — 14 Marks  [KTU University Exam – May 2024 pattern]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture** of an Automated Storage and Retrieval System integrated with a Flexible Manufacturing System. Describe the role of the WMS, PLC, and the S/R machine in this architecture. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An AS/RS has the following specifications:
- Aisle length: 60 m.
- Rack height: 10 m.
- Fork extension: 1.2 m.
- Velocities: $V_x = 80\,m/min$, $V_y = 18\,m/min$, $V_z = 12\,m/min$.

Calculate:
(i) The **Single-Command cycle time**. (3 marks)
(ii) The **Dual-Command cycle time** (use the optimized diagonal form). (2 marks)
(iii) The **throughput rate in loads/hr** if 50% of transactions are dual-command. (2 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

1. **Block diagram (3 marks):** Draw a three-tier architecture: *Host Layer (WMS + Cell Controller)* → *Control Layer (PLC + VFDs)* → *Hardware Layer (S/R Machine, Rack, I/O, Conveyor/AGV)*. Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the canonical diagram.

2. **WMS role (1.5 marks):** Maintains real-time inventory database, slotting logic (random/class-based), SKU addresses, and dispatching of store/retrieve commands.

3. **PLC role (1.5 marks):** Receives commands from WMS, decomposes them into axis-wise servo commands, handles interlocks (rack collision avoidance), and reports back cycle completion.

4. **S/R machine role (1 mark):** Executes the three-axis motion (X, Y, Z) to physically store/retrieve the unit load.

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Convert velocities to m/s:**
- $V_x = 80/60 = 1.333\,m/s$.
- $V_y = 18/60 = 0.300\,m/s$.
- $V_z = 12/60 = 0.200\,m/s$.

**Compute travel times:**
- $t_x = 60 / 1.333 = 45.0\,s$.
- $t_y = 10 / 0.300 = 33.3\,s$.
- $t_z = 1.2 / 0.200 = 6.0\,s$.

**(i) SC cycle time:**

$$
T_{sc} = 2t_x + 2t_y + t_z = 2(45) + 2(33.3) + 6 = 90 + 66.6 + 6 = 162.6\,s
$$

**(ii) DC cycle time (diagonal form):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{dc} &= t_x + t_y + t_z + \sqrt{t_x^{2} + t_y^{2}} \\
&= 45 + 33.3 + 6 + \sqrt{45^{2} + 33.3^{2}} \\
&= 84.3 + \sqrt{2025 + 1108.89} \\
&= 84.3 + \sqrt{3133.89} \\
&= 84.3 + 55.98 = 140.28\,s
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Mixed throughput with $k_c = 0.5$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{eff} &= 0.5 \times 140.28 + 0.5 \times 162.6 \\
&= 70.14 + 81.3 = 151.44\,s \\
\lambda &= \frac{3600}{151.44} \approx 23.78\,\text{loads/hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Velocity unit trap:** Many students forget to convert m/min → m/s. This costs **1–2 marks** silently.
> 2. **DC formula confusion:** Students frequently write $T_{dc} = 2t_x + 2t_y + 2t_z$ (which equals SC). Always use the *optimized diagonal form* unless the question explicitly forbids it.
> 3. **Mixed-mode throughput:** Forgetting that DC handles **2 loads per cycle** (not 1) inflates the apparent throughput incorrectly.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION (B) — 14 Marks  [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 pattern]

**(a)** Describe the **classification of AS/RS** based on unit load size and storage mechanism, with neat sketches of any two types. Explain the **Single-Command** and **Dual-Command** operating modes with a comparison of their cycle-time economics. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mini-load AS/RS has the following specifications:
- 4 horizontal aisles, each 30 m long.
- Rack height: 6 m.
- Fork extension: 0.8 m.
- Velocities: $V_x = 60\,m/min$, $V_y = 15\,m/min$, $V_z = 10\,m/min$.
- Storage capacity per aisle: 1500 totes.
- Annual demand: 250,000 tote-transactions.

Calculate:
(i) The **SC cycle time**. (2 marks)
(ii) The **turnover ratio** (assume active SKUs ≈ number of transactions per year / 12 monthly cycles). (2 marks)
(iii) Comment on whether the AS/RS design is **adequately utilized** for the given demand. (3 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

1. **Classification by unit load (2 marks):**
   - *Unit-Load AS/RS* (1000–2000 kg, pallets).
   - *Mini-Load AS/RS* (< 250 kg, totes/bins).

2. **Classification by mechanism (3 marks):**
   - *Fixed-Aisle Crane AS/RS.*
   - *Movable-Aisle (Rack-Clamping) AS/RS.*
   - *Vertical Carousel AS/RS.*
   - *Vertical Lift Module (VLM).*
   - *Horizontal Carousel AS/RS.*

   Include a labeled sketch of any two (e.g., aisle crane + carousel).

3. **SC vs. DC modes (2 marks):**
   - *SC mode:* One store OR one retrieve per trip.
   - *DC mode:* One store AND one retrieve in a single trip (more efficient when inbound = outbound).

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Convert velocities:**
- $V_x = 60/60 = 1.0\,m/s$.
- $V_y = 15/60 = 0.25\,m/s$.
- $V_z = 10/60 = 0.1667\,m/s$.

**Travel times:**
- $t_x = 30 / 1.0 = 30\,s$.
- $t_y = 6 / 0.25 = 24\,s$.
- $t_z = 0.8 / 0.1667 = 4.8\,s$.

**(i) SC cycle time:**

$$
T_{sc} = 2(30) + 2(24) + 4.8 = 60 + 48 + 4.8 = 112.8\,s
$$

**(ii) Turnover ratio:**

- Total slots = $4 \times 1500 = 6000$ totes.
- Active SKUs ≈ $250{,}000 / 12 \approx 20{,}833$.
- Turnover ratio = $(20{,}833 / 6000) \times 100\% \approx 347\%$.

> **Note:** A ratio > 100% is acceptable and indicates high turnover — each slot is used > 3 times per year.

**(iii) Utilization commentary:**

- Demand per day (assume 250 working days): $250{,}000 / 250 = 1000$ transactions/day.
- With 4 aisles and 8-hr shifts: $T_{shift} = 28{,}800\,s$.
- Available time per aisle per day: $28{,}800\,s$.
- Available cycles per aisle (SC mode): $28{,}800 / 112.8 \approx 255$ cycles/aisle.
- Total system capacity: $255 \times 4 = 1020$ cycles/day.

**Conclusion:** The system is operating at **~98% capacity** ($1000 / 1020$). This is **borderline over-utilized** — adding a fifth aisle, switching to DC mode, or operating in a 10-hr shift is recommended.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B – b):**
> 1. **Turnover ratio definition trap:** Many students compute it as *transactions / slots* without the percentage conversion. While mathematically similar, the KTU preferred form is the **percentage value** for clarity. Deduct **½ mark** if absent.
> 2. **Utilization commentary:** A bare numerical answer without the engineering interpretation (over-utilized / adequately utilized / under-utilized) is considered **incomplete**. Examiners award at most **1 of 3 marks** for the commentary sub-part if no qualitative judgment is offered.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- ✅ AS/RS = Computer-controlled rack + S/R machine + WMS + I/O stations.
- ✅ Three motion axes: **X (aisle), Y (hoist), Z (fork)**.
- ✅ **Unit-Load** = pallets (1000–2000 kg); **Mini-Load** = totes (< 250 kg).
- ✅ **Single-Command (SC)** = one transaction per trip; **Dual-Command (DC)** = two transactions per trip.
- ✅ SC cycle time: $T_{sc} = 2t_x + 2t_y + t_z$.
- ✅ DC cycle time (optimized): $T_{dc} = t_x + t_y + t_z + \sqrt{t_x^{2} + t_y^{2}}$.
- ✅ **Always convert velocities from m/min to m/s** before computing cycle times.
- ✅ Turnover ratio: $R_{to} = (N_{sku}/N_{slot}) \times 100\%$; healthy range **80–95%**.
- ✅ S/R machine utilization target: **30%–85%**; above 85% → add capacity.
- ✅ Mixed-mode throughput: $T_{eff} = k_c T_{dc} + (1 - k_c) T_{sc}$.
- ✅ Throughput: $\lambda = 3600 / T_{eff}$ in loads/hr.
- ✅ Address format: **(Bay, Level, Depth)** → WMS uses this 3-tuple.
- ✅ FMS integration: WMS ↔ Cell Controller ↔ PLC ↔ S/R Machine (Ethernet/IP, PROFINET, OPC-UA).
- ✅ Carousel AS/RS variants: Horizontal Carousel + Vertical Carousel.
- ✅ Vertical Lift Module (VLM) is *not* an aisle crane — it uses an extractor that moves inside a vertical column.
- ✅ Double-deep racks require reach-extended forks (longer $t_z$).
- ✅ AS/RS narrows aisles to **1–1.5 m**, freeing **30–40% floor space** vs. manual warehouses.
- ✅ Common KTU mistake: Forgetting that DC handles **2 transactions** (not 1) in throughput calculations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cutting Tools and Tool Management - Tool Preset
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET757 - FLEXIBLE MANUFACTURING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cutting Tools and Tool Management - Tool Preset

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Flexible Manufacturing Systems (FMS)**, **Tool Preset** (also called **Pre-setting** or **Off-line Tool Setting**) refers to the systematic process of measuring, adjusting, and recording the geometric and dimensional parameters of a cutting tool **outside the CNC machine tool** before it is loaded into the machine's tool magazine.

According to KTU 2024 syllabus terminology:

> **Tool Preset** is a preparatory activity in which the cutting tool's **projected length (Z-offset)**, **diameter/radius (X/Y-offset)**, **wear offsets**, and **tool identity (T-number)** are measured and stored on a dedicated device called a **Tool Presetter**, thereby eliminating in-machine setup time.

The preset data is transferred to the CNC controller via:
- **Manual entry** (punched tape / keyboard)
- **DNC (Direct Numerical Control) link**
- **Barcode / RFID chip scanning**
- **Networked tool management software (TMS)**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English

Imagine you are a chef preparing for a busy dinner service. Instead of measuring spices and cutting vegetables while the customers wait, you **pre-measure all ingredients into small containers** before service begins and label each container ("Salt", "Pepper", "Chili"). When an order comes in, you simply **grab the pre-labeled container** — no measuring needed.

**Tool Preset works exactly like this:**
- The **chef's measuring station** = **Tool Presetter machine**
- The **pre-measured spice containers** = **Pre-set cutting tools**
- The **kitchen label system** = **Tool identification code (barcode/RFID)**
- The **busy dinner rush** = **CNC machine cutting time (must not be wasted)**

By doing all measuring beforehand, the **CNC machine spends ZERO time on setup** — it only cuts metal. This directly enables **unmanned / lights-out machining**, which is the heart of FMS.

### 1.3 Why Tool Preset is Critical in FMS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In FMS, the goal is to achieve **continuous, unmanned production** of mixed parts on shared machines. Tool changes can occur **hundreds of times per shift**. Any in-machine tool measurement wastes expensive spindle hours. Hence, **100% of cutting tools must be pre-set offline** before entering the FMS tool pool.

### 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU Board Standard Definitions)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Tool Offset** | Numerical value telling the CNC how far the tool tip is from the machine zero point |
| **Length Offset (H-code)** | Compensates for tool length variation between sister tools |
| **Diameter Offset (D-code)** | Compensates for tool diameter for cutter compensation (G41/G42) |
| **Wear Offset** | Small incremental compensation applied as the tool wears |
| **Sister Tool** | Backup tool identical to the one in service, swapped when life expires |
| **Tool Magazine** | Rotating carousel / chain storing tools ready for automatic changing |
| **Tool Ident Number (T-code)** | Address code (e.g., T01, T12) identifying a unique tool slot |
| **Pot / Pocket** | Individual slot inside the tool magazine where one tool resides |
| **Standby Tool** | Tool present in the magazine but not currently in the spindle |
| **Active Tool** | Tool currently mounted in the spindle performing cutting |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU examiners frequently test the difference between **Geometric Offset** (measured once on presetter) vs **Wear Offset** (updated during machining). Memorize this distinction — it appears almost every semester.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Tool Preset Workflow

The complete tool preset cycle in an FMS consists of **seven sequential logical steps**:

1. **Tool Selection from Tool Crib**
   - Tool required for a part program is withdrawn from the central tool crib.
   - Tool identity verified using barcode / RFID scan.

2. **Cleaning and Visual Inspection**
   - Tool flutes cleaned of chips / coolant residue.
   - Inspection for wear, chipping, or breakage.

3. **Mounting on Tool Presetter**
   - Tool inserted into the presetter spindle (similar taper — CAT40, BT40, HSK63, etc.).
   - Tool clamped using same force as CNC spindle.

4. **Dimensional Measurement**
   - **Length (Z-axis)** measured from spindle face reference (or gauge line) to tool tip using a non-contact laser or touch-trigger probe.
   - **Diameter (X/Y)** measured at the cutting edge using a CCD camera or laser micrometer.
   - **Run-out** measured using dial / electronic indicators.

5. **Offset Calculation and Storage**
   - Measured values stored in the controller's **offset register** (e.g., H01, D01).
   - For sister tools, identical offsets assigned to different T-codes.

6. **Encoding / Identification Marking**
   - Tool labelled with **barcode sticker** or **RFID chip** containing T-code, offset values, tool life remaining.

7. **Loading into FMS Tool Magazine**
   - Tool transported via **AGV / conveyor** to the CNC station's tool magazine.
   - Loaded into designated pocket using **Automatic Tool Changer (ATC)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The Tool Presetter must use the **same taper and clamping mechanism** as the host CNC machine. Otherwise, **measurement errors of 0.01–0.05 mm** occur due to taper seating variations — a classic KTU viva question.

### 2.2 Mathematical Offset Computation Logic

When a tool is mounted on the presetter, two reference systems exist:

- **Machine Reference Point (M)** — fixed point on CNC spindle nose.
- **Tool Tip Point (T)** — extreme point of cutting edge.
- **Tool Length ($L_t$)** — distance from M to T.

The **geometric length offset** stored in the controller is:

$$L_{offset} = L_{t} - L_{reference}$$

where $L_{reference}$ is the distance from machine zero to spindle reference face (a fixed machine constant).

For **diameter offset**:

$$D_{offset} = D_{measured}$$

where $D_{measured}$ is the actual diameter read by the presetter's optical system.

### 2.3 Taylor's Tool Life Equation (Used During Preset Decisions)

During preset, the tool must be assigned a **remaining tool life budget**. The Taylor equation governs this:

$$V \cdot T^{n} = C$$

Solving for tool life $T$:

$$T = \left(\frac{C}{V}\right)^{1/n}$$

where:

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $V$ | Cutting speed | m/min |
| $T$ | Tool life | minutes |
| $n$ | Taylor's exponent | dimensionless (0.1–0.4) |
| $C$ | Taylor's constant | (m/min)·min$^n$ |

**Worked Insight:** If Taylor's $n = 0.25$ and cutting speed $V$ doubles, tool life becomes $(1/2)^{1/0.25} = 1/16$ — only **6.25%** of original. This is why **tool life predictions drive the preset schedule** in FMS.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Tool Preset Module

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_{offset} = L_{t} - L_{ref}$ | Tool length compensation | $L_t$, $L_{ref}$ | mm |
| 2 | $D_{offset} = D_{measured}$ | Tool diameter compensation | $D$ | mm |
| 3 | $V \cdot T^{n} = C$ | Taylor's tool life | $V$, $T$, $n$, $C$ | m/min, min, —, — |
| 4 | $T = (C/V)^{1/n}$ | Tool life from cutting speed | as above | minutes |
| 5 | $T_{machining} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot L}{f \cdot 1000}$ | Machining time per pass | $D$, $L$, $f$ | mm, mm, mm/rev |
| 6 | $T_{total} = \sum T_i$ | Total tool time budget | per tool | min |
| 7 | $N_{sister} = \lceil T_{total} / T_{life} \rceil$ | Number of sister tools needed | ratio | integer |
| 8 | $P_{unattended} = 1 - \dfrac{t_{setup}}{t_{setup} + t_{cut}}$ | Unattended production fraction | times | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\sigma_{runout} \le 0.01$ mm | Acceptable tool run-out | $\sigma$ | mm |
| 10 | $Z_{cumulative} = \sum (L_t - L_{ref})$ | Cumulative Z-stack error | sum of offsets | mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Pitfall:** Equation (10) — examiners love asking why cumulative Z-offset error matters. Answer: Multiple tools stacked in a machining sequence; any individual error compounds and destroys the part's dimensional accuracy.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The tool preset system is the **backbone of lights-out manufacturing** in industries producing:
- **Aerospace structural components** (long tool lists, expensive spindles).
- **Automotive engine blocks** (24+ tool changes per cycle).
- **Medical implants** (Ti/Co-Cr alloys, no room for trial cuts).
- **Die and mould making** (deep cavities requiring tool length precision < 0.005 mm).

By pre-setting every tool offline, FMS achieves **up to 40% reduction in non-cutting time** and enables continuous 72-hour unmanned shifts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Tool Offset Coordinate System

Consider a CNC vertical machining centre with three Cartesian axes. Define the following points (shown in figure in SECTION 4):

- **Point O** — Machine Work Origin (program zero).
- **Point M** — Spindle Nose Reference Face.
- **Point T** — Tool Cutting Tip.
- **Point R** — Reference Tool Master (a "zero tool" of known length $L_{ref}$).

**Step 1 — Establish Reference Tool Length**

The CNC controller is first calibrated using the **Reference Tool**. When this tool touches a known height block (say 50.000 mm above table), the controller records:

$$Z_{M} = 50.000 + L_{ref}$$

This establishes the **fixed machine coordinate of point M** above the workpiece origin.

**Step 2 — Insert New Tool**

A new tool with measured length $L_t$ is loaded. Its tool tip coordinate relative to M is:

$$Z_{tool\_tip} = Z_{M} - L_t$$

**Step 3 — Compute Geometric Offset**

Subtracting the reference case:

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{offset} &= L_t - L_{ref} \\
&= (Z_{M} - Z_{tool\_tip}) - (Z_{M} - Z_{ref\_tip}) \\
&= Z_{ref\_tip} - Z_{tool\_tip}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Wear Compensation**

During cutting, flank wear $VB$ progresses. The wear offset is added to geometric offset:

$$L_{total\_offset} = L_{geometric} + L_{wear}$$

where $L_{wear}$ is positive when tool shortens (typical for flank wear).

**Step 5 — Verify Cumulative Stack Error**

For a part program using $k$ tools in sequence:

$$Z_{stack\_error} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \delta L_i$$

where $\delta L_i$ is the measurement uncertainty of tool $i$. If each presetter has $\delta L = \pm 0.005$ mm and $k = 20$ tools:

$$Z_{stack\_error} = \sqrt{20} \cdot 0.005 = 0.022 \text{ mm (RSS combination)}$$

This is the **dimensional tolerance floor** of the FMS — a key KTU design parameter.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Taylor's Tool Life and Sister Tool Sizing

**Problem:** A carbide end mill used in FMS has Taylor parameters $C = 300$ (m/min)·min$^n$ and $n = 0.25$. The part program requires the tool to run at $V = 120$ m/min for a total cutting time of $T_{total} = 240$ minutes per shift. Determine:
(a) Tool life per insert.
(b) Number of sister tools needed per shift.
(c) Machining time if feed $f = 0.10$ mm/rev, depth of cut $L = 80$ mm, diameter $D = 12$ mm.

**Step (a): Tool Life per Insert**

Apply Taylor's equation:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{life} &= \left(\frac{C}{V}\right)^{1/n} \\
&= \left(\frac{300}{120}\right)^{1/0.25} \\
&= (2.5)^{4} \\
&= 39.0625 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step (b): Number of Sister Tools**

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{sister} &= \left\lceil \frac{T_{total}}{T_{life}} \right\rceil \\
&= \left\lceil \frac{240}{39.0625} \right\rceil \\
&= \lceil 6.144 \rceil \\
&= 7 \text{ sister tools}
\end{aligned}$$

So **7 identical inserts** must be pre-set and loaded into the FMS tool magazine at the start of the shift to cover uninterrupted production.

**Step (c): Machining Time per Pass**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{machining} &= \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot L}{f \cdot 1000} \\
&= \frac{\pi \cdot 12 \cdot 80}{0.10 \cdot 1000} \\
&= \frac{3015.93}{100} \\
&= 30.16 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step (d): Unattended Production Fraction**

Non-cutting setup time per tool change $t_{setup} = 12$ s. Total cutting time per shift:

$$t_{cut} = \frac{T_{total}}{60} \cdot 3600 = 14400 \text{ s}$$

Total setup time:

$$t_{setup\_total} = N_{sister} \cdot t_{setup} = 7 \cdot 12 = 84 \text{ s}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{unattended} &= 1 - \frac{t_{setup\_total}}{t_{setup\_total} + t_{cut}} \\
&= 1 - \frac{84}{84 + 14400} \\
&= 1 - 0.00580 \\
&= 0.9942 \text{ or } 99.42\%
\end{aligned}$$

This shows the FMS operates **99.42% of the time** on cutting — only 0.58% on tool changes. Excellent!

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Preset vs In-Machine Measurement

| Performance Metric | Preset (Offline) | In-Machine Measurement |
|---|---|---|
| **Spindle occupation** | **Zero** | 30–120 s per tool |
| **Measurement accuracy** | $\pm 0.001$ mm (laser) | $\pm 0.005$–0.010 mm |
| **Operator skill needed** | Low | High |
| **Risk of spindle contamination** | **None** | Possible |
| **Cost per measurement** | Very low (after initial cost) | High (machine hour cost) |
| **Tool damage risk** | None | Possible crash during probe |
| **Setup for new part** | Immediate (preset tools) | 30+ min delay |
| **FMS suitability** | **Essential** | Incompatible |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** When asked "Why is preset preferred in FMS?", the expected answer combines **zero spindle occupation**, **higher accuracy**, and **enables unmanned operation** — all three points must appear.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Tool Preset Workflow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Tool Crib Central Storage] --> B[Barcode RFID Identification Scan]
    B --> C[Visual Inspection and Cleaning Station]
    C --> D[Mount on Tool Presetter Spindle]
    D --> E[Laser Probe Measures Length Z offset]
    D --> F[CCD Camera Measures Diameter D offset]
    D --> G[Dial Indicator Measures Runout]
    E --> H[Offset Register Calculation Unit]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[CNC Controller Offset Table Update]
    I --> J[Encode Tool with RFID Tag and T code]
    J --> K[AGV or Conveyor Transport]
    K --> L[CNC Machine Tool Magazine Pocket]
    L --> M[Automatic Tool Changer ATC Ready]
    M --> N[Machining Operation Begins]
```

### 4.2 Tool Offset Coordinate System Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O[Work Origin O] -->|Z distance| M[Spindle Nose M]
    M -->|Length Lt| T[Tool Tip T]
    M -.->|Reference Lref| R[Reference Master Tool R]
    R -.->|Difference| OFFSET[Loffset = Lt minus Lref]
    T --> OFFSET
```

### 4.3 FMS Tool Management Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TOP[Central Tool Management System TMS Software]
    TOP --> L1[Tool Crib Physical Storage]
    TOP --> L2[Tool Presetter Station]
    TOP --> L3[Tool Identification Barcode RFID]
    TOP --> L4[Tool Magazine Buffers per CNC]
    TOP --> L5[Tool Life and Wear Database]
    L2 --> L4
    L3 --> L2
    L5 --> L2
```

### 4.4 Tool Presetter Hardware Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Presetter Spindle Drive Motor] --> CLAMP[Tool Clamping Unit]
    CLAMP --> TAPER[CAT BT HSK Taper Holder]
    TAPER --> TOOL[Cutting Tool Inserted]
    TOOL --> ZPROBE[Laser Z Probe]
    TOOL --> XPROBE[Laser X Probe]
    TOOL --> YPROBE[Laser Y Probe]
    TOOL --> RUNOUT[Dial Indicator for Runout]
    ZPROBE --> CPU[Controller PC]
    XPROBE --> CPU
    YPROBE --> CPU
    RUNOUT --> CPU
    CPU --> DISPLAY[LCD Display Readout]
    CPU --> DNC[DNC Link to CNC]
    CPU --> PRINTER[Offset Label Printer]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The presetter replicates the CNC's spindle taper exactly. The three laser probes capture **3D coordinates** of the tool tip without any contact, eliminating mechanical deflection errors. The DNC link uploads offsets in seconds — far faster than manual MDI entry.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define the term **Tool Preset**. Why is it considered essential in a Flexible Manufacturing System?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Tool Preset is the process of measuring and recording the geometric dimensions (length, diameter, run-out) of a cutting tool on a dedicated off-line **Tool Presetter** before it is loaded into the CNC machine's tool magazine.

It is essential in FMS because:
1. It **eliminates spindle setup time**, enabling continuous unmanned production.
2. It provides **higher accuracy** ($\pm 0.001$ mm) using laser/CCD probes compared to in-machine touch probes.
3. It **supports automatic tool identification** through barcode/RFID tags for fast magazine loading.
4. It enables **sister-tool pre-staging**, allowing instant swap when a tool's life expires mid-shift.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write only "measuring tool outside the machine" — but they **must include the offset storage and identification aspects** to score full 3 marks. KTU key insists on the **identification + offset transfer** mention.

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Distinguish between **Geometric Offset** and **Wear Offset** in tool preset.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Geometric Offset | Wear Offset |
|---|---|
| Measured **once** on the tool presetter before use | Updated **progressively** during machining |
| Represents **manufacturing dimensional difference** between sister tools | Represents **gradual wear** (flank + crater) during cutting |
| Stored under **fixed offset register** (e.g., H01) | Stored under **wear offset register** (e.g., H01 + Wear) |
| Value typically large (10–300 mm for length) | Value small (0.001–0.500 mm) |
| Set by **tool presetter** | Set by **operator / adaptive controller** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students confuse the two by saying wear offset is "set once". It must be emphasized as **dynamically updated** based on actual cutting feedback.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[Internal Choice — KTU ESE Module Pattern]**

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **construction and working of an off-line Tool Presetter**. List the parameters measured. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a part manufactured in an FMS cell, the tool magazine contains **20 different cutting tools**. Each tool's length has a measurement uncertainty of $\pm 0.005$ mm from the presetter. Calculate the **maximum cumulative stack error** in the Z-axis when all 20 tools are used in a single setup. If the part drawing tolerance is $\pm 0.025$ mm, comment on the suitability. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution A

**Part (a) — Tool Presetter Construction and Working (7 marks)**

A tool presetter is a precision metrology device that mimics the spindle nose of the CNC machine. Its main components are:

1. **Presetter Spindle Assembly** — uses the **same taper (CAT40/BT40/HSK63)** as the host CNC, driven by a small servo motor for tool rotation if dynamic run-out is to be measured.

2. **Z-axis Laser Probe** — a non-contact laser triangulation sensor mounted on a linear slide, traverses vertically to locate the tool tip's Z-coordinate with sub-micron resolution.

3. **X/Y Optical Measuring System** — a high-resolution CCD camera with telecentric lens captures the **tool diameter** at the cutting edge plane. Cross-hairs or image-processing software computes the diameter to $\pm 0.001$ mm.

4. **Run-out Indicator** — a precision dial gauge or electronic probe measures the **radial deviation** of the cutting edge as the spindle is rotated.

5. **PC-based Controller** — runs the measurement software, computes length/diameter offsets, displays values on LCD, and transmits them to the CNC controller via **DNC / Ethernet link**.

6. **Tool Clamping Unit** — applies identical drawbar force as CNC spindle, ensuring taper seating identical to in-machine conditions.

**Working Principle:**
- Tool loaded manually or by robot.
- Clamped using drawbar mechanism.
- Z-probe moves up to find tool tip → measures $L_t$.
- X/Y camera snaps tool diameter image → computes $D$.
- Spindle rotates → run-out measured.
- Offsets uploaded to CNC's offset table.
- Barcode printed and stuck on tool body.
- Tool released to FMS tool pool.

**Parameters Measured:**
1. Tool length (Z-offset)
2. Tool diameter (X/Y offset)
3. Tool run-out (radial deviation)
4. Cutting edge profile (optional)
5. Insert seating height (if applicable)

**Part (b) — Cumulative Stack Error Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- Number of tools, $k = 20$
- Individual tool length uncertainty, $\delta L = \pm 0.005$ mm
- Part tolerance, $\pm 0.025$ mm

**Step 1 — Random Error Combination (Root Sum Square method):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{stack} &= \delta L \cdot \sqrt{k} \\
&= 0.005 \cdot \sqrt{20} \\
&= 0.005 \cdot 4.4721 \\
&= 0.02236 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Worst-case (Arithmetic) Combination:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta_{worst} &= k \cdot \delta L \\
&= 20 \cdot 0.005 \\
&= 0.100 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with Tolerance:**

- RSS cumulative stack error: $0.02236$ mm < tolerance $\pm 0.025$ mm → **Acceptable under statistical analysis**.
- Worst-case stack error: $0.100$ mm > tolerance $\pm 0.025$ mm → **Not acceptable in absolute terms**.

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**

The presetter with $\pm 0.005$ mm accuracy is **suitable for this FMS application** when assessed statistically (RSS method), since 99.7% of parts will fall within $\pm 3\sigma = \pm 0.067$ mm tolerance window. However, to be safe, KTU designers should either:
- Upgrade to a $\pm 0.002$ mm presetter (reduces $\sigma_{stack}$ to $0.009$ mm), OR
- Reduce tools per setup by splitting into multiple fixtures.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to mention **both RSS AND worst-case** methods. KTU key requires explicit comparison with tolerance and a concluding recommendation. Missing the recommendation loses 2 marks.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the **role of tool identification systems** (Barcode and RFID) in modern FMS tool management. List two advantages of RFID over barcode. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CNC end mill in an FMS has Taylor's tool life equation parameters: $C = 250$ and $n = 0.30$ (with $V$ in m/min and $T$ in minutes). The part program requires the tool to operate at $V = 100$ m/min for a total of **300 minutes per shift**. Determine:
- (i) The tool life per insert.
- (ii) The number of sister tools required.
- (iii) The spindle rotation speed if the tool diameter is 16 mm. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution B

**Part (a) — Tool Identification Systems (7 marks)**

**Role of Barcode:**
Barcode labels contain machine-readable data (T-code, offset values, tool life remaining). When scanned at the tool crib or magazine loading station, the FMS **Tool Management Software (TMS)** instantly:
- Verifies the tool identity.
- Updates tool life counter.
- Prevents loading wrong tools into wrong pockets.
- Logs tool usage history.

**Role of RFID:**
RFID (Radio-Frequency Identification) uses an **embedded chip + antenna** on the tool holder. The chip stores the same data as barcode but enables:
- **Non-line-of-sight reading** (no need to expose label).
- **Bulk reading** of multiple tools simultaneously.
- **Higher data capacity** (life curve, last-cut parameters, etc.).
- **Better durability** in coolant / chip environment.

**Two Advantages of RFID over Barcode:**

| S.No | RFID | Barcode |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Works when **dirty, oily, or covered** by chips | Fails if label is smudged or torn |
| 2 | **Multiple tools readable at once** from a rack | Only **one-at-a-time line-of-sight** scanning |
| 3 | Stores **rich data** (life curve, history, certs) | Limited to ~20 characters |
| 4 | **Tamper-resistant** — chip inside holder | Label can be peeled and swapped |

**Part (b) — Taylor's Tool Life Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $C = 250$ (m/min)·min$^n$
- $n = 0.30$
- $V = 100$ m/min
- $T_{total} = 300$ minutes
- $D = 16$ mm

**Step (i) — Tool Life per Insert:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{life} &= \left(\frac{C}{V}\right)^{1/n} \\
&= \left(\frac{250}{100}\right)^{1/0.30} \\
&= (2.5)^{3.3333}
\end{aligned}$$

Computing $(2.5)^{3.3333}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\ln(2.5) &= 0.9163 \\
0.9163 \cdot 3.3333 &= 3.0543 \\
e^{3.0543} &= 21.21 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}$$

So $T_{life} = 21.21$ minutes per insert.

**Step (ii) — Number of Sister Tools:**

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{sister} &= \left\lceil \frac{T_{total}}{T_{life}} \right\rceil \\
&= \left\lceil \frac{300}{21.21} \right\rceil \\
&= \lceil 14.14 \rceil \\
&= 15 \text{ sister tools}
\end{aligned}$$

So **15 inserts** must be pre-set and loaded.

**Step (iii) — Spindle Rotation Speed:**

$$\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D} \\
&= \frac{1000 \cdot 100}{\pi \cdot 16} \\
&= \frac{100000}{50.265} \\
&= 1989.4 \text{ rpm}
\end{aligned}$$

So spindle must rotate at approximately **1990 rpm**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A common mistake in Taylor equation problems is **using $n$ directly as exponent instead of $1/n$**. KTU key specifically tests the rearrangement $T = (C/V)^{1/n}$. Writing $T = C^{1/n} / V^{1/n}$ is acceptable; writing $T = (C/V)^n$ is a **sure 3-mark loss**.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final-minute revision bullets — read these 2 hours before exam.**

- [x] **Tool Preset** = measuring tool dimensions **offline** on a presetter before loading.
- [x] Essential for FMS because it **eliminates spindle setup time** and enables **lights-out machining**.
- [x] **Two reference types**: Geometric offset (fixed, set once) vs Wear offset (dynamic, updated).
- [x] Taylor's tool life: $V \cdot T^n = C$, rearranged as $T = (C/V)^{1/n}$.
- [x] Number of sister tools: $N = \lceil T_{total} / T_{life} \rceil$.
- [x] Cumulative Z-stack error (RSS): $\sigma_{stack} = \delta L \cdot \sqrt{k}$.
- [x] Spindle speed: $N = 1000V / (\pi D)$ — units must be consistent (mm, m/min).
- [x] **RFID > Barcode** for FMS because of chip durability and bulk reading.
- [x] Presetter must use **same taper and clamping force** as host CNC.
- [x] Three parameters measured: **length, diameter, run-out**.
- [x] **Tool Magazine** holds standby tools; **ATC** performs the swap.
- [x] **DNC link / TMS software** transfers offset data to CNC.
- [x] Common taper standards: **CAT, BT, HSK, Capto**.
- [x] Unattended fraction formula: $P = 1 - t_{setup}/(t_{setup}+t_{cut})$.
- [x] Always state **both RSS and worst-case** when computing stack errors.
- [x] KTU frequently asks "Why preset preferred?" — answer must include **zero spindle occupation + higher accuracy + unmanned operation**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET757 - FLEXIBLE MANUFACTURING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Identification and Data Transfer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: IDENTIFICATION AND DATA TRANSFER

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Identification** in a Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS) refers to the automatic recognition and tracking of physical entities (workpieces, pallets, cutting tools, fixtures, and AGVs) through machine-readable codes or electronic markers, enabling the FMS controller to correlate every physical object with its digital record (part program, routing, tool list, inspection data) in real time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Transfer** in an FMS denotes the bidirectional movement of digital information (NC part programs, tool offset tables, status signals, scheduling commands, quality data) between the central host computer, the cell controllers, the CNC machines, the PLCs, the material-handling system, and the workstations over a structured communication network.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a large airport hub like the one in Kochi or Dubai. Every suitcase has a **barcode tag** glued to it. As the suitcase moves on belts, scanners read the tag, and the airport's central computer instantly knows *who owns it, where it came from, and which flight it must board*. Meanwhile, the pilot and ATC exchange **radio messages** continuously to coordinate landings.

In an FMS, the **factory floor is the airport**, the **workpieces/pallets are the suitcases**, the **barcode/RFID readers are the scanners**, and the **communication network is the radio system**. Without identification, the system cannot *recognise* a part; without data transfer, it cannot *instruct* a machine. Together they form the nervous system of the FMS.

### 1.3 Why Both Are Mandatory in FMS

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Why Identification & Data Transfer?**
> An FMS without identification is "blind" — it cannot route a specific pallet of a specific raw casting to the right CNC machine at the right time. An FMS without data transfer is "mute" — even if it knows the routing, it cannot download the part program to the CNC. Both functions together constitute the **information backbone** of the FMS.

- **Identification** answers the question: *"Which physical object is this, and what does the database say about it?"*
- **Data Transfer** answers the question: *"How does this information flow from the central brain to the limbs (machines) and back?"*

### 1.4 Classification Snapshot

| Dimension | Identification Methods | Data Transfer Methods |
|---|---|---|
| **Level** | Part, Tool, Pallet, Fixture, Operator | DNC, LAN, Fieldbus, MAP |
| **Technology** | Barcode, RFID, Magnetic Stripe, OCR | RS-232, RS-422, Ethernet, MAP |
| **Mode** | Contact / Non-contact | Serial / Parallel, Wired / Wireless |
| **Range** | mm to metres | metres to kilometres |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Identification in FMS — Hierarchical Levels

The FMS identification problem is tackled at **four distinct levels**, each requiring a different technology:

### Level 1 — Workpiece / Part Identification
- Identification of the raw blank or semi-finished component.
- Carried on the **pallet** which is the carrier that visits every station.
- Technology: 1-D or 2-D barcode on the pallet; RFID tag embedded in the pallet.

### Level 2 — Pallet Identification
- Every pallet has a unique **ID number** (often 6 to 10 digits).
- The pallet ID is the *primary key* used by the host to retrieve the part program, routing, and inspection sheet.
- Most critical identification in an FMS.

### Level 3 — Tool Identification
- Tools in a carousel or chain magazine must be tracked for **tool life, usage count, breakage, and offset compensation**.
- Each tool holder is fitted with a code carrier read at a tool-identification station.

### Level 4 — Fixture / Workholding Identification
- The fixture that locates the part on the pallet must also be identified so the controller knows the datum reference frame.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Standard:** The most common pallet ID format is the **Interleaved 2-of-5 (ITF)** or **Code 39** barcode, holding a 6- to 8-digit decimal ID.

## 2.2 Barcode Technology — Detailed Architecture

### 2.2.1 Anatomy of a Barcode Symbol

A barcode symbol (following the EAN/UPC or Code 39 standard) is composed of the following regions, scanned from left to right:

1. **Quiet Zone** — A clear blank margin (typically **10× the narrowest bar width**) at each end. Required by scanners to calibrate.
2. **Start Character** — A special pattern (e.g., `*` for Code 39) that tells the scanner the beginning of the code.
3. **Data Characters** — The encoded alphanumeric value (pallet ID, tool ID, etc.).
4. **Check Digit** — A mathematical verification character computed using a weighted-sum algorithm.
5. **Stop Character** — A special pattern indicating end of code.

### 2.2.2 Symbology Families (KTU High-Yield)

| Symbology | Type | Density | Data Capacity | Common Use in FMS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **UPC-A** | 1-D Numeric | 12 digits | Fixed 12 | Retail / shipping carton |
| **EAN-13** | 1-D Numeric | 13 digits | Fixed 13 | European retail |
| **Code 39** | 1-D Alphanumeric | Variable | 43 characters | Industrial pallets, tools |
| **Code 128** | 1-D High-density | Variable | 128 ASCII | Compact pallet tags |
| **Interleaved 2-of-5** | 1-D Numeric | Variable | 30+ digits | Pallet ID, warehouse |
| **PDF417** | 2-D Stack | Variable | 1.1 KB | Tool data sheets |
| **Data Matrix** | 2-D Matrix | Variable | 2 KB | Small tool markings |
| **QR Code** | 2-D Matrix | Variable | 3 KB | Maintenance data, manuals |

### 2.2.3 Barcode Reading Devices

| Scanner Type | Operating Principle | Range | Industrial Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Wand / Pen** | Manual contact, photodiode reads reflected light | Contact | Low-volume stations |
| **Slot Scanner** | Part pulled through a slot | Fixed slot | Conveyor lines |
| **CCD (Charge-Coupled Device)** | Linear array of LEDs | 0 to 50 mm | Pallet stations |
| **Laser Scanner** | Oscillating mirror sweeps a laser beam | up to 1 m | Pallet read-out stations, AGV |
| **Image-based 2-D** | CMOS camera + decoder | 50 to 300 mm | Data Matrix on tools |

### 2.2.4 Check Digit Calculation (Code 39 Modulo-43)

While Code 39 itself uses an optional Mod-43 check character, the more common industrial variant is **UPC-A/EAN-13 with Mod-10 check digit**, computed as follows:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mod-10 Check Digit Algorithm (Luhn Algorithm)**
> 1. Number the digits from the **right** (excluding the check digit itself).
> 2. Multiply every **second** digit (i.e., digits in odd positions from the right) by **3**.
> 3. Sum all the products and the un-multiplied digits.
> 4. The check digit is the number that makes the total sum a **multiple of 10** (i.e., sum mod 10 = 0).

## 2.3 Radio Frequency Identification (RFID)

### 2.3.1 Principle of Operation

An RFID system uses **electromagnetic coupling** between a *reader antenna* and a *transponder (tag)*. The reader broadcasts an RF signal; the tag either reflects it back (passive tag) or transmits its own signal (active tag), modulated with its stored ID.

### 2.3.2 Components of an RFID System

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Tag / Transponder** | Stores the ID; contains an antenna and microchip |
| **Reader / Interrogator** | Generates RF field, decodes tag response |
| **Antenna** | Coupled coil (LF/HF) or dipole (UHF) |
| **Host Software** | Maps ID to database record |

### 2.3.3 Frequency Bands (KTU High-Yield Table)

| Band | Frequency | Typical Range | Coupling | FMS Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **LF** | 125 – 134 kHz | 0 to 10 cm | Inductive | Tool IDs |
| **HF** | **13.56 MHz** | 0 to 1 m | Inductive | Pallet, fixture ID |
| **UHF** | 860 – 960 MHz | 1 to 10 m | Backscatter | AGV, tote tracking |
| **Microwave** | 2.45 GHz / 5.8 GHz | 1 to 30 m (active) | Backscatter | Yard logistics |

### 2.3.4 Passive vs Active Tags

| Feature | Passive Tag | Active Tag |
|---|---|---|
| **Power Source** | Induced from reader RF field | Internal battery |
| **Range** | Short (mm to metres) | Long (up to 100 m) |
| **Cost** | **USD 0.10 – 1.00** | **USD 10 – 50** |
| **Lifespan** | Indefinite | 5 to 10 years (battery) |
| **Read Rate** | Lower | Higher, faster |
| **Memory** | 64 bits – 8 KB | Up to 1 MB |

> [!NOTE]
> **FMS Preference:** Most modern FMS pallet identification uses **passive HF (13.56 MHz) ISO 15693** tags because they are cheap, robust, and survive oil/coolant. AGVs typically carry **active UHF tags** for long-range identification.

### 2.3.5 RFID vs Barcode — Engineering Comparison

| Parameter | Barcode | RFID |
|---|---|---|
| **Line of Sight** | Required | **Not required** |
| **Read Through Oil/Mud** | No | Yes (HF/UHF) |
| **Read Multiple Items** | One at a time | **Bulk reading possible** |
| **Read/Write** | Read-only | Read/Write (memory) |
| **Data Capacity** | Low (≤ 30 chars 1-D) | High (KB range) |
| **Cost per Tag** | ₹ 1 – 5 | ₹ 10 – 100 |
| **Durability** | Prints fade | Encapsulated, high durability |
| **Security** | Easy to copy | Encrypted, harder to spoof |

## 2.4 Other Identification Methods

### 2.4.1 Magnetic Stripe
- A strip of **magnetic tape** laminated on the back of a card or tool holder.
- Encodes up to **79 alphanumeric characters** in three tracks.
- Read by *swiping* or *insertion* into a magnetic-head reader.
- Used for **operator ID cards**, **tool holder ID** in older CNC cells.
- Limitation: Easily damaged by oil, coolant, magnetic fields.

### 2.4.2 Optical Character Recognition (OCR) and Vision Systems
- A **CMOS/CCD camera** captures an image of an alphanumeric or 2-D data-matrix code.
- Image processing extracts the data.
- Used where the marking cannot be a barcode (e.g., directly engraved on a tool shank).
- Costlier than barcode but extremely flexible.

### 2.4.3 Smart Cards (Contact & Contactless)
- Contact: ISO 7816 chip on the card (e.g., SIM card).
- Contactless: ISO 14443 (NFC) short-range RFID.
- Used for **operator authentication** and **production-data logging** in modern FMS.

## 2.5 Data Transfer in FMS — Architecture

### 2.5.1 Modes of Data Transfer

1. **Manual Entry:** Operator types program on CNC keyboard — slowest, error-prone.
2. **Offline Transfer (Traditional NC):** Programs written on a separate PC and transferred via **floppy disk, USB, or RS-232 cable** — machine must be idle for download.
3. **On-line Transfer (DNC):** Programs reside on a **central host** and are *streamed on demand* to any CNC — eliminates idle time.
4. **Distributed Transfer:** Programs distributed across cell controllers, with shared database.

### 2.5.2 Direct Numerical Control (DNC)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus):** DNC is a manufacturing system in which a **central host computer** directly controls and downloads Numerical Control (NC) part programs to a network of **CNC machines** in real time, eliminating the use of paper tape, magnetic tape, or manual input.

**Key Functionalities of a DNC System:**

- Centralised **NC program storage** in the host's database.
- **Bi-directional** data flow: download (host → CNC) and upload (CNC → host) for feedback (tool life, alarms, cycle time).
- **Simultaneous** communication with multiple CNCs.
- **Editing, verification, and post-processing** of programs at the host.

### 2.5.3 Communication Interfaces (KTU High-Yield)

| Interface | Standard | Speed | Distance | Use Today |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **RS-232-C** | Serial | up to **20 kbps** | 15 m | Legacy DNC, single CNC |
| **RS-422 / RS-423** | Balanced serial | up to 10 Mbps | 1200 m | Industrial DNC |
| **RS-485** | Multi-drop serial | up to 10 Mbps | 1200 m | Fieldbus (Modbus) |
| **IEEE 802.3 Ethernet** | LAN | 10 / 100 / 1000 Mbps | 100 m | Modern FMS backbone |
| **IEEE 802.11 Wi-Fi** | WLAN | up to 600 Mbps | 50 m | AGV, mobile terminals |
| **IEEE 802.15.4 / ZigBee** | WPAN | 250 kbps | 10 m | Sensor networks |
| **USB 2.0/3.0** | Point-to-point | 480 Mbps – 5 Gbps | 5 m | Local file transfer |
| **Fieldbus (Profibus, DeviceNet)** | Industrial | 12 Mbps | up to 12 km | Sensor/actuator level |

### 2.5.4 Network Topologies

| Topology | Structure | Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Star** | All nodes connect to a central hub | Easy to add/remove nodes; single node failure does not affect others | Hub failure brings down network |
| **Ring** | Each node connected to two neighbours | Deterministic, equal access | Single node/break fails network; difficult to add nodes |
| **Bus** | Single backbone cable (coax) shared | Simple, low cabling cost | Collisions; cable break fails all |
| **Tree** | Hierarchical star of stars | Scalable, structured | Dependent on root switches |
| **Mesh** | Every node connected to every other | Highly redundant, robust | Expensive, complex wiring |

> [!NOTE]
> **FMS Convention:** The most common topology used in FMS today is the **Tree topology with Ethernet switches**, because the FMS itself is hierarchically organised (Host → Cell Controllers → CNCs).

## 2.6 Manufacturing Automation Protocol (MAP)

### 2.6.1 What is MAP?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MAP (Manufacturing Automation Protocol):** A **vendor-neutral, open communication standard** developed by General Motors (1980s) and later standardised by **ISO and IEEE**, built on the **OSI 7-layer model**, designed specifically for the **factory floor** so that CNCs, robots, PLCs, and computers from different vendors can interoperate.

MAP version **3.0 (MAP 3.0)** uses:
- **IEEE 802.4 Token Bus** at Layers 1 & 2 (originally) → later migrated to Ethernet.
- **OSI Full Stack (all 7 layers)**.
- **MMS (Manufacturing Message Specification)** at Layer 7.

### 2.6.2 Companion Protocols

- **TOP (Technical and Office Protocol):** MAP's office counterpart, based on the same OSI stack.
- **MINI-MAP:** A reduced 3-layer version of MAP for **low-cost, real-time** devices like sensors. Uses only Physical, Data Link, and Application layers.

### 2.6.3 Why MAP Was Needed (Pre-MAP Problem)

Before MAP, every CNC vendor (FANUC, Siemens, Heidenhain) had its **own proprietary protocol**. Connecting a Siemens CNC to a FANUC robot required an expensive *gateway* (protocol converter). MAP eliminated this "Tower of Babel" by imposing a single, open protocol.

### 2.6.4 Layer-wise Function (KTU High-Yield Summary)

| OSI Layer | Function | MAP Specification |
|---|---|---|
| 7 — Application | User services, MMS | **MMS (ISO 9506)** |
| 6 — Presentation | Data formatting | ASN.1 (Abstract Syntax Notation) |
| 5 — Session | Connection management | ISO Session (8327) |
| 4 — Transport | Reliable end-to-end | ISO TP (8073) |
| 3 — Network | Routing | ISO IP (8473) / CLNP |
| 2 — Data Link | Framing, MAC | **IEEE 802.4 Token Bus** (MAP 3.0) / IEEE 802.3 Ethernet (later) |
| 1 — Physical | Electrical/optical | **10BASE5 (Broadband)** / 10BASE2 |

---
