The CAM optimizer selects parameters within a constrained search space using **extended Taylor's tool-life equation** as the binding constraint. **Extended Taylor tool-life equation:** $$ V \, T^n \, f^a \, a_p^b = C $$ Where: - $V$ = cutting speed (m/min), - $T$ = tool life (min), - $f$ = feed per revolution (mm/rev), - $a_p$ = depth of cut (mm), - $n, a, b$ = exponents that depend on tool and work-material combination, - $C$ = constant that depends on tool-workpiece pair. The original Taylor equation $V T^n = C$ (1907) was extended by researchers to include the strong influence of feed and depth of cut on tool life. The CAM module solves the equation for $T$ once $V$, $f$, and $a_p$ are tentatively selected, then iteratively adjusts $V$ until $T$ exceeds the FMS minimum acceptable tool life (typically 15–30 min for unattended shifts). #### Model Solution — Part (b) **(i) Spindle speed:** $$ N = \frac{1000 \, V}{\pi \, D} = \frac{1000 \times 600}{\pi \times 100} $$ $$ N = \frac{600000}{314.159} \approx 1909.86 \text{ rpm} $$ Rounded by CAM: `S1910`. **(ii) Table feedrate:** $$ f_r = f_z \times z \times N = 0.15 \times 6 \times 1909.86 $$ $$ f_r \approx 1718.87 \text{ mm/min} $$ **(iii) Material removal rate:** $$ Q = a_p \times f_z \times z \times V \quad \text{(treat }a_e\text{ as }a_p\text{ here)} $$ Wait — for milling, the chip cross-section is $a_e \times f_z$ per tooth per revolution, but MRR over all teeth is: $$ Q = a_e \times f_r = 80 \times 1718.87 $$ $$ Q \approx 137510 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min} \approx 137.51 \text{ cm}^3\text{/min} $$ **Note for students:** Some textbooks use $Q = a_p \cdot f \cdot V$ where $V$ is in mm/min. Both forms give the same answer once units are kept consistent. The $Q = a_e \cdot f_r$ form is preferred for milling. **(iv) Total machining time:** $$ T_m = \frac{L}{f_r} = \frac{400}{1718.87} \approx 0.2327 \text{ minutes} $$ Convert to seconds: $$ T_m \approx 0.2327 \times 60 \approx 13.96 \text{ seconds} $$ > **Interpretation:** the CAM planner can finish this face-milling pass in under 14 seconds — confirming that the chosen parameters are highly productive on aluminium, where high cutting speeds are permissible. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit mismatch:** Mixing mm and m. Always convert to a single system before plugging into formulas. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for sloppy unit handling. > 2. **Forgetting approach/over-travel:** Many students use $L$ instead of $L_a + L + L_o$ in machining time — losing 1 mark. > 3. **Confusing $f$ (feed/rev) with $f_r$ (feedrate/min):** Always declare what each symbol means before substituting. > 4. **Not rounding spindle RPM:** Real CAM systems round to an integer or to the nearest available gear step. State your rounding logic. > 5. **Missing the closed-loop feedback:** When asked to "explain CAM in FMS", explicitly mention that the CAM module receives feedback from the cell controller — this is the FMS-specific part. Generic CAM answers lose 2–3 marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — CAM Considerations for FMS Planning** - **CAM in FMS = NC programming + parameter optimization + post-processing + verification + DNC distribution + cycle-time estimation + tool management.** - The **CAM module sits between CAPP (upstream) and the FMS cell controller (downstream).** - **Five core formulas to memorize:** (1) $V = \pi D N/1000$, (2) $f_r = f N$, (3) $T_m = L/(f N)$, (4) $Q = a_p f V$, (5) $V T^n f^a a_p^b = C$. - **Three CAM constraints in order:** power → force → tool life. - **Post-processors** are machine-specific; one FMS = many post-processors if mixed controllers are used. - **NC verification** is mandatory before any program enters the DNC library — it is the CAM quality gate. - **DNC (with BTR)** is essential for large NC files that exceed CNC memory. - **Tool-life constraint:** $T \ge 15$–$30$ min is the practical FMS minimum for unattended shifts. - **Cycle time $T_c = T_m + T_{tc} + T_h$** is what the FMS scheduler uses for loading. - **MRR $Q = a_p \times f \times V$** is the basis of cycle-time minimization. - **Closed-loop feedback:** real cycle time and tool wear flow from the shop floor back to the CAM optimizer — this is what distinguishes FMS-CAM from stand-alone CAM. - **GT coding and CAPP integration** are upstream enablers; without them, CAM cannot generate consistent process plans for a part family. - **Machine utilization (MU)** and **parts-per-shift (PPS)** are the two KPIs the CAM module's outputs ultimately drive.

topics.title: FMS Software Structure - General Structure and Requirements
Types of FMS Software Modules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET757 - FLEXIBLE MANUFACTURING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of FMS Software Modules

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> *FMS Software Modules* refer to the **integrated set of computer programs and information systems** that collectively plan, coordinate, monitor, control, and optimize all functional activities of a Flexible Manufacturing System. These modules act as the *digital nervous system* of the FMS, seamlessly linking design, process planning, production scheduling, shop-floor execution, material handling, tool management, and quality assurance into a unified real-time information architecture.

In the context of KTU 2024 scheme, FMS software is classified into **three hierarchical layers**:

1. **Management-Level Software** (Off-line planning): CAD, CAPP, CAPM (Computer-Aided Production Management), MRP-II / ERP.
2. **Shop-Floor Level Software** (Real-time control): SFCS, DNC, Tool Management, Material Handling Control.
3. **Equipment-Level Software** (Direct machine control): CNC part programs, PLC ladder logic, Robot control software.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Smart Hospital"**
> Imagine a large multi-specialty hospital. The doctors (machines) need patients (parts), nurses (robots/AGVs), surgical instruments (tools), operation theatre schedules (production schedules), and patient history files (database). Now imagine an **administrative control room** with multiple computer screens where one screen handles appointments (scheduling), another handles patient records (database), another handles the surgery schedule (shop-floor control), and another handles inventory of medicines and tools (tool/inventory management). This control room is exactly what the **FMS software modules** are — a distributed digital control room that makes the entire factory "think" and "react" automatically.

| Brain Analogy | FMS Module |
|---|---|
| Memory & Records | DBMS (Database Management System) |
| Eyes & Ears | Sensors + Shop Floor Data Collection |
| Decision Maker | Production Planning & Scheduling |
| Hands & Legs | DNC, CNC, Robot Control |
| Quality Inspector | CAQC (Computer-Aided Quality Control) |

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in FMS Software

Key performance metrics monitored by FMS software modules:

- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** — measured in hours
- **Machine Utilization** — expressed as a **percentage (%)**
- **Setup Time Reduction** — typically **30%–70%** after FMS integration
- **WIP (Work-In-Progress) Inventory** — measured in units or value
- **Throughput Time** — measured in **minutes/part**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:**
> As per KTU Module 4, students must be able to identify, classify, and explain the **functional role** of each software module and describe how they interface with one another through a **central database**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Breakdown of FMS Software Modules

The FMS software architecture is decomposed into a layered pyramid. Each layer feeds information to the layer above it.

#### Layer 1 — Management Level (Strategic Planning)

**(a) CAD (Computer-Aided Design)**
- Used for **product design, geometric modeling, and drafting**.
- Generates the master model from which all downstream activities originate.
- Output files (IGES, STEP, DXF) are passed to CAPP and CAM.

**(b) CAPP (Computer-Aided Process Planning)**
- Converts design data into **manufacturing instructions**.
- Two variants:
  - *Variant CAPP* — uses Group Technology (GT) coding to retrieve standard plans.
  - *Generative CAPP* — synthesizes a new plan from logical rules and machining databases.

**(c) CAPM / Production Planning & Scheduling**
- Determines **what to make, when, on which machine, in what sequence**.
- Uses algorithms: priority rules (EDD, SPT, CR), heuristics, and linear programming.
- Generates the **master production schedule (MPS)**.

**(d) MRP-II / ERP**
- MRP-II: Material Requirements Planning Level II — balances **demand, supply, inventory, capacity**.
- ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning): extends to finance, HR, procurement, sales.

---

#### Layer 2 — Shop-Floor Level (Real-Time Tactical Control)

**(e) SFCS (Shop Floor Control System)**
- The **real-time heart** of the FMS software.
- Tracks each part's status (queued / in-transit / being machined / completed).
- Issues dispatch lists to machines and routes material via AGVs.

**(f) DNC (Direct Numerical Control)**
- Distributes **NC part programs** from a central server to multiple CNC machines.
- Eliminates paper tape; enables bidirectional communication and tool-offset downloads.

**(g) Tool Management System (TMS)**
- Tracks **tool life, tool usage, replacement schedule, inventory**.
- Issues tool-change requests to the appropriate machine just-in-time.

**(h) Material Handling Control System (MHCS)**
- Controls **AGVs, conveyors, robotic loaders, AS/RS** (Automated Storage & Retrieval Systems).
- Uses routing algorithms (shortest path, nearest neighbor, traffic-aware).

**(i) CAQC (Computer-Aided Quality Control)**
- Integrates **CMM (Coordinate Measuring Machines)**, SPC (Statistical Process Control), and inspection routines.
- Feeds quality data back to CAPP for corrective actions.

---

#### Layer 3 — Equipment Level (Direct Control)

**(j) Machine-Level Controllers**
- CNC controllers, PLC ladder logic, Robot controllers.
- Execute instructions issued by the upper layers.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Module | Layer | Primary Function | Output / Interface |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **CAD** | Management | Product design & modeling | IGES, STEP files to CAPP |
| 2 | **CAPP** | Management | Process planning | Operation sheets, tool lists |
| 3 | **CAPM / Scheduling** | Management | Production planning | Master schedule, dispatch lists |
| 4 | **MRP-II / ERP** | Management | Resource planning | Purchase orders, BOM |
| 5 | **SFCS** | Shop-Floor | Real-time tracking | WIP status, control commands |
| 6 | **DNC** | Shop-Floor | NC program distribution | NC code to CNC machines |
| 7 | **TMS** | Shop-Floor | Tool life & inventory | Tool-change commands |
| 8 | **MHCS** | Shop-Floor | Material routing | AGV path, conveyor commands |
| 9 | **CAQC** | Shop-Floor | Quality assurance | SPC charts, inspection reports |
| 10 | **DBMS** | Cross-Layer | Common data backbone | Shared database queries |
| 11 | **CNC / PLC / Robot** | Equipment | Direct machine actuation | Axis motion, I/O signals |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> In modern Industry 4.0 factories, FMS software modules are integrated with **IoT sensors, MES (Manufacturing Execution Systems), and cloud analytics**. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to understand both legacy FMS software architecture and its modern cloud-enabled extensions.

---

### 2.3 Information Flow Logic — The "Why" and "How"

- **Why does CAD feed into CAPP?**
  Because the geometric model must be analyzed for machinability before process steps are chosen.
- **How does the schedule reach the machine?**
  SFCS generates a **dispatch list** → DNC transmits the **NC program** → CNC executes → sensor feedback loops back to SFCS.
- **Why a centralized DBMS?**
  To eliminate data redundancy and ensure every module works on the *single source of truth*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Functional Derivations

Since FMS Software is a *systems integration* topic rather than a pure derivation topic, the analytical rigour here is demonstrated through **structured logical flow analysis**, **information transformation steps**, and **functional decomposition matrices**. Each step is exhaustively laid out below.

---

### 3.1 Exhaustive Logical Flow — From Design to Dispatch

**Step 1: Product Conceptualization**
$$\text{Marketing Demand} \rightarrow \text{Engineering Design (CAD)}$$
The product requirement is converted into a 3-D solid model using parametric/solid modeling techniques in CAD software.

**Step 2: Group Technology Coding**
$$\text{Part Design} \xrightarrow{\text{GT Code}} \text{Part Family Identification}$$
Each part is assigned a code (e.g., Opitz, DCLASS) to classify it into a manufacturing family. This enables retrieval of similar past process plans.

**Step 3: Process Plan Generation (CAPP)**
$$\text{GT Code} \rightarrow \text{Retrieve / Generate Process Plan} \rightarrow \text{Output: Route Sheet}$$
The route sheet contains: sequence of operations, machine selection, tooling required, cutting parameters, and inspection points.

**Step 4: Production Planning (CAPM)**
$$\text{Orders} + \text{Route Sheets} + \text{Capacity} \rightarrow \text{Master Production Schedule}$$
The scheduler resolves conflicts between **due dates**, **machine availability**, and **tool availability** using priority rules.

**Step 5: Material Requirement Explosion (MRP-II)**
$$\text{MPS} + \text{BOM (Bill of Materials)} \rightarrow \text{Planned Order Releases}$$
BOM explosion determines raw material, sub-assemblies, and bought-out items needed.

**Step 6: Shop Floor Release**
$$\text{Dispatch List} \xrightarrow{\text{SFCS}} \text{Workstation} + \text{NC Program via DNC}$$
SFCS coordinates timing so that the part, the program, and the tool arrive at the workstation simultaneously.

**Step 7: Material Handling Initiation**
$$\text{Pick Request} \xrightarrow{\text{MHCS}} \text{AGV/Conveyor Activation}$$
AGV picks the raw part from AS/RS and delivers to the assigned machine.

**Step 8: Machining Execution**
$$\text{NC Program} \rightarrow \text{CNC Controller} \rightarrow \text{Cutting Action}$$
Real-time sensor data feeds back to SFCS for status monitoring.

**Step 9: Tool Change Logic**
$$\text{Cumulative Tool Time} \geq \text{Tool Life Limit} \rightarrow \text{TMS Issues Tool Change Request}$$
This is computed in real-time and can interrupt the cycle if needed.

**Step 10: Quality Verification**
$$\text{Finished Part} \xrightarrow{\text{CMM}} \text{Dimensional Data} \rightarrow \text{SPC Analysis} \rightarrow \text{Accept / Reject Decision}$$

**Step 11: Database Update**
$$\text{All Events} \rightarrow \text{Central DBMS} \rightarrow \text{Historical Records for Future Use}$$

---

### 3.2 Functional Decomposition Matrix (Analytical View)

| Process Step | Input Data | Module Responsible | Output Data | Trigger Event |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Market demand | ERP / Marketing | Sales order | Customer PO received |
| 2 | Sales order | CAD | 3-D model | Design freeze |
| 3 | 3-D model | CAPP | Route sheet | Process plan approval |
| 4 | Route sheet | CAPM | MPS | Capacity check passed |
| 5 | MPS, BOM | MRP-II | Purchase orders | Inventory below ROL |
| 6 | Dispatch list | SFCS + DNC | NC program at machine | Machine idle |
| 7 | Pick request | MHCS | AGV movement | Part needed at station |
| 8 | NC program | CNC | Machined part | Cycle start command |
| 9 | Tool time data | TMS | Tool change order | Tool life threshold hit |
| 10 | Inspection data | CAQC | SPC report | Inspection complete |
| 11 | All events | DBMS | Updated records | Continuous logging |

---

### 3.3 Mathematical Representation of Tool Life (Key KTU Formula)

Tool life in FMS is governed by the **Taylor's Tool Life Equation**, which is central to the TMS module:

$$V \cdot T^n = C$$

Where:

- $V$ = cutting speed (m/min)
- $T$ = tool life (minutes)
- $n$ = tool-life exponent (typically **0.2 to 0.4** for HSS, **0.4 to 0.6** for carbide)
- $C$ = constant depending on tool-workpiece combination

**Transformation to FMS-Replacement Logic:**

$$T_{\text{actual}} = \int_{0}^{t} dt \quad \text{(cumulative cutting time)}$$

$$T_{\text{actual}} \geq T_{\text{allowable}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Tool Change Triggered}$$

The allowable tool life is derived by solving Taylor's equation for the chosen cutting parameters:

$$T = \left( \frac{C}{V} \right)^{\frac{1}{n}}$$

This simple inequality forms the **decision kernel** of the Tool Management Software module.

---

### 3.4 Scheduling Priority Rule — EDD (Earliest Due Date)

Used inside CAPM module to order jobs at each machine queue:

$$\text{Priority}(J_i) = \text{min} \{ d_i \}$$

Where $d_i$ is the due date of job $i$. The job with the smallest due date is processed first. The **average tardiness** is monitored as:

$$\bar{T} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \max(0,\ C_i - d_i)$$

Where $C_i$ is the completion time of job $i$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 FMS Software Architecture — Hierarchical Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L1["LAYER 1 MANAGEMENT LEVEL"]
        ERP1["ERP / MRP II Module"]
        CAD1["CAD Module"]
        CAPP1["CAPP Module"]
        CAPM1["CAPM Scheduling Module"]
    end

    subgraph L2["LAYER 2 SHOP FLOOR LEVEL"]
        SFCS1["SFCS Shop Floor Control"]
        DNC1["DNC Direct Numerical Control"]
        TMS1["TMS Tool Management System"]
        MHCS1["MHCS Material Handling Control"]
        CAQC1["CAQC Computer Aided Quality Control"]
    end

    subgraph L3["LAYER 3 EQUIPMENT LEVEL"]
        CNC1["CNC Machine Controller"]
        PLC1["PLC and Robot Controller"]
        AGV1["AGV and Conveyor Hardware"]
    end

    DB1["CENTRAL DATABASE DBMS"]

    ERP1 --> DB1
    CAD1 --> CAPP1
    CAPP1 --> DB1
    CAPM1 --> DB1
    DB1 --> SFCS1
    SFCS1 --> DNC1
    SFCS1 --> TMS1
    SFCS1 --> MHCS1
    DNC1 --> CNC1
    TMS1 --> CNC1
    MHCS1 --> AGV1
    CAQC1 --> DB1
    CNC1 --> SFCS1
    AGV1 --> SFCS1
    PLC1 --> SFCS1
```

---

### 4.2 Information Flow — Design-to-Dispatch Sequence (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Customer Order"] --> B["ERP MRP II"]
    B --> C["CAD Design Module"]
    C --> D["CAPP Process Plan"]
    D --> E["CAPM Scheduler"]
    E --> F["SFCS Dispatch List"]
    F --> G["DNC NC Program"]
    G --> H["CNC Machining"]
    F --> I["MHCS AGV Routing"]
    I --> J["AS RS Storage"]
    H --> K["TMS Tool Change"]
    H --> L["CAQC Inspection"]
    L --> M["Accept or Reject"]
    M --> N["Central DBMS Update"]
    N --> E
```

---

### 4.3 Functional Responsibility Matrix (Mermaid Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CORE["CENTRAL DBMS"]
        DB2["Shared Manufacturing Database"]
    end

    subgraph PLAN["PLANNING MODULES"]
        M1["CAD Geometry Data"]
        M2["CAPP Process Plans"]
        M3["CAPM Schedules"]
        M4["MRP II BOM and Orders"]
    end

    subgraph EXEC["EXECUTION MODULES"]
        M5["SFCS Real Time Control"]
        M6["DNC NC Distribution"]
        M7["TMS Tool Tracking"]
        M8["MHCS Material Routing"]
        M9["CAQC Quality Module"]
    end

    PLAN --> DB2
    DB2 --> EXEC
    EXEC --> DB2
    DB2 --> PLAN
```

---

### 4.4 Functional Block Architecture — Module Interactions

| Source Module | Data Sent | Destination Module | Trigger |
|---|---|---|---|
| ERP | Order quantity, due date | CAPM | New order release |
| CAD | 3-D model (STEP/IGES) | CAPP | Design freeze |
| CAPP | Route sheet, tooling | CAPM + DBMS | Process approved |
| CAPM | Dispatch sequence | SFCS | Schedule update |
| SFCS | Machine allocation | DNC + MHCS + TMS | Machine becomes idle |
| DNC | NC code | CNC controller | Job start signal |
| MHCS | Pick/Drop commands | AGV controllers | Part requested |
| TMS | Tool change | CNC controller | Tool-life threshold |
| CAQC | Inspection data | DBMS + CAPP | Inspection complete |
| DBMS | Historical data | CAPM + CAPP | Continuous feedback |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q1. List and briefly explain any three software modules used in a Flexible Manufacturing System.**
*(3 Marks — CO4 / Remembering Level)*

**Model Answer:**
*(Any three of the following — 1 mark each for name + 2 marks collectively for brief explanation)*

1. **CAD (Computer-Aided Design)** — Used for creating the digital 3-D model of the product; output is fed to CAPP.
2. **CAPP (Computer-Aided Process Planning)** — Converts the design into a manufacturing route sheet containing operations, machines, tools, and parameters.
3. **SFCS (Shop Floor Control System)** — Real-time module that tracks every part's status and issues dispatch commands to machines, AGVs, and tool magazines.
4. **DNC (Direct Numerical Control)** — Distributes NC programs from a central computer to multiple CNC workstations over a network.
5. **MRP-II** — Plans material, capacity, and shop orders to meet the master production schedule.
6. **TMS (Tool Management System)** — Monitors tool life, schedules replacements, and maintains tool inventory.
7. **CAQC (Computer-Aided Quality Control)** — Integrates CMMs and SPC software to maintain quality in real time.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q2. What is the role of a Central Database (DBMS) in integrating FMS software modules? Why is it critical?**
*(3 Marks — CO4 / Understanding Level)*

**Model Answer:**
The **Central Database (DBMS)** acts as the **single shared repository** where every FMS module reads and writes data. CAD stores geometric models, CAPP stores route sheets, CAPM stores schedules, SFCS logs real-time events, CAQC stores inspection results, and TMS tracks tool data — all into the same database.

**Why it is critical:**
- Eliminates **data redundancy** and prevents version conflicts.
- Ensures every module works on the **same updated information** (single source of truth).
- Enables **real-time feedback** between shop-floor and planning modules.
- Supports **traceability** of every part, tool, and operation.

Without DBMS, the modules would operate in isolation, leading to scheduling conflicts and inconsistent decisions.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style Question]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *(CO4 / Applying + Analyzing Level)*

**(a)** Explain the **hierarchical structure of FMS software modules** with a neat block diagram. List at least **eight distinct modules** and state one key function of each. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** With the help of an information-flow diagram, describe how a **part is processed from design stage to dispatch** in an FMS, indicating which software module is responsible at each step. *(7 Marks)*

---

### **Model Answer — Question A**

#### (a) Hierarchical Structure of FMS Software (7 Marks)

The FMS software is structured in a **three-layer pyramid architecture**:

**Layer 1 — Management Level (Off-line Planning)**
- **CAD** — Creates the 3-D product model.
- **CAPP** — Generates the manufacturing route sheet.
- **CAPM / Scheduling** — Decides what, when, and on which machine.
- **MRP-II / ERP** — Plans materials, capacity, and shop orders.

**Layer 2 — Shop-Floor Level (Real-Time Control)**
- **SFCS** — Tracks each part's status and issues dispatch lists.
- **DNC** — Distributes NC programs to CNC machines.
- **TMS** — Monitors tool life and issues tool-change orders.
- **MHCS** — Controls AGVs, conveyors, and AS/RS.
- **CAQC** — Performs online inspection and SPC analysis.

**Layer 3 — Equipment Level**
- **CNC, PLC, Robot controllers** — Execute the actual motion commands.

A neat block diagram should be drawn (use the Mermaid diagram from Section 4.1 as reference). Award **1 mark for the diagram** and **6 marks distributed across the eight modules** (~0.75 mark per module description).

---

#### (b) Information Flow — Design to Dispatch (7 Marks)

The information flow proceeds sequentially:

| Step | Stage | Module |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Customer order received | ERP / MRP-II |
| 2 | Product design created | CAD |
| 3 | Process plan generated | CAPP |
| 4 | Production schedule prepared | CAPM |
| 5 | Material requirements planned | MRP-II |
| 6 | Dispatch list issued | SFCS |
| 7 | NC program loaded | DNC |
| 8 | Part transported | MHCS |
| 9 | Tool prepared | TMS |
| 10 | Machining performed | CNC |
| 11 | Quality inspection | CAQC |
| 12 | Data updated | DBMS |

Award **1 mark per significant step** (or aggregated as 7 marks for completeness of the flow, with credit for the diagram and module identification).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain in detail the **functions of SFCS, DNC, TMS, and MHCS** modules. How do they coordinate with each other during real-time production? *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **role of CAD/CAPP integration in FMS**. Explain *variant* and *generative* CAPP approaches with suitable examples. *(7 Marks)*

---

### **Model Answer — Question B**

#### (a) Functions of SFCS, DNC, TMS, MHCS (7 Marks)

- **SFCS (Shop Floor Control System):** Acts as the real-time coordinator. It tracks every part's status (queued, in-transit, machining, completed), maintains WIP records, issues dispatch lists, and coordinates with DNC, TMS, and MHCS. *(2 marks)*

- **DNC (Direct Numerical Control):** Distributes NC part programs from a central file server to multiple CNC machines over a LAN. Supports bidirectional communication, remote tool-offset upload, and online editing. *(2 marks)*

- **TMS (Tool Management System):** Monitors cumulative tool life using Taylor's equation. Issues tool-change requests just-in-time, maintains tool inventory, and schedules tool pre-setting. *(1.5 marks)*

- **MHCS (Material Handling Control System):** Routes AGVs, controls conveyors, and manages AS/RS. Uses shortest-path or traffic-aware algorithms to deliver parts to workstations. *(1.5 marks)*

**Coordination:**
When a machine becomes idle → SFCS receives signal → SFCS consults schedule → selects next job → informs DNC (load program), TMS (prepare tools), and MHCS (deliver raw part) **simultaneously** → machining begins only when all three confirm readiness. This is called the *"all-resources-ready"* synchronization.

---

#### (b) CAD/CAPP Integration & CAPP Variants (7 Marks)

**CAD/CAPP Integration:**
The CAD module produces a 3-D solid model with feature information (holes, pockets, slots, fillets). This geometric model is the **input to CAPP**, which converts it into a manufacturing route sheet. Modern CAD systems embed **feature-recognition algorithms** that automatically extract machinable features, enabling seamless CAD→CAPP integration. *(2 marks)*

**Variant CAPP:**
- Uses **Group Technology (GT) coding** to classify the new part into an existing family.
- Retrieves a **standard process plan** from the database and applies minor edits.
- Example: A new flanged shaft, after GT coding, is identified as belonging to the *rotational family*, and the standard turning + milling + grinding plan is reused. *(2.5 marks)*

**Generative CAPP:**
- Uses **logical rules, decision trees, and machining databases** to synthesize a brand-new process plan from scratch.
- The system reasons through *if-then-else* logic — e.g., "IF hole diameter > 50 mm AND depth < 100 mm THEN use boring operation on VMC."
- Example: A complex turbine blade with multiple curved surfaces is planned entirely from rules, since no prior family plan exists. *(2.5 marks)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in This Topic:**
> 1. **Listing modules without explaining functions** — Examiners expect at least *one clear functional line per module*. Avoid writing only acronyms like "CAD, CAPP, CAPM" without elaboration. Loss: up to **3 marks**.
> 2. **Missing the Central DBMS** — Many students forget to mention the DBMS as the backbone. The DBMS is **not a separate module in name** but is the *integration enabler*. Always include it.
> 3. **Confusing SFCS with ERP** — SFCS is *real-time shop-floor*; ERP is *long-term planning*. Mixing them up leads to **wrong context** in long answers.
> 4. **Skipping the diagram** — A block diagram of the software hierarchy is **almost always expected** in 14-mark questions. Omitting it can cost **2–3 marks**.
> 5. **Not mentioning Taylor's Tool Life Equation** for TMS — this is a *favorite KTU examiner question*. Memorize $V \cdot T^n = C$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ FMS software has **three layers**: Management → Shop-Floor → Equipment.
- ✅ **CAD** = design, **CAPP** = process planning, **CAPM** = scheduling, **MRP-II** = resource planning.
- ✅ **SFCS** = real-time shop-floor coordinator; it is the **heart of the FMS**.
- ✅ **DNC** distributes NC programs over LAN to multiple CNC machines.
- ✅ **TMS** uses **Taylor's Tool Life Equation** $V \cdot T^n = C$ to trigger tool changes.
- ✅ **MHCS** routes AGVs and controls AS/RS using shortest-path algorithms.
- ✅ **CAQC** integrates CMMs and SPC charts for real-time quality assurance.
- ✅ **DBMS** is the *central shared database* that all modules read/write — eliminates redundancy.
- ✅ CAPP has two flavours: **Variant (GT-based)** and **Generative (rule-based)**.
- ✅ Scheduling priority rules: **EDD** (Earliest Due Date), **SPT** (Shortest Processing Time), **CR** (Critical Ratio).
- ✅ Always draw a **hierarchical block diagram** in 14-mark answers to score full marks.
- ✅ Modern FMS integrates with **MES and Industry 4.0 / IoT layers** above the legacy architecture.
- ✅ **Single source of truth principle**: every module must refer to the same DBMS to avoid conflicts.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: FMS Installation and Implementation - System Installation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET757 - FLEXIBLE MANUFACTURING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** FMS Installation and Implementation - System Installation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**FMS System Installation** is the systematic, engineered process of translating a designed and planned Flexible Manufacturing System from a vendor's blueprint into a fully operational, integrated production reality on the shop floor. It encompasses all activities from **site preparation, equipment receiving, physical placement, utility hookup, control software deployment, integration testing, to final commissioning and operator handover**.

According to KTU Module 4 directives, installation is not merely a mechanical act — it is a **project management discipline** that bridges the gap between the *theoretical FMS layout* and the *live production cell*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** FMS Installation is treated as a *cyclical activity* — Plan → Procure → Place → Power → Program → Prove → Produce.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have ordered a **fully-furnished, modular smart kitchen** from a premium vendor.

- The **FMS Layout Drawing** is the architectural plan.
- The **CNC machines, robots, AGVs, conveyors** are the kitchen modules (oven, fridge, dishwasher).
- The **Tool magazine, FMS host computer** are the smart controllers.
- The **Installation team** are the engineers who assemble the kitchen, wire the gas/electricity, install the Wi-Fi, calibrate the smart-hub, and finally hand over the keys.

If the plumber connects the dishwasher pipe to the gas line, the kitchen fails — just as mis-routing **M-code signals** between the FMS host and a CNC controller can collapse the entire cell.

> [!NOTE]
> **In Simple Words:** FMS installation is the *on-site, hands-on transformation* of paperwork and parts into a synchronized, talking, moving manufacturing organism.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Bold-Standardized)

| Standard KTU Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **System Installation** | On-site assembly and commissioning of FMS hardware and software |
| **Site Readiness Audit** | Pre-delivery inspection of floor, foundation, utilities |
| **Cold Commissioning** | Power-on testing without production parts |
| **Hot Commissioning** | Live production validation with real workpieces |
| **SAT (Site Acceptance Test)** | Customer-side verification of contractual performance |
| **FAT (Factory Acceptance Test)** | Vendor-side test conducted before shipment |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Phased Breakdown of FMS Installation

**Phase 1 — Pre-Installation Planning**
- Final review of the **System Functional Specification (SFS)**.
- Verification of **civil foundation drawings**, anchor bolt templates, and **utility load schedules**.
- Risk assessment: identify hazards (high-voltage panels, hydraulic lines, robotic envelopes).
- Logistics planning: route for **heavy-lift cranes**, access corridors, customs clearance (for imported CNC/robotic cells).

**Phase 2 — Site Preparation**
- Floor must sustain **5–10 tonnes/m²** static load for standard machining centers (varies with machine footprint).
- Anti-vibration pads or **isolated concrete plinths** are mandatory for precision machining centers to decouple vibrations from neighboring cells.
- Compressed air: minimum **6 bar (0.6 MPa)** at point-of-use; **7 bar** at compressor, accounting for line losses.
- Electrical: **3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz** supply with isolated neutral; **earth resistance < 1 Ω**.
- Network: dedicated **industrial Ethernet (Cat 6A / PROFINET / EtherCAT)** with shielded cabling for the FMS LAN.

**Phase 3 — Equipment Receiving & Inspection**
- Crate opening under controlled humidity (avoid condensation on precision spindles).
- Verification against the **packing list, bill of materials, and calibration certificates**.
- Initial dimensional check using a **laser tracker** or precision level.

**Phase 4 — Physical Installation**
- Cranes lift machines to within **±2 mm** of planned coordinate.
- Levelling: **≤ 0.02 mm/1000 mm** for precision CNC turning/milling centers.
- Geometric alignment of machine tool axes to the **FMS reference datum** (typically the AGV guide path or conveyor centerline).

**Phase 5 — Utility Connections**
- Power, pneumatic, hydraulic, coolant, exhaust, and **data networks** are routed through **trunking or overhead busways**.
- Cable trays must follow the **EMC segregation rule** — power and signal cables separated by at least **300 mm** to avoid electromagnetic interference (EMI).

**Phase 6 — Control Software Installation**
- Loading the **FMS Host Software (scheduler, dispatcher, tool management, AGV traffic control)**.
- Loading **PLC ladder logic**, **HMI screens**, and **CNC macro programs**.
- Configuring **DNC (Distributed Numerical Control)** links via Ethernet.

**Phase 7 — Integration & Testing**
- **Cold commissioning:** energize without parts; verify interlocks, E-stops, light curtains.
- **Hot commissioning:** run actual part programs; validate tool changes, pallet transfers, robotic pick-and-place.
- **Site Acceptance Test (SAT):** measured metrics must match contract — typically **MTBF ≥ 500 hours**, **availability ≥ 95%**.

**Phase 8 — Training & Documentation**
- Operator training on HMI, alarm handling, basic TPM.
- Maintenance training on spindle replacement, pallet changer diagnostics.
- As-built documentation: electrical schematics, pneumatic diagrams, network topology, software backups.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulae below are **directly examinable** in KTU University exams for FMS cost / installation problems.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{total} = T_{pre} + T_{inst} + T_{comm} + T_{train}$ | Total installation project time | days |
| 2 | $C_{total} = C_{equip} + C_{site} + C_{util} + C_{labor} + C_{train} + C_{down}$ | Total installation cost | ₹ / USD |
| 3 | $PBP = \dfrac{C_{install}}{S_{annual}}$ | Payback period (simple) | years |
| 4 | $ROI = \dfrac{(S_{annual} - C_{operate})}{C_{install}} \times 100$ | Return on investment | % |
| 5 | $A = \dfrac{(MTBF)}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100$ | System availability | % |
| 6 | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | Overall Equipment Effectiveness | dimensionless |
| 7 | $N_{eff} = \dfrac{\text{Compressed air at POU}}{\text{Compressor rated pressure - line loss}}$ | Pneumatic availability check | bar |
| 8 | $\eta_{floor} = \dfrac{L_{machine}}{A_{cell}}$ | Floor-space utilization ratio | % |

Where:
- $MTBF$ = Mean Time Between Failures
- $MTTR$ = Mean Time To Repair
- $A$ = Availability, $P$ = Performance, $Q$ = Quality
- $L_{machine}$ = Footprint of machines, $A_{cell}$ = Total cell area

### 2.3 Engineering Utility of These Concepts

In real **automotive, aerospace, and heavy-engineering industries**, FMS installation projects worth **₹50 crore – ₹500 crore** routinely use these formulas to:
- Bid tender pricing.
- Justify CAPEX to boards.
- Track project health in **Gantt charts and Earned Value Analysis (EVA)**.
- Benchmark SAT performance against original equipment manufacturer (OEM) claims.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Installation Cost Derivation

**Problem Statement (KTU Pattern):**
> An FMS cell costs **₹2,00,00,000** (equipment). Site preparation adds **8%**, utility hookup **5%**, labor and rigging **12%**, training **3%**, and commissioning downtime loss is **₹4,00,000/day for 15 days**. Compute the total installed cost.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Let us define each cost component from the given problem data.

The base equipment cost is denoted as $C_{equip} = ₹\,2,00,00,000$.

$$
C_{equip} = 2.0 \times 10^{7} \text{ ₹}
$$

The site preparation cost is computed as a percentage of the equipment cost.

$$
C_{site} = 0.08 \times C_{equip} = 0.08 \times 2.0 \times 10^{7}
$$

$$
C_{site} = 1.6 \times 10^{6} \text{ ₹}
$$

The utility hookup cost is similarly derived.

$$
C_{util} = 0.05 \times C_{equip} = 0.05 \times 2.0 \times 10^{7}
$$

$$
C_{util} = 1.0 \times 10^{6} \text{ ₹}
$$

The labor and rigging cost follows the same percentage rule.

$$
C_{labor} = 0.12 \times C_{equip} = 0.12 \times 2.0 \times 10^{7}
$$

$$
C_{labor} = 2.4 \times 10^{6} \text{ ₹}
$$

The training cost is similarly computed.

$$
C_{train} = 0.03 \times C_{equip} = 0.03 \times 2.0 \times 10^{7}
$$

$$
C_{train} = 6.0 \times 10^{5} \text{ ₹}
$$

The downtime cost is the product of daily loss and duration.

$$
C_{down} = 4.0 \times 10^{5} \times 15 = 6.0 \times 10^{6} \text{ ₹}
$$

The total installed cost is the algebraic sum of all six components.

$$
C_{total} = C_{equip} + C_{site} + C_{util} + C_{labor} + C_{train} + C_{down}
$$

Substituting all computed values into the summation.

$$
C_{total} = (2.0 + 0.16 + 0.10 + 0.24 + 0.06 + 0.60) \times 10^{7}
$$

$$
C_{total} = 3.16 \times 10^{7} \text{ ₹}
$$

**Result:** Total installed cost = **₹ 3,16,00,000** (a **58%** premium over equipment cost alone).

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Payback Period & ROI Derivation

**Problem Statement (KTU Pattern):**
> The installed FMS generates an annual saving of **₹80,00,000** by replacing manual cells. Annual operating cost (power, consumables, maintenance, indirect labor) is **₹25,00,000**. Using the $C_{total}$ from the previous example, compute the simple payback period and the ROI.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

The annual net saving (annual surplus) is the difference between the gross saving and the operating cost.

$$
S_{annual} = 8.0 \times 10^{6} - 2.5 \times 10^{6} = 5.5 \times 10^{6} \text{ ₹}
$$

The simple payback period is the ratio of installed cost to net annual saving.

$$
PBP = \dfrac{C_{install}}{S_{annual}} = \dfrac{3.16 \times 10^{7}}{5.5 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
PBP = 5.745 \text{ years}
$$

The ROI is the ratio of net annual saving to installed cost, expressed as a percentage.

$$
ROI = \dfrac{S_{annual}}{C_{install}} \times 100 = \dfrac{5.5 \times 10^{6}}{3.16 \times 10^{7}} \times 100
$$

$$
ROI \approx 17.41 \, \%
$$

**Result:** Payback period ≈ **5.75 years**, ROI ≈ **17.4% per annum**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Always state *net* saving before computing payback. Forgetting to subtract operating cost is the **#1 mark-deduction mistake** in cost analysis questions.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Installation Risk Severity

| Risk Category | Severity | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Foundation settling after crane lift | High | Allow **28-day concrete curing**; use laser tracker for re-check |
| Utility phase mismatch (415 V vs 480 V) | Critical | Transformer / phase converter pre-installed |
| EMC interference on PROFINET | Medium | Shielded cables, **300 mm** segregation, ferrite cores |
| Software license mismatch (DNC seats) | Medium | Pre-audit license count vs HMI count |
| Operator-induced damage during early run | High | Restricted-access mode for first **30 days** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 FMS Installation Process Flow (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Project Kickoff and Charter] --> A2[Site Readiness Audit]
    A2 --> A3[Civil Foundation and Utilities]
    A3 --> A4[Equipment Receipt and Inspection]
    A4 --> A5[Physical Placement and Levelling]
    A5 --> A6[Utility Power Air Network Hookup]
    A6 --> A7[Control Software and DNC Loading]
    A7 --> A8[Cold Commissioning No Parts]
    A8 --> A9[Hot Commissioning Live Parts]
    A9 --> A10[Site Acceptance Test SAT]
    A10 --> A11[Operator and Maintenance Training]
    A11 --> A12[Production Handover and Documentation]

    A8 -. Fail .-> A7
    A10 -. Fail .-> A9
```

### 4.2 FMS Installation Functional Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PL[Planning Layer]
        P1[Project Manager]
        P2[Risk Register]
        P3[Gantt Schedule]
    end

    subgraph SL[Site Layer]
        S1[Civil Foundation]
        S2[Power Distribution]
        S3[Compressed Air and Coolant]
        S4[Industrial Ethernet LAN]
    end

    subgraph EL[Equipment Layer]
        E1[CNC Machining Centers]
        E2[Robotic Loader]
        E3[AGV or Conveyor]
        E4[Tool Magazine and Wash]
    end

    subgraph CL[Control Layer]
        C1[FMS Host Computer]
        C2[Cell Controller PLC]
        C3[CNC and Robot Controllers]
        C4[HMI Stations]
    end

    subgraph VL[Validation Layer]
        V1[Cold Commissioning]
        V2[Hot Commissioning]
        V3[SAT Report]
        V4[Training Records]
    end

    P1 --> S1
    P2 --> S3
    P3 --> E1
    S1 --> E1
    S2 --> C3
    S3 --> E1
    S4 --> C1
    E1 --> C3
    E2 --> C3
    E3 --> C2
    C3 --> C1
    C1 --> C4
    C4 --> V1
    V1 --> V2
    V2 --> V3
    V3 --> V4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The architecture flows from *planning intent* (left) → *physical reality* (centre) → *validated, trained operation* (right). Any **broken arrow** in the SAT phase signals a fallback loop, which is normal during installation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern]**

**Q1.** What is meant by **Site Acceptance Test (SAT)** in FMS installation? Differentiate it from **Factory Acceptance Test (FAT)**.

**Model Answer:**
SAT is the **on-customer-site validation test** conducted after the FMS has been fully installed, integrated, and powered up. It verifies that the system meets contractual performance metrics (cycle time, MTBF, tool-change time, pallet-switch time) under *actual production conditions*.

| Aspect | FAT | SAT |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | Vendor's factory | Customer's shop floor |
| **Purpose** | Prove design before shipment | Prove installation before handover |
| **Environment** | Controlled, vendor-staffed | Real production, customer-staffed |
| **Witnessed by** | Customer's engineers | Customer's acceptance team |
| **Risk covered** | Design defects | Installation + integration defects |

**(3 Marks Awarded: 1 for SAT definition + 1 for FAT definition + 1 for crisp comparison.)**

---

**Q2.** List any **six pre-installation site readiness checks** for an FMS.

**Model Answer:**
1. **Floor load-bearing capacity** verified against heaviest machine + pallet + fixture mass.
2. **Foundation curing** — minimum 28 days for concrete plinths.
3. **Compressed air pressure** ≥ **6 bar** at point of use, with adequate **dryer and FRL** unit.
4. **Electrical supply** — 3-phase **415 V, 50 Hz** with earth resistance **< 1 Ω**.
5. **Industrial Ethernet cabling** laid with **300 mm segregation** from power cables.
6. **Coolant, hydraulic oil, and exhaust ducting** routed and terminated.
7. **Lighting ≥ 500 lux** at operator stations and inspection zones.
8. **Ergonomic clearance** for operator reach, HMI visibility, and robotic envelope barriers.

**(Award ½ mark per valid point, max 3 marks.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Pattern, Module 4]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **phased methodology of FMS system installation**, from site preparation through final commissioning. Discuss the critical checkpoints at each phase. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An FMS with an equipment cost of **₹1.5 crore** has the following installation cost components: site preparation 10%, utility hookup 6%, labor 14%, training 4%, and downtime loss of **₹3 lakh/day for 20 days**. The system saves **₹60 lakh/year** with an annual operating cost of **₹20 lakh/year**. Compute the **total installed cost, payback period, and ROI**. **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Phased Installation Methodology (7 Marks)

The FMS installation is executed in **eight logical phases**, each with defined deliverables and exit criteria.

**Phase 1 — Project Initiation (0.5 mark)**
Charter signed, stakeholders identified, KPIs locked (cycle time, MTBF, throughput).

**Phase 2 — Site Readiness Audit (1.0 mark)**
Floor strength, ceiling height, utility stubs, network drops verified using the *Civil Readiness Checklist*.

**Phase 3 — Civil and Foundation Work (1.0 mark)**
Anti-vibration plinths cast; cable trenches, coolant pits, exhaust hoods constructed.

**Phase 4 — Equipment Receipt and Inspection (1.0 mark)**
Crate humidity check, dimensional verification against packing list, latent-damage recording.

**Phase 5 — Physical Placement and Levelling (1.0 mark)**
Cranes position machines to within ±2 mm of FMS reference datum; laser tracker alignment to **≤ 0.02 mm/1000 mm**.

**Phase 6 — Utility Connections and Software Loading (1.0 mark)**
Power, pneumatic, hydraulic, network, FMS host software, PLC, HMI, DNC configured.

**Phase 7 — Cold and Hot Commissioning (1.0 mark)**
Cold: interlocks, E-stops, light curtains. Hot: real parts, real tool changes, real AGV traffic.

**Phase 8 — SAT, Training, Handover (0.5 mark)**
Performance metrics verified; operator and maintenance teams certified; as-built documents handed over.

**Critical Checkpoints:** Each phase has a **gate-review meeting** with the customer; passing criteria are documented before progressing to the next phase.

#### Part (b) — Cost and ROI Computation (7 Marks)

The base equipment cost is defined as $C_{equip}$.

$$
C_{equip} = 1.5 \times 10^{7} \text{ ₹}
$$

The site preparation cost is ten percent of the equipment cost.

$$
C_{site} = 0.10 \times C_{equip} = 1.5 \times 10^{6} \text{ ₹}
$$

The utility hookup cost is six percent of the equipment cost.

$$
C_{util} = 0.06 \times C_{equip} = 9.0 \times 10^{5} \text{ ₹}
$$

The labor and rigging cost is fourteen percent of the equipment cost.

$$
C_{labor} = 0.14 \times C_{equip} = 2.1 \times 10^{6} \text{ ₹}
$$

The training cost is four percent of the equipment cost.

$$
C_{train} = 0.04 \times C_{equip} = 6.0 \times 10^{5} \text{ ₹}
$$

The downtime loss is the product of daily loss and project duration.

$$
C_{down} = 3.0 \times 10^{5} \times 20 = 6.0 \times 10^{6} \text{ ₹}
$$

The total installed cost is the sum of all six components.

$$
C_{total} = (1.5 + 0.15 + 0.09 + 0.21 + 0.06 + 0.60) \times 10^{7}
$$

$$
C_{total} = 2.61 \times 10^{7} \text{ ₹}
$$

The net annual saving is computed first.

$$
S_{net} = 6.0 \times 10^{6} - 2.0 \times 10^{6} = 4.0 \times 10^{6} \text{ ₹/year}
$$

The payback period is then derived as the ratio.

$$
PBP = \dfrac{2.61 \times 10^{7}}{4.0 \times 10^{6}} = 6.525 \text{ years}
$$

Finally, the ROI is computed and expressed as a percentage.

$$
ROI = \dfrac{4.0 \times 10^{6}}{2.61 \times 10^{7}} \times 100 \approx 15.33 \, \%
$$

**Tabulated Result for Clarity:**

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Total Installed Cost | **₹ 2,61,00,000** |
| Net Annual Saving | **₹ 40,00,000** |
| Payback Period | **6.53 years** |
| ROI | **15.33% per annum** |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Discuss the **role of utilities (electrical, pneumatic, hydraulic, network)** in FMS installation. What standards must be met for each utility? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** During FMS commissioning, the system recorded **MTBF = 600 hours** and **MTTR = 4 hours**. The cell operates on a **3-shift schedule (24 hours/day)** for **300 days/year**. Determine (i) the system **availability**, (ii) the **OEE** if performance efficiency is **90%** and quality rate is **98%**, and (iii) the **annual downtime hours**. **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — Utilities in FMS Installation (7 Marks)

**Electrical Utility (1.5 marks):** FMS requires stable **3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz** supply. **Voltage fluctuation tolerance is ±10%**, and **frequency tolerance is ±2%**. Earth resistance must be **< 1 Ω**. Power and signal cables must be segregated by **300 mm** to avoid EMI. A dedicated **UPS and servo stabilizer** protects the FMS host computer.

**Pneumatic Utility (1.5 marks):** Required for clamping, pallet locking, and tool unclamping. Pressure at point-of-use must be **6 bar**, with **FRL (Filter-Regulator-Lubricator)** units. Dew point must be **≤ 3°C** to avoid moisture damage. ISO 8573-1 Class 3 air quality is mandated.

**Hydraulic Utility (1.0 mark):** Used in heavy-duty fixtures and pallet changers. Pressure ratings are typically **70–210 bar**. Filtration to **ISO 4406 18/16/13** cleanliness code is essential. Temperature must remain below **60°C**.

**Coolant Utility (1.0 mark):** Required for CNC spindles. Flow rate depends on kW rating — generally **1 L/min per kW**. Coolant must be filtered to **25 µm** and maintained at **pH 8.5–9.5** to prevent corrosion and microbial growth.

**Network Utility (2.0 marks):** **Industrial Ethernet (PROFINET, EtherCAT, or EtherNet/IP)** at **100 Mbps minimum, 1 Gbps preferred**. Shielded Cat 6A cabling in dedicated trays. Use of **managed switches with VLAN segmentation** isolates FMS traffic from corporate IT traffic for cybersecurity.

#### Part (b) — Availability, OEE, and Downtime (7 Marks)

The system availability is computed first using the MTBF and MTTR values.

$$
A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100 = \dfrac{600}{600 + 4} \times 100
$$

$$
A = \dfrac{600}{604} \times 100 \approx 99.34 \, \%
$$

The Overall Equipment Effectiveness is the product of availability, performance, and quality.

$$
OEE = A \times P \times Q = 0.9934 \times 0.90 \times 0.98
$$

$$
OEE \approx 0.8762 \approx 87.62 \, \%
$$

The total available operating hours per year is calculated from shift duration and working days.

$$
H_{total} = 24 \times 300 = 7200 \text{ hours/year}
$$

The annual downtime is the complement of availability multiplied by total hours.

$$
H_{down} = H_{total} \times \left(1 - \dfrac{A}{100}\right) = 7200 \times (1 - 0.9934)
$$

$$
H_{down} = 7200 \times 0.0066 \approx 47.52 \text{ hours/year}
$$

**Summary of Computed Metrics:**

| Metric | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| System Availability | $A$ | **99.34 %** |
| Overall Equipment Effectiveness | $OEE$ | **87.62 %** |
| Annual Downtime | $H_{down}$ | **47.52 hours/year** |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in FMS Installation Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting downtime cost** when summing $C_{total}$ — loses up to **2 marks**.
> 2. **Using gross saving** instead of *net saving* in payback/ROI formulas — wrong answer, **0 marks** awarded.
> 3. **Confusing SAT with FAT** — examiners award only **half marks** if the student does not explicitly differentiate.
> 4. **Skipping utility segregation (300 mm rule)** — this is a *favorite KTU question*, lose **1 mark** if omitted.
> 5. **Mixing OEE inputs** — always use **decimal form** (0.90, not 90%) inside the OEE multiplication, otherwise the result is off by a factor of 100.
> 6. **Ignoring cable EMC segregation** while describing network installation — recurring pitfall.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — FMS Installation**

- **Installation is a project**, not a one-day activity — it spans **3 to 12 months** for typical mid-size FMS cells.
- Always follow the **eight-phase methodology**: Kickoff → Site Audit → Civil → Receipt → Placement → Utilities + Software → Commissioning → SAT + Training.
- **Site readiness precedes equipment arrival** — *never* accept machines before foundation is cured and utilities are stubbed.
- **Levelling tolerance** of **≤ 0.02 mm/1000 mm** is non-negotiable for precision CNC centers.
- **Compressed air** at point-of-use must be **≥ 6 bar** with dew point **≤ 3°C**.
- **Earth resistance** must be **< 1 Ω**; **voltage tolerance** ±10%; **frequency tolerance** ±2%.
- **Power and signal cable segregation** = minimum **300 mm** (EMI control).
- **Total installed cost** = Equipment + Site + Utility + Labor + Training + Downtime loss.
- **Payback Period** = $C_{install} / S_{net}$; **ROI** = $(S_{net} / C_{install}) \times 100$.
- **System Availability** = $(MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)) \times 100$.
- **OEE** = $A \times P \times Q$ — world-class benchmark is **≥ 85%**.
- **FAT** happens at the vendor's factory; **SAT** happens at the customer's shop floor.
- **Cold commissioning** is energization without parts; **hot commissioning** runs real workpieces.
- **Network** uses shielded industrial Ethernet; segregate FMS VLAN from corporate IT.
- **Documentation deliverables**: as-built drawings, parameter backups, training records, SAT report.
- **Risk mitigation**: 28-day concrete curing, restricted operator access for first 30 days, license audit before software load.
- Always state **net saving** in cost-benefit questions — examiners deduct marks for gross saving.
- **Three-shift operation** of 24 hours × 300 days = **7200 available hours/year**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Acceptance Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET757 - FLEXIBLE MANUFACTURING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Acceptance Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Acceptance Testing** in a Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS) is the formal, contractually binding verification procedure executed after system installation to objectively confirm that the delivered FMS — comprising CNC machines, automated material handling systems (AGVs/RGVs/conveyors), central control computer, tool management, and supervisory software — conforms in full to the **functional specifications, performance benchmarks, geometric accuracies, and reliability targets** mutually agreed upon in the purchase contract and technical annexures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Standard):**
> *"Acceptance Testing is a structured, measurable, and documented evaluation process performed to certify that the Flexible Manufacturing System has achieved the design performance, machining accuracy, throughput, and reliability levels stipulated in the system specification, prior to formal hand-over from vendor to user."*

The procedure typically comprises two sequential phases:
1. **Factory Acceptance Test (FAT)** — Conducted at the vendor's premises before dispatch.
2. **Site Acceptance Test (SAT)** — Conducted at the user's site after full installation and integration.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have ordered a **custom-built, fully automated kitchen** for a five-star hotel restaurant. Before you pay the final instalment and start serving customers, you would naturally want to verify that:

- The **ovens** reach the exact temperature promised (performance verification).
- The **dishwasher** cleans 500 plates per hour as claimed (throughput test).
- The **robotic arms** place dishes at the right spot 99.9 times out of 100 (accuracy/repeatability).
- The **cold storage** does not fail for at least 5,000 hours continuously (reliability test).
- The **automated doors** open within 2 seconds every single time (response time).

You would document every test on paper, sign it, and only then accept the kitchen.

**This is exactly what Acceptance Testing is for an FMS.** The FMS is the "automated kitchen," and the tests ensure the entire integrated system — not just individual machines — performs as a unified whole.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** Acceptance Testing is **NOT** debugging or trial running. It is a **legal and contractual milestone** where the financial liability shifts from the vendor to the buyer upon successful completion. A failed test means the vendor must rectify at their own cost.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (Highlighted)

The following performance and reliability indicators form the backbone of any FMS acceptance test:

- **MTBF** = Mean Time Between Failures (hours)
- **MTTR** = Mean Time To Repair (hours)
- **System Availability** = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR) × 100%
- **OEE** = Overall Equipment Effectiveness (%)
- **Positioning Accuracy** measured in micrometres (µm)
- **Repeatability** measured in micrometres (µm)
- **Throughput** measured in parts/hour or jobs/shift
- **MTM** = Mean Time to Manufacture a part (minutes)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Acceptance Tests in FMS

Acceptance testing in an FMS is not a single test — it is a **structured hierarchy of tests**, each verifying a different layer of system performance. The hierarchy (from unit-level to system-level) is as follows:

#### A. Geometric & Accuracy Tests
These tests validate the static and dynamic positioning capability of the CNC machines and the entire handling subsystem.

- **Positioning Accuracy Test** — Measures the deviation between the commanded axis position and the actual position achieved. Standard reference: **ISO 230-2** and **VDI 3441**.
- **Repeatability Test** — Measures the spread (bi-directional) of positions when the same axis is commanded to the same coordinate multiple times. Critical for unmanned operation.
- **Hirth Coupling / Indexing Accuracy Test** — For rotary axes and pallet changers.
- **Squareness, Straightness, and Flatness Tests** — Per **ISO 230-1**.

#### B. Performance Tests
These verify the dynamic, time-based operating capabilities of the FMS.

- **Tool Change Time Test** — Time from end of one tool cut to start of next cut with new tool (typically **T_ct ≤ 8 to 15 seconds** for modern machining centres).
- **Pallet Change Time Test** — Time to swap a finished workpiece pallet for a raw one.
- **Automatic Workpiece Change Time Test**.
- **Chip-to-Chip Time Test** — End-to-end tool swap including retract, magazine index, and re-engage.

#### C. Functional & Control Tests
- **DNC / Network Communication Test** — Data transfer integrity from host computer to CNCs.
- **Software Functionality Test** — All modules of the FMS supervisory software (scheduling, routing, tool management, diagnostics) exercised.
- **Interlock & Safety Test** — E-stops, light curtains, door interlocks, fire suppression interfaces.
- **Tool Management System Test** — Tool ID recognition, tool life tracking, broken-tool detection.

#### D. Productivity / Throughput Tests
- **Shift Production Test** — Run a representative mix of part programs for a full simulated shift and count completed parts.
- **System Utilisation Test** — Time study to determine the ratio of value-adding time to total available time.

#### E. Reliability / Endurance Tests
- **Unmanned Shift Test (Lights-Out Test)** — Continuous 8-hour (or 40-hour, depending on contract) unmanned operation with statistical sampling of part quality.
- **MTBF Verification Test** — Observed MTBF ≥ Contractually agreed MTBF (commonly **≥ 300 to 500 hours** for FMS workstations).

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Test Layer

| Test Layer | What it Answers | Why it Matters for FMS |
|---|---|---|
| Geometric | *Where is the tool?* | FMS runs unattended — positional drift causes scrap batches. |
| Performance | *How fast does it move?* | Determines throughput and ROI of the entire system. |
| Functional | *Do the parts talk to each other?* | FMS is a cyber-physical system — integration failures are common. |
| Productivity | *How many parts per shift?* | Direct measure of commercial viability. |
| Reliability | *Will it survive the night shift?* | FMS is sold on the promise of unmanned operation. |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are board-exam relevant. Memorise the bold terms and the symbolic relationships.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes / KTU Trick |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **System Availability (A)** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | dimensionless (or %) | Range 0 to 1; FMS target $\geq$ 0.95 |
| 2 | **MTBF** | $MTBF = \dfrac{T_{total}}{N_{failures}}$ | hours | $T_{total}$ = total operating time |
| 3 | **MTTR** | $MTTR = \dfrac{\sum T_{repair,i}}{N_{failures}}$ | hours | Includes diagnosis, repair, restart |
| 4 | **OEE** | $OEE = A \times P_e \times Q$ | dimensionless (or %) | $P_e$ = Performance, $Q$ = Quality |
| 5 | **Performance Efficiency ($P_e$)** | $P_e = \dfrac{T_{ideal}}{T_{actual}}$ | dimensionless | Often capped at 85% per industry convention |
| 6 | **Quality Rate (Q)** | $Q = \dfrac{N_{good}}{N_{total}}$ | dimensionless | $N_{good}$ = conforming parts |
| 7 | **Positioning Accuracy (A_bi)** | $A_{bi} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i \uparrow - x_i \downarrow)$ | mm or µm | Bi-directional mean error |
| 8 | **Repeatability (R)** | $R = 6\sigma_x$ | mm or µm | Six-sigma spread per ISO 230-2 |
| 9 | **Throughput (Th)** | $Th = \dfrac{N_{parts}}{T_{shift}}$ | parts/hour | Direct output measure |
| 10 | **Unmanned Utilisation (U_u)** | $U_u = \dfrac{T_{unmanned, productive}}{T_{total, available}}$ | dimensionless | Indicator of lights-out viability |

> [!NOTE]
> **LaTeX Tip for KTU Answer Sheets:** When writing $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$, always mention that the result is multiplied by **100%** when stated as a percentage. Examiners allocate marks for the unit/scale.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern automotive and aerospace production, an FMS acceptance test certificate is the **single document that triggers the warranty period, the final 10–20% payment milestone, and the activation of penalty clauses** if the system under-performs. The documented test data is also used as the **baseline for future preventive maintenance schedules**. Hence, acceptance testing is simultaneously a **legal artefact, a technical benchmark, and a maintenance reference** — a tri-purpose deliverable unique to large-scale manufacturing automation projects.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) in FMS Context

The OEE metric, originally formulated by **Seiichi Nakajima** for TPM (Total Productive Maintenance), is the most quoted single number in FMS acceptance testing. Below is the rigorous derivation accepted by KTU examiners.

#### Step 1: Define the three constituent losses

An FMS workstation has three fundamental loss categories:
- **Availability Loss** = Downtime (breakdowns, set-ups, adjustments).
- **Performance Loss** = Reduced speed (idling, minor stoppages, sub-optimal feeds).
- **Quality Loss** = Defective parts (rejects, reworks, start-up rejects).

#### Step 2: Express each component as a ratio

Availability is the fraction of scheduled time the equipment is *actually* running.

$$
A = \dfrac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}}
$$

Performance Efficiency compares the actual cycle rate to the theoretical standard cycle rate.

$$
P_e = \dfrac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces}}{\text{Operating Time}}
$$

Quality Rate is the fraction of pieces that meet specification first time.

$$
Q = \dfrac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces Produced}}
$$

#### Step 3: Combine multiplicatively

The OEE is the product of the three independent ratios because each loss is independent and multiplicative in nature (losses compound, they do not add linearly).

$$
\begin{aligned}
OEE &= A \times P_e \times Q \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}} \times \dfrac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces}}{\text{Operating Time}} \times \dfrac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces Produced}}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4: Simplify the expression

Notice that **Operating Time cancels** from numerator and denominator between the first and second fractions, and **Total Pieces cancels** between the second and third fractions.

$$
\begin{aligned}
OEE &= \dfrac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Planned Production Time}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{\text{Value-Adding Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The final simplified form reveals a deep truth — *OEE is the fraction of scheduled time during which the FMS is producing good parts at theoretical maximum speed*. This is why world-class OEE targets are around **85%** (the product of 90% × 95% × 99%), and any FMS delivering above 70% OEE is considered excellent.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Board-Exam Style)

**Problem:** During a 1-week Site Acceptance Test of an FMS workstation, the following data was collected:
- Total scheduled production time = 168 hours
- Total downtime (failures + set-ups) = 8 hours
- Total pieces produced = 2,400 units
- Standard cycle time per piece = 0.05 hours (3 minutes)
- Number of defective pieces = 60 units

**Step 1: Compute Operating Time**

$$
T_{op} = 168 - 8 = 160 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Availability**

$$
A = \dfrac{160}{168} = 0.9524 = 95.24\%
$$

**Step 3: Compute Performance Efficiency**

$$
P_e = \dfrac{0.05 \times 2400}{160} = \dfrac{120}{160} = 0.75 = 75\%
$$

**Step 4: Compute Quality Rate**

$$
Q = \dfrac{2400 - 60}{2400} = \dfrac{2340}{2400} = 0.975 = 97.5\%
$$

**Step 5: Compute OEE**

$$
OEE = 0.9524 \times 0.75 \times 0.975 = 0.6966 \approx 69.66\%
$$

**Step 6: Interpretation**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An OEE of **69.66%** is above the global manufacturing average (~60%) but below the **world-class benchmark (85%)**. If the contract specified OEE $\geq$ 75%, the FMS **fails acceptance** and the vendor must improve performance efficiency (likely through better NC program optimisation or reduced minor stoppages).

### 3.3 MTBF / MTTR Derivation Skeleton

If during a 1000-hour acceptance test an FMS workstation fails 4 times, and the total repair time across all four failures is 6 hours, then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
MTBF &= \dfrac{1000 - 6}{4} = \dfrac{994}{4} = 248.5 \text{ hours} \\[6pt]
MTTR &= \dfrac{6}{4} = 1.5 \text{ hours} \\[6pt]
A &= \dfrac{248.5}{248.5 + 1.5} = \dfrac{248.5}{250} = 0.994 = 99.4\%
\end{aligned}
$$

This extremely high availability is typical of modern CNC machines; the limiting factor in FMS availability is usually the **material handling system (AGVs, conveyors)**, not the machine tools themselves.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 FMS Acceptance Testing — End-to-End Process Flow

The diagram below maps the complete acceptance testing lifecycle, from pre-test preparation through final certification, illustrating the iterative loops that occur when a sub-test fails.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Contract Signing and Specification Freeze] --> B[Define Acceptance Test Protocol ATP]
    B --> C[Factory Acceptance Test FAT at Vendor Site]
    C --> D{FAT Pass?}
    D -- No --> E[Vendor Rectification at Vendor Cost]
    E --> C
    D -- Yes --> F[Dispatch and Re-installation at User Site]
    F --> G[Site Acceptance Test SAT - Phase 1 Geometric and Accuracy]
    G --> H[SAT - Phase 2 Performance and Cycle Time]
    H --> I[SAT - Phase 3 Functional and Software]
    I --> J[SAT - Phase 4 Unmanned Endurance Run]
    J --> K{All SAT Phases Pass?}
    K -- No --> L[Documented Punch List and Vendor Re-work]
    L --> M[Re-test Specific Failed Subsystems]
    M --> K
    K -- Yes --> N[Sign Final Acceptance Certificate]
    N --> O[Warranty Period Commences]
    O --> P[Baseline Data Fed to CMMS for Maintenance]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an FMS Under Test

The schematic below illustrates the eight functional sub-systems whose integration is jointly verified during acceptance testing.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CNCC[CNC Machining Centres] --> CTL[Central Control Computer]
    AGVS[AGV or RGV Material Handling] --> CTL
    CVS[Conveyor and Buffer Systems] --> CTL
    TMS[Tool Management System] --> CNCC
    WPC[Workpiece and Pallet Changers] --> CNCC
    QC[In-process Quality Stations] --> CTL
    SCADA[Supervisory SCADA and MES] --> CTL
    SAF[Safety and Interlock Network] --> CNCC
    SAF --> AGVS
    SAF --> CVS
    CTL --> DB[(Shared Database and Tool Life Logs)]
    SCADA --> DB
    QC --> DB
    DB --> RPT[Acceptance Test Report and KPIs]
```

### 4.3 Acceptance Test Hierarchy — Test Pyramid

The pyramid below shows the recommended sequencing: start narrow (per-component) and widen to full-system. This is the de-facto industry pattern taught in KTU Module 4.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Level 1 Individual Machine Tool Tests] --> L2[Level 2 Workstation Integration Tests]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3 Material Handling and WPC Tests]
    L3 --> L4[Level 4 Central Control and Software Tests]
    L4 --> L5[Level 5 Full FMS Unmanned Endurance Test]
    L5 --> L6[Level 6 Final Performance and Throughput Verification]
```

### 4.4 Interpretation of the Diagrams

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide for Examiners:** When asked to "explain the acceptance testing procedure," a labelled flowchart of the kind above, even if hand-drawn in the answer book, typically fetches **full 7 marks** for a 7-mark sub-question. Always include the **{decision diamond}** for pass/fail gates — it demonstrates understanding of the iterative rectification loop, which is the most often missed element in student answers.
