## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**
> **Define Acceptance Testing of an FMS. List the two principal phases of acceptance testing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Acceptance Testing is the formal, contractually binding verification procedure performed to confirm that the installed Flexible Manufacturing System meets the design specifications, performance benchmarks, geometric accuracies, and reliability targets agreed upon in the system contract, before final hand-over to the user. (2 marks)

The two principal phases are:
1. **Factory Acceptance Test (FAT)** — performed at the vendor's premises before dispatch. (0.5 marks)
2. **Site Acceptance Test (SAT)** — performed at the user's site after full installation and integration. (0.5 marks)

---

> **Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
> **List any six parameters typically verified during FMS acceptance testing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.5 mark each):**

1. Positioning accuracy and repeatability of CNC axes (per ISO 230-2).
2. Tool change time and pallet change time.
3. System availability (MTBF / MTTR).
4. Throughput (parts per shift).
5. Unmanned (lights-out) operation capability.
6. Software functionality — scheduling, tool management, DNC communication.

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]**
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **Factory Acceptance Test (FAT)** procedure for an FMS. What are the key geometric, functional, and documentation checks performed? **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** An FMS workstation was run for 720 hours during Site Acceptance Testing. During this period, 3 failures occurred with individual repair times of 2 hours, 1.5 hours, and 2.5 hours respectively. Calculate the **MTBF, MTTR, and System Availability**. Comment on whether the workstation would pass an acceptance criterion of **Availability $\geq$ 98%**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

The Factory Acceptance Test is the **first formal acceptance milestone** and is performed at the vendor's manufacturing facility before the FMS is dismantled and shipped. It aims to detect integration defects when rectification is still cheap and convenient.

**Geometric Checks (2 marks):**
- **Positioning accuracy** of each linear axis measured per **ISO 230-2** using a laser interferometer.
- **Repeatability** of each axis computed as 6$\sigma$ of bi-directional positioning data.
- **Squareness** between X and Y axes verified with a square master and dial indicator.
- **Spindle run-out** and **axis backlash** measurements.

**Functional Checks (2 marks):**
- **Tool changer** cycle time verified over 100 consecutive changes.
- **Pallet changer** operation tested for full rotation and clamping force.
- **Coolant, chip conveyor, and hydraulics** run continuously for 8 hours.
- **Safety interlocks, e-stops, and door switches** validated.
- **DNC link** between control computer and CNC is exercised with sample NC programs.

**Documentation Checks (1.5 marks):**
- Verification of operating manuals, maintenance manuals, NC parameter lists, ladder logic printouts, electrical schematics, and software licenses.
- Calibration certificates of all measuring instruments used.

**Pre-shipment Inspection (1.5 marks):**
- Painting, labelling, packaging, and shock-watch indicators checked.
- A signed **FAT Punch List** is prepared, listing any deviations; the vendor must close them before dispatch.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Step 1: Compute total repair time (1 mark)**

$$
T_{repair} = 2 + 1.5 + 2.5 = 6 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 2: Compute net operating time (0.5 mark)**

$$
T_{op} = 720 - 6 = 714 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 3: Compute MTBF (1.5 marks)**

$$
MTBF = \dfrac{T_{op}}{N_{failures}} = \dfrac{714}{3} = 238 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 4: Compute MTTR (1 mark)**

$$
MTTR = \dfrac{T_{repair}}{N_{failures}} = \dfrac{6}{3} = 2 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 5: Compute Availability (1.5 marks)**

$$
A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \dfrac{238}{238 + 2} = \dfrac{238}{240} = 0.9917 = 99.17\%
$$

**Step 6: Comparison and Conclusion (1.5 marks)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since 99.17% > 98% (the acceptance criterion), the workstation **PASSES** the availability test. The MTBF of 238 hours is also healthy for an FMS workstation, indicating good inherent reliability of the machine tool subsystem.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**
> **(a)** Discuss the **concept of OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** in the context of FMS acceptance testing. Derive the OEE expression from its three constituent ratios. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** During a 200-hour SAT endurance run, an FMS workstation recorded: downtime = 4 hours, total pieces produced = 1,200, ideal cycle time = 0.15 hours/piece, and defective pieces = 24. Compute **Availability, Performance Efficiency, Quality Rate, and OEE**. State whether the system meets the typical world-class benchmark of **OEE $\geq$ 85%**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

OEE is the **gold-standard KPI** for evaluating the true productive effectiveness of any manufacturing equipment, including FMS workstations. It was popularised by **Seiichi Nakajima** as part of the TPM (Total Productive Maintenance) framework and is the metric most commonly used in FMS acceptance test certificates. (1 mark for introduction)

**The Three Constituent Ratios (3 marks):**

1. **Availability (A)** = Operating Time / Planned Production Time. It captures the impact of unplanned downtime (breakdowns) and planned downtime (set-ups, changeovers).

2. **Performance Efficiency ($P_e$)** = (Ideal Cycle Time × Total Pieces) / Operating Time. It captures the impact of sub-optimal speeds, idling, and minor stoppages. Theoretically it can exceed 100% if the machine runs faster than ideal, but for TPM purposes it is conventionally **capped at 100%** (or 85% in some industry models to leave a margin for un-foreseen slowdowns).

3. **Quality Rate (Q)** = Good Pieces / Total Pieces. It captures the impact of defective production — rejects, reworks, and start-up scrap.

**Derivation (3 marks):**

The OEE is defined as the product of the three independent ratios:

$$
\begin{aligned}
OEE &= A \times P_e \times Q \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{T_{op}}{T_{plan}} \times \dfrac{t_{cycle,ideal} \times N_{total}}{T_{op}} \times \dfrac{N_{good}}{N_{total}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Upon cancellation of $T_{op}$ and $N_{total}$:

$$
OEE = \dfrac{t_{cycle,ideal} \times N_{good}}{T_{plan}}
$$

This elegant final form expresses OEE as the **fraction of scheduled time that is converted into good parts at the ideal cycle rate**. (Final simplification explanation fetches 1 mark.)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why multiplication, not addition?** The three loss categories are statistically independent, and their effects compound multiplicatively, not additively. This is a board-exam favourite sub-question.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Given Data:**
- $T_{plan} = 200$ hours, Downtime = 4 hours, so $T_{op} = 196$ hours
- $N_{total} = 1200$, $N_{defect} = 24$, so $N_{good} = 1176$
- $t_{cycle,ideal} = 0.15$ hours/piece

**Step 1: Availability (1.5 marks)**

$$
A = \dfrac{196}{200} = 0.98 = 98\%
$$

**Step 2: Performance Efficiency (1.5 marks)**

$$
P_e = \dfrac{0.15 \times 1200}{196} = \dfrac{180}{196} = 0.9184 = 91.84\%
$$

**Step 3: Quality Rate (1.5 marks)**

$$
Q = \dfrac{1176}{1200} = 0.98 = 98\%
$$

**Step 4: OEE (1.5 marks)**

$$
OEE = 0.98 \times 0.9184 \times 0.98 = 0.8821 = 88.21\%
$$

**Step 5: Conclusion (1 mark)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since **88.21% > 85%**, the FMS workstation **exceeds the world-class OEE benchmark** and qualifies for full acceptance on productivity grounds. The performance is dominated by high availability and quality; performance efficiency is the marginal lever for further improvement (e.g., NC feedrate optimisation).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in FMS Acceptance Testing Answers:**
>
> 1. **Confusing FAT with SAT** — Many students interchange the two or fail to mention the *order* (FAT first, then SAT). Examiners explicitly test this sequencing.
> 2. **Forgetting units in MTBF/MTTR** — Always state **hours**. A number without a unit loses 0.5 marks.
> 3. **Writing OEE as a sum instead of a product** — This is a *very common error*. OEE is multiplicative; adding A, $P_e$, and Q is worth **zero marks**.
> 4. **Omitting the rectification loop** — When asked to "explain the procedure," always include the iterative **re-test after failure** loop. A linear-only answer is incomplete.
> 5. **Confusing repeatability with accuracy** — Accuracy is *how close to the commanded value*; repeatability is *how close to each other on repeated moves*. They are distinct, and the distinction is tested annually.
> 6. **Not referencing ISO/VDI standards** — Mentioning **ISO 230-2** or **VDI 3441** for accuracy tests adds professional credibility and often fetches the "extra mile" mark.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section. It is your last-minute revision sheet for the 2-mark / 7-mark / 14-mark questions on Acceptance Testing.**

- **Acceptance Testing** = Formal verification that delivered FMS meets contracted specifications.
- **Two main phases** = **FAT** (at vendor site) → **SAT** (at user site).
- **FAT precedes SAT**; a successful FAT is a *necessary but not sufficient* condition for SAT success.
- **Six categories of tests:** Geometric, Performance, Functional, Productivity, Reliability, Safety.
- **Key geometric standards:** **ISO 230-2** (accuracy & repeatability), **VDI 3441**.
- **Key time-based KPIs:** Tool change time, Pallet change time, Chip-to-chip time, Cycle time.
- **Reliability KPIs:** **MTBF** $\uparrow$ good, **MTTR** $\downarrow$ good.
- **System Availability $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$**; FMS target typically $\geq$ **95%**.
- **OEE = A × $P_e$ × Q**; world-class benchmark = **85%**.
- **OEE simplification:** $OEE = \dfrac{t_{ideal} \times N_{good}}{T_{plan}}$ = (Value-adding time) / (Scheduled time).
- **Unmanned endurance test** is the *most stringent* acceptance gate — proves lights-out capability.
- **Punch List** = formal list of deviations raised during testing; vendor must close before sign-off.
- **Sign-off triggers:** Final payment milestone + Warranty commencement + Baseline data for CMMS.
- **Documented deliverables:** FAT report, SAT report, Punch list closure, Calibration certificates, As-built drawings, Software licenses.
- **Iterative rectification loop** must be shown in any procedural flowchart — this is the most-missed element.
- **Always quote units** (hours, %, µm, parts/hour) — examiner's quick mark allocation.
- **Acceptance Test is contractual and legal** — distinguishes it from informal commissioning or trial runs.
